meta.review: HOSTILE REVIEW round 1 — adversarial scientific-accuracy audit
meta.method: 39 claim cards (tools/review-claim-card.mjs) -> 1 adversarial mineralogist per scenario (self-verified) -> independent skeptic defends the sim per material finding -> synthesis. 148 agents.

synthesis.headline: Across 39 scenarios the simulator's core paragenetic engines are largely sound — primary-stage sulfides, carbonates, silicates, zeolites, and evaporite cascades mostly grow the right species in the right order — but a single dominant failure mode recurs everywhere: OXIDATION-STATE and TEMPERATURE gates are too permissive, so supergene/oxidized and low-temperature phases nucleate inside hot, reducing, hypogene fluids (and vice versa). Roughly two-thirds of confirmed defects reduce to a fluid whose redox and thermal trajectory is not honestly held to the deposit's real regime, letting a mineral's own documented redox_requirement/T_range envelope be crossed by a broth that should never reach it. The most causally actionable root causes are (1) redox trajectories that drift oxidizing when the geology is reducing (and stay flat when the geology demands an oxidation event), (2) missing temperature-floor/ceiling gates on kinetically-controlled polymorphs and high-/low-T phases, (3) nucleation gates that read raw ion concentration instead of the already-computed saturation index, and (4) a leaked FluidChemistry F=10 ppm default that confabulates fluorite into fluorine-free deposits.

cross_cutting_mechanisms.mechanism: Oxidized/supergene phases (willemite, cerussite, smithsonite, greenockite, mimetite, wulfenite, chalcocite, covellite) nucleate inside hot reducing hypogene brines because the fluid's redox trajectory drifts oxidizing (O2 0.24-0.35, Eh +25 to +58 mV) instead of being held in the sulfide/H2S field, so each mineral's own 'oxidizing' gate opens where the deposit's chemistry forbids it.
cross_cutting_mechanisms.root_cause_hypothesis: Scenario redox trajectories are authored too oxidizing (or left flat) for reducing hypogene settings, and the oxidation gate keys on absolute O2/Eh crossing a low threshold rather than on a sulfide-saturation/aHS- veto; a sulfide-saturated fluid (galena+sphalerite+pyrite precipitating) can momentarily cross O2>=0.3 and unlock supergene minerals that are chemically incompatible with active sulfide deposition.
cross_cutting_mechanisms.growth_condition_fix: Drive reducing/sulfidic Eh trajectories (O2 -> ~0, Eh negative) from the outset in hypogene MVT/vein scenarios, and add an aHS-/sulfide-saturation VETO to every oxidizing-gated supergene phase so it cannot co-precipitate with galena/sphalerite/pyrite. Where a genuine supergene cap is wanted, model it as an explicit LATE oxidizing scenario EVENT (Eh-up + O2-up + sulfide depletion), never as a phase firing while Eh is dropping.

cross_cutting_mechanisms.mechanism: Temperature-uncontrolled polymorph and phase selection: aragonite grows in 120-260 C hydrothermal veins where calcite is stable, wurtzite grows as dominant ZnS at 180-277 C where sphalerite is the equilibrium phase, low-T supergene phases (gypsum/selenite, goethite, cerussite, mimetite) grow above their thermal ceilings, and quartz nucleates at 23-70 C where only opal/chalcedony forms. Nucleation functions ignore the documented T_min/T_max envelope.
cross_cutting_mechanisms.root_cause_hypothesis: Several nucleation engines either document a T_min/T_max but never enforce it inside _nuc_* (quartz T_min=50 unenforced; arsenopyrite has no T floor), or apply a misfiring 'T>50 low-Mg -> aragonite' clause, so kinetically/thermally forbidden phases clear their sigma gate purely on supersaturation. The gap between the metadata envelope and the runtime gate is the systemic hole.
cross_cutting_mechanisms.growth_condition_fix: Wire each mineral's documented T_range into its runtime nucleation gate: T-ceiling aragonite (~60-90 C) unless high Mg/Ca or SO4/Sr flag; retire the 'T>95 -> wurtzite' stability reading and gate wurtzite on acidic/low-T/S-deficient colloform conditions; T-floor quartz (~50 C) and arsenopyrite (~250-300 C) and route sub-floor silica to opal/chalcedony; T-ceiling supergene gypsum/goethite/cerussite/mimetite to their weathering-zone temperatures.

cross_cutting_mechanisms.mechanism: Sulfate and carbonate phases nucleate while their OWN computed saturation index is negative for the entire run: selenite/gypsum and celestine fire with SI<0 across many scenarios, because the nucleation gate reads raw Ca x S / Sr x S / Na x Cl concentration products instead of the SI the sim already computes, and no carbonate sink drains Ca/Sr to keep sulfates undersaturated.
cross_cutting_mechanisms.root_cause_hypothesis: Sulfate (and some evaporite) nucleation gates use a normalized concentration product rather than gating on the computed SI crossing 0, so a perpetually-undersaturated phase can still clear sigma_crit; compounded by missing early carbonate sinks (calcite/gaylussite/strontianite) that in nature draw Ca/Sr down on the alkaline (CO3>Ca) chemical divide.
cross_cutting_mechanisms.growth_condition_fix: Gate sulfate/evaporite nucleation on the mineral's own computed SI (require SI>0), reusing the existing thermodynamic instrument, and plumb early carbonate sinks that consume Ca/Sr so the sulfate stays undersaturated. This increases causal control by making the scoreboard follow the SI the model already knows.

cross_cutting_mechanisms.mechanism: Halite, sylvite, and thenardite (and other saturation-limited evaporite salts) nucleate from dilute or non-concentrating brines: halite fires 8-12x from ~800 ppm Cl / 4-6 psu fluids ~4 orders of magnitude below NaCl saturation, sylvite fires before halite (inverted bittern order), and thenardite fires from a Ca-SO4 brine — because the halide/sulfate gates normalize to ~100-500 ppm as 'saturation' and evaporation events never actually raise concentration.
cross_cutting_mechanisms.root_cause_hypothesis: The halite/sylvite supersaturation normalization is keyed to ppm thresholds ~1000x below real NaCl/KCl solubility, and several 'evaporation' events set fluid_surface_ring without actually ratcheting the concentration factor above 1, so ordinary brackish water clears the salt gates and the most-soluble bittern salts fire out of sequence.
cross_cutting_mechanisms.growth_condition_fix: Re-scale halite/sylvite/thenardite nucleation to real solubility (halite ~26 wt%, sylvite at the bittern point) keyed to the ion activity product vs Ksp(T) or an evaporative concentration factor >>1; gate sylvite behind halite having precipitated; and make evaporation half-cycles genuinely raise salinity/concentration instead of holding it flat.

cross_cutting_mechanisms.mechanism: The leaked FluidChemistry default `this.F = opts.F ?? 10.0` confabulates fluorite (and HF side-effects) into fluorine-free deposits: any scenario that omits an explicit F field silently inherits 10 ppm, which clears fluorite's fluid_min F>=5 gate wherever Ca is present, minting fluorite in caves, rodingite, amphibolite clefts, and marl systems that carry no fluorine source.
cross_cutting_mechanisms.root_cause_hypothesis: A non-zero class default for a trace anion means 'unset' reads as 'present at gate-clearing level'; fluorite's gate keys only on Ca and F minima, so the inherited default is indistinguishable from a modeled fluorine input.
cross_cutting_mechanisms.growth_condition_fix: Audit every scenario that omits F and set it explicitly to a deposit-appropriate low value (~0-2 ppm) below fluorite's fluid_min; consider changing the FluidChemistry default to 0 so 'unset' means 'absent'. A broth-input fix, not a scoreboard edit.

cross_cutting_mechanisms.mechanism: Tiger's eye (a chalcedony pseudomorph after crocidolite, a Precambrian banded-iron-formation phenomenon) nucleates in basalt amygdales, topaz veins, and pegmatite pockets because its nucleation cascade falls through to a bare-wall / bare-hematite 'tiger iron' fallback when no crocidolite substrate exists, decoupling the pseudomorph from its required parent.
cross_cutting_mechanisms.root_cause_hypothesis: The tiger's-eye substrate-priority cascade (dissolving-crocidolite > hematite > magnetite) has a permissive bare-wall/bare-hematite fallback, so generic late silica+Fe+O2 chemistry mints the most locality-specific mineral in the catalog wherever the numeric gate is met.
cross_cutting_mechanisms.growth_condition_fix: Require a dissolving-crocidolite substrate (or at minimum a BIF hematite/magnetite substrate) for tiger's-eye nucleation and delete the bare-wall fallback, so a broth with no sodic amphibole and no BIF Fe-oxide returns sigma=0. Genuine BIF scenarios that grow crocidolite first are unaffected.

cross_cutting_mechanisms.mechanism: Trace metals (Cd, Ag) that in nature substitute into a host sulfide lattice instead nucleate as abundant discrete phases (greenockite/hawleyite outnumbering sphalerite; early acanthite outnumbering and preceding its galena host), because the discrete-phase gates fire on low absolute trace concentrations with no partition sink into the dominant sulfide and no paragenetic host-first ordering.
cross_cutting_mechanisms.root_cause_hypothesis: The engine has no Cd-into-ZnS / Ag-into-PbS partition sink drawing the trace down as the host sulfide grows, and discrete-CdS/Ag2S nucleation lacks a paragenetic gate requiring the host framework first, so trace ppm inputs mis-partition into their own over-abundant minerals.
cross_cutting_mechanisms.growth_condition_fix: Add lattice partition sinks that consume Cd into sphalerite and Ag into galena as those hosts grow, raise the discrete-phase nucleation sigma so CdS/Ag2S appear only at much higher trace levels, and add a host-framework-exists paragenetic gate so the inclusion phase cannot precede its host.

cross_cutting_mechanisms.mechanism: District-diagnostic phases are total no-shows because the required engine or broth ion is missing, not because conditions forbid them: magnetite (serpentinization), siegenite/chalcopyrite (Viburnum Cu-Ni-Co), magnesite (ultramafic weathering), spinel (Mogok), and calcite (Sicilian BSR) never grow — the sim silently omits the mineral that defines the deposit.
cross_cutting_mechanisms.root_cause_hypothesis: Two sub-causes: (a) the species has no growable engine at all (magnesite, siegenite, spinel absent from minerals.json), or (b) the engine exists but its gate is mis-scoped to an oxidizing/skarn regime (magnetite gated O2_min 0.1 for skarn, cannot fire under reducing serpentinization); plus broths missing the required cations (no Cu/Ni/Co in reactive_wall) or under-modeled drivers (BSR alkalinity too weak for calcite SI to cross 0).
cross_cutting_mechanisms.growth_condition_fix: Author the missing engines with correct condition gates (reducing-side magnetite branch keyed to H2-generating serpentinization; Mg-rich low-T magnesite; Mg-exhaustion-gated spinel prioritized above corundum; chalcopyrite->siegenite thiospinel sequence), add the missing broth cations, and raise the modeled driver (DIC/CO3 for BSR calcite) so the diagnostic phase reaches saturation. Never accept the no-show by editing expects_species.

cross_cutting_mechanisms.mechanism: Missing scenario EVENTS that are the real ore-forming trigger: boiling/flashing (Sunnyside gold + manganocalcite cap), CO2 degassing (calcite saturation), and late oxidation pulses are absent, so the fluid trajectory is monotonic where nature has a step-change — gold, calcite caps, and supergene overprints that depend on the event never form.
cross_cutting_mechanisms.root_cause_hypothesis: Scenario authors modeled smooth cooling/mixing but omitted the discrete degassing/boiling/oxidation events that drive saturation ledges; e.g. Sunnyside's CO2aq RISES 49->142 mg/L (loading) where the deposit BOILED (degassing), so Au(HS)2- is never destabilized and pH never rises to cross calcite saturation.
cross_cutting_mechanisms.growth_condition_fix: Add the named events to the trajectory: a Period-VI boiling/flashing pulse (CO2/H2S degassing, Eh step) to precipitate gold and lift pH past calcite saturation for the manganocalcite cap; late meteoric/uplift oxidation events (Eh+, O2+, sulfide depletion) where a genuine supergene cap is documented. Fix the CO2 trajectory direction, don't lower the SI threshold.

cross_cutting_mechanisms.mechanism: Redox-blind saturation instruments report reduced-phase (siderite) supersaturation in demonstrably oxidizing fluids because SI is computed from total Fe as if all ferrous, with no Eh/Fe2+-Fe3+ Nernst partitioning — an internal thermodynamic contradiction (fluid ferric enough to grow epidote/goethite/carnotite yet siderite-supersaturated) that is currently harmless only because a separate O2 gate blocks growth.
cross_cutting_mechanisms.root_cause_hypothesis: The carbonate SI functions use the total dissolved Fe pool without speciating by Eh, so oxidizing fluids report false siderite supersaturation; growth is prevented only by a bolt-on O2_max gate on the separate growth path, leaving the reported saturation state contradictory and any future SI-driven engine exposed.
cross_cutting_mechanisms.growth_condition_fix: Redox-couple the siderite (and other Fe(II)-carbonate) SI itself: derive Fe2+ activity from total Fe via the Nernst equation before forming IAP/Ksp, so oxidizing Eh collapses SI_siderite and reducing Eh raises it. One Eh lever then governs both the ferric-phase stability and siderite suppression, increasing causal control.

top_fixes.fix: Hold reducing/sulfidic redox trajectories (O2 -> ~0, negative Eh) in hypogene MVT/vein scenarios AND add a sulfide-saturation (aHS-) veto to every oxidizing-gated supergene phase, so willemite/cerussite/smithsonite/greenockite/chalcocite cannot co-precipitate with actively-growing galena+sphalerite+pyrite.
top_fixes.rationale: This single redox-trajectory + veto lever repairs the review's dominant failure mode (oxidized phases in reducing fluids) across the most scenarios, and it is the purest expression of the drift doctrine: we understand why the phases fired (a momentary O2>=0.3 crossing) and can shut them off by moving the growth condition, not the scoreboard.

top_fixes.fix: Enforce each mineral's documented T_min/T_max inside its runtime nucleation gate (the metadata envelope currently exists but is not read): T-floor quartz (~50 C, route sub-floor silica to opal/chalcedony) and arsenopyrite (~250-300 C); T-ceiling aragonite (~60-90 C unless high Mg/Ca or SO4/Sr) and supergene gypsum/goethite/cerussite/mimetite; retire the 'T>95 -> wurtzite' reading and gate wurtzite on acidic/low-T/S-deficient conditions.
top_fixes.rationale: Temperature-uncontrolled polymorph/phase selection is the second most widespread defect and the fix is mechanical and low-risk: wire the already-documented T envelope into the _nuc_* gate so kinetically-forbidden phases stop clearing sigma on supersaturation alone.

top_fixes.fix: Change the FluidChemistry F default from 10 ppm to 0 (or audit every F-omitting scenario and set F explicitly to a deposit-appropriate ~0-2 ppm below fluorite's fluid_min), so 'unset fluorine' stops confabulating fluorite into fluorine-free deposits.
top_fixes.rationale: A one-line default change (plus a broth audit) eliminates a whole class of confirmed surprise-species defects at once; it is the highest ROI per line of code and cleanly removes the mineral's fuel rather than deleting it from any scoreboard.

top_fixes.fix: Require a real crocidolite (or BIF hematite/magnetite) substrate for tiger's-eye nucleation and delete the bare-wall / bare-hematite 'tiger iron' fallback in the substrate cascade.
top_fixes.rationale: Tiger's eye is repeatedly the single most disbelievable species in a run; a precursor-substrate gate permanently excludes it from any non-BIF deposit while leaving genuine BIF scenarios (which grow crocidolite first) untouched — high credibility payoff for a targeted gate change.

top_fixes.fix: Re-scale halite/sylvite/thenardite nucleation to real solubility (keyed to ion activity product vs Ksp or an evaporative concentration factor >>1), gate sylvite behind halite having precipitated, and make evaporation half-cycles actually ratchet salinity/concentration up instead of holding it flat.
top_fixes.rationale: The halide gates currently treat ~100-500 ppm as saturation (~1000x too low), so dilute brackish water mints salt crusts and bittern salts fire out of order; fixing the normalization protects every dilute scenario at once and is explicitly a gate-threshold lever, not a baseline patch.

top_fixes.fix: Author the missing district-defining engines with correct condition gates and add the required broth cations: a reducing-side serpentinization magnetite branch, chalcopyrite->siegenite (thiospinel) for the Viburnum Cu-Ni-Co suite (add Cu/Ni/Co to the broth), Mg-rich low-T magnesite, and Mg-exhaustion-gated spinel prioritized above corundum.
top_fixes.rationale: These are diagnostic no-shows where the deposit's defining mineral simply cannot nucleate because no engine or broth ion exists; adding the correctly-gated engine restores the biography and increases causal control, whereas accepting the no-show would be the doctrine-forbidden scoreboard edit.

top_fixes.fix: Add the missing ore-forming EVENTS to the fluid trajectory: a Period-VI boiling/flashing pulse (CO2/H2S degassing + Eh step) for Sunnyside to precipitate native gold and lift pH past calcite saturation for the manganocalcite cap, plus a stronger modeled BSR alkalinity (DIC/CO3) surge so secondary calcite reaches SI>0 in the Sicilian sulfur deposit.
top_fixes.rationale: Where the fluid trajectory is monotonic but the deposit has a step-change, the namesake products (Sunnyside gold, Sicilian secondary calcite) can never form; adding the real event is a growth-condition lever that fixes the CO2 trajectory direction rather than lowering an SI threshold to fake the phase.

top_fixes.fix: Redox-couple the siderite (and other Fe(II)-carbonate) saturation index itself by deriving Fe2+ activity from total Fe via the Nernst equation before computing IAP/Ksp, so oxidizing Eh collapses SI_siderite instead of relying on a bolt-on O2 growth gate.
top_fixes.rationale: The redox-blind SI instrument reports reduced-carbonate supersaturation in oxidizing fluids — currently harmless but internally contradictory and a latent trap for any future SI-driven engine; one Eh lever then governs both ferric-phase stability and siderite suppression, maximizing causal control.

confirmed.claim: willemite (Zn2SiO4) nucleates at step 19, co-occurring with galena, pyrite, wurtzite and 4 steps before sphalerite
confirmed.objection: Willemite is an OXIDIZED-zone / oxidizing-hydrothermal Zn silicate that precipitates over sphalerite only under oxidizing, hematite-stable, LOW-aHS- conditions. A fluid simultaneously precipitating galena+pyrite+wurtzite+sphalerite is sulfide-saturated (high aHS-) and reducing — the exact regime that suppresses willemite in favour of sphalerite. Tri-State is a sulfide district with NO willemite; willemite is diagnostic of the OPPOSITE settings (Franklin/Sterling metamorphic, or Skorpion/Vazante/Otavi nonsulfide oxidized zones).
confirmed.evidence: The sim's own minerals.json willemite entry: redox_requirement:'oxidizing', scenarios:['supergene_oxidation'], required O2>=0.3, pH 6-9. Literature: Brugger et al. 2003 (Econ. Geol. 98, 819) 'Formation of Willemite in Hydrothermal Environments' — willemite over sphalerite is preferred under oxidizing (hematite-stable) conditions, low aHS-, neutral-basic pH, >100-150C. It nucleated here because the mvt broth momentarily crossed O2>=0.3 (0.315 mg/L) at step 19 while sulfides were also precipitating — chemically incoherent.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Make the mvt redox TRAJECTORY reducing from the outset — start O2 ~0 and Eh <0 (basinal MVT brines are H2S-bearing and reducing), so willemite's oxidizing O2>=0.3 gate never opens. Additionally add an aHS-/sulfide-saturation VETO to willemite's nucleation gate so it can never co-precipitate with galena/sphalerite/pyrite. This is a redox-trajectory + gate-threshold fix, not a baseline edit.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest defense I could build, and why each prong fails:

(1) "Willemite has a legitimate HYPOGENE hydrothermal mode, not just supergene — Vazante (Brazil) is a famous primary/hydrothermal willemite deposit, so willemite from a 131°C basinal fluid isn't inherently absurd." Fails: every willemite mode — Vazante hypogene, Skorpion supergene, Franklin/Sterling metamorphic — is defined by a sulfide-POOR, oxidizing, low-aHS- environment. Willemite is the flagship "nonsulfide zinc" mineral precisely because it forms where ZnS is NOT stable. It cannot co-precipitate with fresh sulfides.

(2) "O2=0.311 clears the sim's own gate (O2_min 0.3) and is defensible." Fails hard against ground truth. I ran mvt/seed-42 headless: at step 19 the fluid_mixing event has just injected Zn 0→150, S 0→119.27, Pb 40→65. The willemite gate (supersaturation_willemite in js/39) checks only Zn>=50, SiO2>=100, O2>=0.3, T, pH — it has NO sulfide-activity term. So at step 19 the sim nucleates Willemite #6 in the SAME step, from the SAME fluid (S=119.27, Eh=+48mV, O2=0.311), that nucleates Wurtzite #7, Pyrite #8, and Galena #9. A Zn silicate and three sulfides from one hyper-sulfidic brine. The sim's own log line even tags it "SUPERGENE nonsulfide (Skorpion)" — a nonsulfide mineral crystallizing in a fluid drowning in 119 units of dissolved sulfide. That O2=0.31 and S=119 coexist at all is itself a sim artifact (O2 and S are decoupled fields; in reality free O2 would oxidize the sulfide), and the willemite gate rides that decoupling. The reviewer actually understated it — they blamed a "momentary O2 crossing," but O2 is above 0.3 at most early steps; the real trigger is Zn arriving via fluid_mixing, and the deeper fault is the missing aHS- suppression.

(3) "Co-nucleation at one step isn't a strict-sequence claim." True but irrelevant — the defect is simultaneous saturation, not ordering: the same fluid is saturated in ZnS and Zn2SiO4 at once, which is a flat contradiction, not a paragenetic-order nuance.

(4) Locality check: Tri-State hosts NO willemite. Its oxidized-Zn zone is smithsonite + hemimorphite ("dry-bone"/"calamine" ore). Willemite is diagnostic of the opposite settings the reviewer names. So the phase is both chemically impossible in this fluid AND absent from the anchor district.

Root cause is a clean growth-condition lever, fully doctrine-compatible: supersaturation_willemite() (and hemimorphite likewise) uses O2 as its sole redox proxy and never returns 0 when the fluid is sulfide-saturated; the mvt broth's deliberately-boundary O2=0.25 baseline noises above 0.30, and once Zn+S land together the Zn/SiO2/O2 gates all clear in the same breath as the sulfides. Adding a sulfide-activity suppression term (willemite → 0 when S/aHS- indicates ZnS is precipitating) is the causal fix — not a scoreboard edit. This is also a class bug, not a one-off: the same gap would let willemite/hemimorphite fire in any sulfidic Zn-Si scenario. The objection stands.

confirmed.claim: greenockite (CdS) fires 5 events (first step 21) and hawleyite (CdS) 1 event, together the most abundant sulfide events in the run, while sphalerite fires ONCE (step 23, last)
confirmed.objection: Cd at Tri-State is a lattice TRACE substituting for Zn inside sphalerite (~<1%), not a dominant discrete CdS mineral. Discrete greenockite is a rare yellow SUPERGENE coating formed when Cd-sphalerite oxidizes — an oxidized-zone phase, wrong for a primary reducing brine. Having CdS out-nucleate the district's defining ore (sphalerite, which is ~5x more abundant than galena at Tri-State) is a confabulated-abundance inversion: the trace phase dominates and the main ore is a lone late afterthought.
confirmed.evidence: The sim's own greenockite description: 'Forms by supergene oxidation of Cd-bearing sphalerite... re-precipitates as CdS... in the supergene zone', scenarios:['supergene_oxidation'], max_nucleation_count:5. The author's note defends Cd via Schwartz 2000 — but that is Cd-IN-SPHALERITE (lattice trace), NOT discrete supergene CdS in the primary sequence, so the discrete phase is undefended. Tri-State reference (KGS/mindat): sphalerite dominant, 5x galena.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Route Cd as a trace_ingredient CONSUMED into sphalerite's lattice rather than nucleating discrete CdS; gate greenockite/hawleyite on oxidizing/supergene O2 (which the reducing broth from finding 1 then denies), and cap max_nucleation_count so a hypogene brine cannot spawn 5 CdS events. Also raise sphalerite's nucleation weight/lower its gate so the defining ore nucleates early and dominantly, not once at step 23.

refutation.reasoning:
STRONGEST DEFENSE I could build, then tested against the actual v226 seed-42 run:

DEFENSE 1 — "event count ≠ abundance; sphalerite's single firing is RNG-cascade luck, and its one crystal could be the volumetric giant (max_size_cm=80 vs greenockite's 3, hawleyite's 0.5)." This was my best hope. It DIES on the data. I ran the current build (SIM_VERSION 226, seed 42): sphalerite = ONE crystal, 26 µm; greenockite = 5 crystals, max 2396 µm, summed 7742 µm; hawleyite = 87 µm. Greenockite out-grows the district's defining ore ~92x in linear size (and galena's summed 21580 µm dwarfs it 268x). The inversion is real in BOTH event count (6 CdS vs 1 sphalerite) AND crystal size. Size does not rescue the sim; it makes it worse.

DEFENSE 2 — "greenockite is genuinely a Tri-State phase; Cd=2 ppm is within Schwartz 2000's raw-fluid range; the fluid is reducing (Eh→−230 mV, S=120) so CdS is thermodynamically stable — no supergene step required." TRUE but insufficient. Presence ≠ dominance. The mineralogy demands CdS be a trace: Cd substitutes into the sphalerite lattice at <1%, and discrete greenockite is only a rare yellow supergene coating. The scenario's own greenockite description says it "forms by supergene oxidation... re-precipitates as CdS... in the supergene zone" — a supergene-genesis phase, here made the dominant primary sulfide of a reducing ore brine.

DEFENSE 3 — "sphalerite is under-grown because Zn was consumed first (willemite+wurtzite)." DIES: I tracked the fluid — Zn sits at ~150 ppm essentially untouched (150→149.6 by step 40) while sphalerite stays 26 µm. It is NOT Zn depletion. The mechanism is that the sim rewards CdS's extreme insolubility (Ksp ~10⁻²⁸) with a huge supersaturation, so 2 ppm trace Cd + 120 ppm S builds big discrete greenockite, while 150 ppm Zn barely grows sphalerite. The root cause is that vugg has NO solid-solution sink: in nature that 2 ppm Cd is swept into the 150 ppm Zn sphalerite lattice and never reaches discrete-CdS dominance; the sim lets free Cd form large discrete crystals.

Does the objection survive? YES. The canonical output of the flagship Tri-State MVT scenario inverts the deposit's defining feature: sphalerite (the Zn ore — the whole point of the district) is a lone 26 µm speck, out-nucleated 6:1 and out-grown ~92x by a trace supergene CdS phase. A geologist reading this biography would say "this is not Tri-State — where is the zinc ore?" Corroborating: galena also massively out-abundances sphalerite (a Pb-over-Zn inversion in a Zn-dominant district), an artifact of the same v226 reducing-Eh-trend that the note brags "boosts late galena 1x→4x" while it silently crushed sphalerite from 621 µm (v99) to 26 µm.

The fix is a legitimate growth-condition lever, fully consistent with the drift doctrine and NOT scoreboard-editing (greenockite isn't even in expects_species): drop the free broth Cd toward zero (the discrete-CdS-available Cd after lattice partitioning is near-nil in a Zn-rich primary brine), and/or gate greenockite behind an oxidation/supergene event (its documented home scenario), and/or add a Cd-into-sphalerite trace sink. That the fix is a causal lever confirms — not refutes — that the current output is wrong.

Steelman that fails: "greenockite presence is defensible; only sphalerite is mis-tuned." Even restoring sphalerite to its v99 621 µm leaves greenockite at 2396 µm still dominating it — the trace phase over-growing the ore is itself unphysical, so fixing sphalerite alone does not resolve the inversion.

confirmed.claim: cerussite (PbCO3) nucleates at step 41, when Eh is ~-50 mV and falling, with galena/pyrite sulfide still present
confirmed.objection: Cerussite is a SUPERGENE oxidation product of galena — forming it requires oxidizing the sulfide (S2- -> removed as sulfate) so PbCO3 replaces PbS. In a reducing, sulfide-saturated brine galena is stable and cerussite cannot form. Worse, the Eh is DROPPING (more reducing) as cerussite appears — the redox arrow points the wrong way. In real MVT paragenesis cerussite is only a late weathering overprint AFTER uplift oxidizes the deposit, not a mid-sequence hypogene phase.
confirmed.evidence: minerals.json lists cerussite in the supergene_oxidation nucleatable set. Standard mineralogy: cerussite forms in the oxidized weathering zone of galena ore. Strip Eh at step 41 ~ -50 mV and monotonically falling to -230 mV — a strengthening reducing trend, opposite to what cerussite genesis needs.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Same reducing-broth lever as finding 1 (a sulfide-saturated/negative-Eh fluid vetoes cerussite's oxidizing gate). If a supergene lead-carbonate cap is wanted, model it as an explicit LATE oxidizing scenario EVENT (meteoric/uplift oxidation) appended after the primary sulfides, with Eh flipped positive — not as a phase firing while Eh is dropping.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest defense I could build: (1) Tri-State genuinely hosts cerussite, and (2) PbCO3 precipitation is NOT itself a redox reaction (Pb stays 2+), so a Pb-carrying MVT brine — which demonstrably transports 100s–1000s ppm Pb as chloride complexes (Wenz 2012, Stoffell 2008) — could in principle drop cerussite directly from Pb2+ + CO3 without any oxidation, unlike the Cu2+/Zn2+ carbonates whose metals must be oxidatively mobilized. This is the only real escape route.

It fails on the sim's own fluid state. I ran seed-42 mvt and read the fluid at the nucleation step: at step 41 the brine holds 105.1 ppm DISSOLVED SULFIDE (S) coexisting with 65 ppm Pb, Eh -45.7 mV and falling, on actively-growing galena. The direct-precipitation defense only works in a sulfide-POOR fluid; here galena (Ksp ~1e-27.5) outcompetes cerussite (Ksp ~1e-13.1) by ~14 orders of magnitude, so that Pb sinks as PbS — and the sim indeed fires galena at these very steps. There is no sulfide-depletion window (S stays >100 ppm through step 45; the sulfide only arrived at the step-20 mixing event, and before that sigma_cerussite was 0.03–0.07, far below the 1.0 gate). So cerussite fires in the single worst window. It also fires for the wrong REASON: sigma only crosses 1.0 at steps 40–45 because temperature dips below ~100°C (lifting the high-T penalty on Pb×CO3), then a step-46 reheat to 148°C crushes it back — a transient cooling artifact, not oxidation. That is precisely the loss of causal control the doctrine warns against.

Decisive corroboration is internal: the sim gates every sibling supergene carbonate on redox — smithsonite (ZnCO3, cerussite's exact analog, O2_min 0.2, T_max 100, "Supergene-only"), malachite 0.3, azurite 1.0, rosasite/aurichalcite 0.8 — all via carbonateRedoxAvailable(). Cerussite alone was explicitly (and wrongly) classified with the redox-neutral calcite/dolomite/aragonite (js/20c-chemistry-redox.ts:494-496: "cerussite have no fluid.O2 reference"). At O2=0.131 mg/L, giving cerussite smithsonite's O2_min 0.2 would suppress it here while leaving the legitimate supergene_oxidation scenario untouched. The reviewer's "redox arrow points the wrong way" and "supergene product in a reducing sulfide field" are both correct. Fix is a clean growth-condition lever (add O2_min/Eh gate and/or high-S suppression to MINERAL_GATES_cerussite), not a scoreboard edit — fully doctrine-compliant. Objection survives.

confirmed.claim: smithsonite (ZnCO3) nucleates at step 86 in the primary hypogene Elmwood MVT run, at a flat +25 mV Eh, after/alongside sphalerite, galena and pyrite.
confirmed.objection: Smithsonite is a SUPERGENE oxidation product of sphalerite — it forms only once the sulfide ore is weathered under oxidizing meteoric conditions around the water table. A deep, well-preserved hypogene MVT brine that is dropping ZnS (sphalerite) is in the sulfidic field and locks Zn as sulfide; it cannot simultaneously drop ZnCO3. Elmwood is a classic deep hypogene deposit and carries no smithsonite in its documented species list.
confirmed.evidence: Web-verified Elmwood species (Prospector Minerals; mindat loc-4125) list sphalerite/fluorite/barite/calcite/galena/marcasite/quartz/dolomite/celestite/bitumen — NO smithsonite. USGS OFR-95-0831 and MDPI/ScienceDirect non-sulfide-Zn papers: smithsonite forms 'during supergene oxidation,' replacing sphalerite below the water table. minerals.json tags smithsonite redox_requirement:'oxidizing', scenarios:['supergene_oxidation'], required_ingredients O2:0.3 — yet the strip's O2 is a flat 0.236 (below its own gate) and Eh is constant +25 mV, so a mildly-oxidizing steady redox is clearing a gate meant for a weathering profile.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Give elmwood a REDUCING/sulfidic Eh trajectory once the S-bearing groundwater mixes in at step 15 (drive Eh into the H2S field, O2 -> ~0). Zn then stays sphalerite and smithsonite's oxidizing redox gate never clears. Do NOT bolt a supergene-oxidation event onto this hypogene showcase — the fix is the broth redox arc, not deleting the species.

refutation.reasoning: Strongest defense assembled: (1) smithsonite fires at step 86/~55 C, ~72 steps and ~65 C after sphalerite (step 14/~120 C), so it is a LATE cool phase, not co-nucleation — the objection's "simultaneously drop ZnCO3" is imprecise; (2) the engine correctly encodes smithsonite as retrograde-solubility, so cooling is a genuine driver; (3) early sulfides exhaust S (initial S=0, added only at the step-15 mixing event), so residual Zn could route to carbonate. This defense does NOT survive. The decisive failures: (a) The Eh is FLAT +25 mV and O2 ~0.236 for the ENTIRE run from step 0 — there is no oxidation front, meteoric influx, or redox excursion. Supergene smithsonite requires a redox TRANSITION to oxidizing meteoric water; a steady mildly-oxic value that never changes is not that. (b) The scenario's OWN notes describe a hydrocarbon-bearing brine (bitumen/oil inclusions color the golden calcite) — that fluid is REDUCING, so O2=0.25/Eh +25 mV contradicts the scenario's own documented chemistry. (c) Web + reviewer confirm Elmwood is the world-reference PRISTINE, un-weathered deep hypogene MVT; documented species are fluorite/sphalerite/barite/calcite with NO smithsonite (mindat loc-4125, Prospector Minerals), and smithsonite is textbook supergene. (d) Corroboration: cerussite (PbCO3, step 108), the analogous supergene oxidation product of galena, ALSO fires — two spurious supergene carbonates = a systematic redox-setting error. (e) The sim's own metadata tags smithsonite scenarios:['supergene_oxidation'], redox_requirement:'oxidizing' — it self-flags as out of place. MECHANISM (from code): the binding runtime gate is MINERAL_GATES_smithsonite (js/32-supersat-carbonate.ts) with O2_min 0.2 (NOT the minerals.json metadata 0.3 the reviewer cited); carbonateRedoxAvailable(fluid,0.2) needs Eh >= ~0 mV, which +25 mV clears; Zn=40>=20 and the golden-calcite CO3 pulse train (amps 460-552) push carbonate sigma over 1.0. So the reviewer's "O2 below its own gate" phrasing is factually off — the gate is CLEARED, which is precisely WHY smithsonite fires — but that detail strengthens, not rescues, the objection. DOCTRINE FIX is a clean growth-condition lever: drop scenario O2 from 0.25 to <=0.15 (Eh below ~-50 mV) to represent the reducing hydrocarbon-bearing MVT brine; this closes smithsonite's and cerussite's redox gate, is MORE accurate to the documented fluid, and leaves the calcite showcase untouched (calcite has no O2 gate). Objection stands.

confirmed.claim: cerussite (PbCO3) nucleates at step 108, after primary galena (step 14), in the same flat +25 mV Eh run.
confirmed.objection: Cerussite is the terminal supergene oxidation product of galena (galena -> anglesite -> cerussite) and requires an oxidizing weathering overprint. In a hypogene sulfidic brine Pb precipitates as galena (PbS), not PbCO3. The trajectory contains NO oxidation event — Eh is dead flat — so the sim manufactures a lead-oxidation product without ever oxidizing anything. Not in Elmwood's documented species.
confirmed.evidence: Elmwood species list (web) has no cerussite. Literature (WGNHS; supergene Pb studies): galena masses are 'coated with and partially replaced by cerussite,' and cerussite forms at HIGHER Eh above the water table — i.e. an oxidized regime distinct from ore deposition. minerals.json cerussite redox_requirement:'oxidizing', scenarios:['supergene_oxidation'], T_range 0-80 C — the run's cooled 53 C tail plus flat oxidizing Eh is exactly what lets it clear.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: The same reducing-Eh trajectory that keeps Pb as galena also holds cerussite's oxidizing gate shut. Keep this scenario's redox in the sulfide field; reserve cerussite for a dedicated supergene scenario with a genuine Eh-up + O2-up + sulfide-depletion event.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest defense I could build for the sim: (1) Pb does NOT change valence between galena (PbS) and cerussite (PbCO3) — both are Pb(II). So cerussite's "oxidizing requirement" is indirect (it needs sulfide destroyed to free Pb2+), not a cation-redox reaction; a fluid with S exhausted by earlier galena and a late CO3 pulse could in principle drop PbCO3 with no Pb oxidation. (2) The elmwood run is actually ABOVE the engine's oxidizing threshold: Eh +25 mV > the ~0 mV / O2 0.2 gate that smithsonite (cerussite's Zn analog) legitimately clears at step 86 — so the fluid reads as mildly oxidizing, not a "manufactured" product. (3) The sim even prefers to nucleate cerussite ON galena (js/82 lines 224-225), mimicking the galena→cerussite paragenesis.

Why the defense does NOT survive: (a) The scenario has NO oxidation event — Eh is dead flat +25 mV — yet it deposits BOTH reduced primary sulfides (galena/pyrite/sphalerite, step 14) AND supergene oxidation-zone carbonates (smithsonite 86, cerussite 108) at the identical Eh. That is thermodynamically impossible: a real MVT ore fluid deposits sulfides at reducing Eh (~-150 to -300 mV, sulfidic) and only forms cerussite after uplift/weathering pushes Eh up above the water table. The reviewer's core point — a supergene lead-oxidation product with no oxidation transition — is exactly right. (b) The fluid is still a 21-psu chloride brine, where Pb stays Cl-complexed and cerussite is undersaturated; direct hypogene PbCO3 precipitation there is not real. (c) Elmwood-Gordonsville is a deep sealed hypogene deposit with essentially no supergene oxidation zone; cerussite is not a documented species (reviewer's web list + WGNHS supergene literature both agree). (d) Decisive independent code evidence: cerussite is MISSING the `carbonateRedoxAvailable` oxidizing gate (and the `O2_min` gate-registry field) that ALL its supergene-carbonate siblings enforce (smithsonite/azurite/rosasite/aurichalcite in js/32-supersat-carbonate.ts), despite its own minerals.json declaring redox_requirement:"oxidizing", scenarios:["supergene_oxidation"]. So the declared intent is real and the code silently violates it — a genuine gate-audit miss, not a design choice.

Per the drift doctrine this is fixable by a growth-condition lever (NOT by editing expects_species/baseline): add the `carbonateRedoxAvailable(fluid, O2_min)` gate to `supersaturation_cerussite()` to match its siblings + its declared requirement, AND set the elmwood ore stage to properly reducing Eh so sulfides deposit reducing and no oxidation-zone carbonate leaks. The objection stands.

confirmed.claim: selenite (gypsum, CaSO4-2H2O) nucleates twice at step 97 even though SI_selenite is negative for the entire run (max -0.504, never reaches saturation).
confirmed.objection: Gypsum/selenite is a low-T oxidized-sulfate evaporite phase, not part of a reduced MVT sulfide+sulfate hydrothermal assemblage, and it is absent from Elmwood's species list. On top of the facies mismatch there is an internal saturation contradiction: a phase whose own saturation index is UNDERSATURATED throughout the run should not nucleate at all.
confirmed.evidence: Elmwood documented species (web) include celestite and quartz but NOT gypsum/selenite. Strip SI_selenite = [-8, -0.504] never crosses 0, yet 2 events fire at step 97; SI_anhydrite (max -0.441) and SI_celestine (max -0.252) likewise stay <0. So the model nucleates an oxidized evaporite that both the deposit and the run's own thermodynamics forbid.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Keep the brine reduced and below gypsum saturation: tighten selenite's nucleation gate so a perpetually SI<0 phase cannot fire, and keep the S budget dominantly reduced sulfide (feeding sphalerite/galena/marcasite) with only enough SO4 activity for the documented barite + celestine.

refutation.reasoning:
STRONGEST DEFENSE I could mount: The reviewer's "internal saturation contradiction" is a partial category error. The nucleation gate (_nuc_selenite, js/90-nucleation-sulfate.ts:187) reads supersaturation_selenite() — a LEGACY capped-ratio heuristic σ=(Ca/60)*(S/50)*redox, sigma_crit=1.0 (js/40-supersat-sulfate.ts:407) — NOT the SI_selenite chip. SI_selenite is a rigorous log Ω thermodynamic OBSERVER added v165 (js/40b), and the 40b header explicitly documents σ and SI as intentionally decoupled ("different stories — both useful"). So the engine never gates nucleation on SI; there is no literal internal contradiction in its own logic. That is the best defense.

It does NOT survive. It reframes the bug rather than dissolving it: (1) The sim's OWN better-science instrument (SI, the entire purpose of the v164/165 sulfate Ksp engine) says gypsum is undersaturated for the whole run (max Ω≈0.31), and the divergence is not marginal — σ≈18×redox before T-damping vs SI≈-0.5. A 21-psu chloride brine keeping gypsum undersaturated is geologically CORRECT (MVT vugs carry dissolved sulfate but precipitate no gypsum); the σ heuristic simply omits the ionic-strength/activity suppression SI captures → false positive. (2) Facies: Elmwood is a reduced MVT sulfide+sulfate assemblage; the curated Prospector-Minerals list (calcite, fluorite, barite, sphalerite, galena, marcasite, dolomite, quartz, celestite, bitumen) and the reviewer's mindat check both exclude gypsum/selenite, as do the scenario's own expects_species and notes. (3) No real phenomenon is captured: the nucleation code's comment invokes "oxidation-zone paragenesis / dissolved sulfide surfaces," but THIS run has flat reducing Eh (25 mV), no oxidation events, and growing (not dissolving) pyrite — so it is not modeling a supergene gypsum overprint; it is the σ heuristic firing on the enormous Ca (450, needed for the calcite showcase) × S=120 from fluid_mixing (needed for sphalerite/barite), riding the single-S-field (S-not-SO4) conflation. Note celestine shows the same σ-vs-SI divergence (SI -0.25, nucleates) but celestine IS a documented Elmwood species and its SI sits within the documented Phase-1 SI simplification noise (~0.25) — so celestine is defensible; selenite (SI -0.5, facies-wrong oxidized evaporite) is not. Doctrine fit: the fix is a proper growth-condition/gate lever (gate selenite on the already-computed SI, add a salinity term to σ, or raise sigma_crit), never a scoreboard patch, and not the strawman "delete from expects_species" (it isn't in expects_species). The superior instrument exists in-engine and the gate ignores it — precisely the causal-control loss the doctrine flags.

confirmed.claim: aragonite is the first carbonate to nucleate (step 0, at the 121 C thermal peak), ahead of calcite, in a low-Mg brine (Mg/Ca ~= 0.2).
confirmed.objection: Elmwood's carbonate is famously CALCITE (giant golden dogtooth); aragonite is not a documented phase. At 121 C aragonite is metastable and inverts to calcite rapidly, and in LOW-Mg/Ca water calcite — not aragonite — is the kinetically favored polymorph. Nucleating aragonite first, at the hottest step, in a low-Mg brine, inverts the real polymorph selection.
confirmed.evidence: Elmwood species list (web) = calcite, no aragonite. minerals.json aragonite carries a 'T > ~50 C in low-Mg waters' trigger (cited Morse 1997 / Sun 2015) and redox 'any'; that clause fires at 121 C and over-produces aragonite where, at Mg/Ca 0.2, calcite should win. The mineral's own note concedes aragonite half-life ~10^3 yr at 80 C, faster hotter — so a preserved primary aragonite here is not what the deposit shows.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: T-gate aragonite nucleation to low T and/or require high Mg/Ca (> ~1.5) so at the 121 C peak the stable calcite nucleates instead; tighten the misfiring 'T>50 C low-Mg -> aragonite' clause. Elmwood's Mg/Ca ~0.2 and hot start should route the first carbonate to calcite.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest defense I could build, and where it breaks:

(1) REFUTE THE WORDING — partly succeeds. The claim "aragonite is the first carbonate to nucleate, AHEAD of calcite" is factually wrong about the sim. Seed-42 log: `Calcite #1` fires first (σ=4.91), THEN `Aragonite #2` (σ=1.14), both at step 0. Calcite leads the cascade and is the dominant, persisting showcase phase; aragonite is a single subordinate crystal. The claim card's table lists aragonite above calcite only because it sorts by (first_step, then alphabetical) — the reviewer read an alphabetical table as a paragenetic sequence. So "ahead of calcite / inverts the polymorph selection" is overstated: calcite still clearly wins the deposit.

(2) GROUND THE SCIENCE — partly succeeds. Aragonite fires on real controls, not RNG: mg_factor=0.013 (Mg correctly excluded, as it should be), T_factor=0.99 at 120°C (Burton & Walter 1987 / travertine-aragonite T-threshold), plus a genuine trace boost — Elmwood's brine IS Sr/Pb/Ba-rich (it grows celestine, cerussite, and 11 barite events), and Sr/Pb/Ba really do promote aragonite via its 9-fold Ca site. So the firing is a defensible "hot, trace-rich carbonate fluid" signal, and Ostwald-step metastable-precursor-first is mechanistically real.

WHERE THE DEFENSE FAILS — the objection survives in substance:
- Aragonite is NOT a documented Elmwood phase (mindat + prospectorminerals: calcite golden dogtooth + dolomite only). The sim injects a species absent from the world-reference type locality, at the HOTTEST/earliest step — the opposite pole from where the one plausible real occurrence (late, cool, supergene karst speleothem aragonite) would sit.
- Preservation is impossible: at 120°C aragonite is strongly metastable; the mineral's own note gives half-life ~10^3 yr at 80°C (faster hotter). In a Paleozoic-mineralized MVT deposit it would have inverted ~10^8 yr ago. The mineral even CARRIES a `polymorphic_conversion` (aragonite→calcite at T>100°C, which elmwood satisfies) — but it is not wired into the runtime, so the sim grows and PRESERVES the impossible phase.
- Causal-control regression (the drift-doctrine crux): the aragonite entry's own audit_status states low-Mg scenarios "correctly favor calcite" and should NOT nucleate aragonite at seed 42. Elmwood's Mg/Ca was deliberately set to 0.2 precisely to get calcite-not-aragonite — the 0.70-weighted Mg lever — yet the 0.20-weighted T-term (a travertine/open-system-degassing effect over-extended into a closed 200-bar burial vug) plus the trace boost defeat it. That is exactly the "change growth conditions and it should move; here it doesn't" failure the doctrine flags.

The fix is a clean growth-condition lever, not a scoreboard patch, and aragonite isn't in expects_species: (a) wire the already-documented T>100°C polymorphic_conversion so the metastable precursor inverts to calcite (leaving exactly the calcite showcase nature shows), or (b) add a Mg/Ca floor to the aragonite gate so low-Mg brines can't clear it on T+trace alone. Because the sim grows and preserves a phase that is undocumented at the locality and physically un-preservable, and does so against its own author's stated low-Mg contract, the objection stands.

confirmed.claim: ZnS nucleates as WURTZITE (hexagonal, step 19, ~180 °C) as the dominant zinc phase, while sphalerite fires only once, late and cool (step 112).
confirmed.objection: The North Pennine zinc ore is textbook SPHALERITE ('blende/blackjack'); natural wurtzite there is essentially absent. Wurtzite is the metastable ZnS polymorph — the equilibrium sphalerite→wurtzite inversion is ~1020 °C, so there is no thermodynamic basis for treating 95-1020 °C as a wurtzite-stability field. Real low-T wurtzite (schalenblende) is a colloform, ACIDIC (pH<~5), sulfur-deficient, high-supersaturation curiosity — the opposite of this near-neutral (pH 7.2) 180 °C vein. The sim's own note concedes low-T wurtzite is metastable, yet the active gate makes 180 °C ZnS wurtzite, inverting the natural polymorph selection for a sphalerite district.
confirmed.evidence: js/41-supersat-sulfide.ts: MINERAL_GATES_wurtzite T_optimal 200, note 'high-T sphalerite dimorph + low-T metastable (pH<4, Fe>5)'; sphalerite note 'Wurtzite dimorph above 95°C'. Card shows wurtzite first at step 19 (T~180) vs sphalerite a single event at step 112. Accepted mineralogy (Scott & Barnes; equilibrium ~1020 °C) makes sphalerite the stable ZnS at all crustal T; wurtzite is kinetically favored only by low pH / high supersaturation. North Pennine Zn = sphalerite (BGS).
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Retire the 'T>95 => wurtzite' stability reading (no thermodynamic basis) and gate wurtzite on the genuine natural window — acidic pH (<~5), high supersaturation, S-deficiency, low-T colloform. At this scenario's pH 7.2 / 180 °C the ZnS then routes to sphalerite, matching the district. This is a nucleation-gate threshold change (a growth-condition lever), not deleting wurtzite.

refutation.reasoning:
Best defense: wurtzite does occur in some low-T hydrothermal settings, and Scott and Barnes 1972 make the transition sulfur-activity dependent, so 180C wurtzite is not per se impossible. But this fails. The sim high-T branch (js/41) fires whenever S is above 10 in a near-neutral brine, i.e. exactly when the fluid is S-rich, the opposite of the sulfur-deficient condition that stabilizes wurtzite. Real low-T wurtzite is correctly captured by the separate metastable branch (gated pH under 4, Fe over 5, per Murowchick and Barnes 1986); the high-T branch has no such gating and out-competes sphalerite (factor 1.4 vs 0.33 at 180C) across the whole 95 to 300C window, making wurtzite the default ZnS. That contradicts the sim own research doc line 23: sphalerite is the equilibrium phase at all crustal temperatures, sphalerite always wins below 1000C. The code puts the crossover at 95C yet calls it the equilibrium regime, an internal contradiction. The anchor is a textbook sphalerite district (North Pennine blackjack, Dunham 1990 BGS) with no notable wurtzite; the card itself flags wurtzite as an unintended surprise. The sim inverts polymorph selection for a blende district. Fix is a growth-condition lever: raise the wurtzite high-T crossover toward realistic T or gate it on sulfur-deficiency and rapid cooling so sphalerite wins by default in S-rich near-neutral fluids. Severity medium because it is a systemic polymorph inversion across every hot Zn-S scenario, but sphalerite is still present and it is a mislabel within the correct chemistry, not a phantom phase.</parameter>
</invoke>

confirmed.claim: Aragonite (orthorhombic CaCO3) nucleates at step 19 in a 180 °C hydrothermal vein alongside calcite.
confirmed.objection: In a hot (180 °C) vein CaCO3 must crystallize as calcite; aragonite is metastable at these temperatures and inverts to calcite. Aragonite is favored by LOW T and HIGH Mg/Ca (Mg poisons calcite growth) — but this broth has Mg/Ca = 30/300 = 0.1, far too low to force aragonite, and SI_calcite (0.378) exceeds SI_aragonite (0.315), so calcite is the more supersaturated phase. Real North Pennine carbonate gangue is calcite (with ankerite/siderite/dolomite), never aragonite. Growing aragonite here mislabels the vein carbonate.
confirmed.evidence: Card environment: T 180 °C, Mg/Ca=0.1, SI_calcite 0.378 > SI_aragonite 0.315. Morse & Mackenzie / Folk: aragonite precipitation requires elevated Mg/Ca and/or low T; it is metastable and inverts to calcite above ~85 °C over geologic time. minerals.json notes aragonite was added as a 'Mg/Ca-driven polymorph competition with calcite (Folk 1974/Morse 1997)' phase — that competition should resolve to calcite at Mg/Ca 0.1 and 180 °C.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Gate aragonite nucleation on a low-T ceiling (<~50-60 °C) AND an elevated Mg/Ca floor; at 180 °C with Mg/Ca=0.1 it must not clear the gate. If aragonite competition is desired it belongs to the coolest breach tail with the Mg axis raised, not the 180 °C stage-1 broth. Threshold lever on the existing polymorph gate.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest defense I could build, and why each prong fails:

(1) "Aragonite occurs in the district." Aragonite does exist in Pennine mine workings as a LATE, ambient-T, supergene/speleothem phase (flos-ferri style). But the sim fires it at STEP 19-20, as an early first-generation phase at T=152 C alongside the primary sulfides — not as a cool waning-stage secondary. North Pennine PRIMARY vein carbonate gangue is calcite (+ ankerite/ferroan dolomite, siderite, witherite), never primary aragonite. So the occurrence fact doesn't rescue the specific claim.

(2) "The environment is within range." I ran the real v226 engine: aragonite nucleates at T=152 C, Mg/Ca=0.075, with sig_calc (2.99->5.0) vastly exceeding sig_arag (0.66->1.15). That is squarely calcite territory on every real axis. The ONLY thing giving aragonite a foothold is the model's T_factor.

(3) "Trace Pb/Ba/Sr templates aragonite" (real chemistry — cerussite/witherite/strontianite ARE aragonite-group). In the code trace_factor is only a 1.0-1.3 multiplier; it is not what crosses the threshold, and dissolved Pb/Ba/Sr at 150 C form their OWN carbonates, not aragonitic CaCO3. Not a valid rescue.

(4) "Paragenetic misread — co-nucleation isn't strict sequence." True that calcite is step 0 and aragonite step 19, but the objection's core isn't ordering; it's that aragonite forms AT ALL at 150-180 C / low Mg. Unaffected.

Root cause (decisive): the aragonite kinetic-favorability T_factor = 1/(1+exp(-(T-50)/15)) is a MONOTONIC-INCREASING sigmoid saturating at ~1.0 for all T>100 C. It encodes the Burton & Walter 1987 low-T marine 'warm favors aragonite' relationship (valid ~0-50 C, at most ~90 C travertine) and mis-extrapolates it into the 150-180 C hydrothermal regime, where aragonite is metastable and rapidly inverts to calcite (minerals.json itself: half-life ~10^3 yr at 80 C, 'faster in hot wet systems'). Favorability decomposition shows the T_factor supplies ~88% of the favorability that fires aragonite; mg_factor is correctly ~0.009. So the model has the physics BACKWARDS at these temperatures: it maximally favors the polymorph nature destroys. This also falsifies minerals.json's own audit claim that 'existing low-Mg brines correctly favor calcite' — a Mg/Ca=0.075 brine nucleated aragonite purely because the saturated T_factor overrode the (correctly near-zero) mg_factor.

The defense genuinely fails. And the fix is a clean growth-condition/law lever, not a scoreboard edit: reshape T_factor into a band-pass function (rise from cold, peak ~40-90 C, decline toward 0 above ~100-150 C into the inversion regime). That increases causal control — aragonite then fires only when conditions truly favor it (warm-not-hot + high Mg/Ca + high Omega + trace) — which is exactly why the finding is doctrine-useful and the objection stands.

confirmed.claim: Greenockite (CdS) nucleates as a discrete phase 4 times (step 21), more often than sphalerite (1 event), from a broth trace of Cd=2.
confirmed.objection: Cadmium at trace level is a lattice guest — it substitutes into sphalerite/wurtzite (ZnS) and is what makes 'cadmium-bearing sphalerite' yellow; discrete greenockite is a rare, usually supergene, yellow coating on sphalerite. Four discrete primary CdS events from 2 ppm Cd, outnumbering the actual zinc-sulfide events, is a confabulated abundance that mis-partitions a trace metal into its own mineral instead of into the dominant sulfide.
confirmed.evidence: Card: greenockite 4 events from Cd=2 vs sphalerite 1 event. Standard sulfide geochemistry: Cd(II) is chalcophile and camouflages in sphalerite (typ. hundreds-to-thousands ppm Cd in ZnS); greenockite as a distinct species is uncommon and mostly supergene/coating. js/61 grow_greenockite fires on a low Cd threshold without a ZnS-partition sink.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Add a Cd-into-ZnS partition sink (draw Cd down as sphalerite/wurtzite grows) and/or raise greenockite's nucleation sigma so a discrete CdS phase only appears at much higher Cd. Trace Cd then reports as Cd-bearing sphalerite, not four greenockite crystals — a partitioning/threshold growth-condition change.

refutation.reasoning:
STRONGEST DEFENSE I could mount, and why it fails:

(A) "The deposit really hosts greenockite." True — North Pennine sphalerite is notably cadmium-bearing and greenockite is a documented rare YELLOW SUPERGENE COATING on sphalerite there. But this defense actually confirms the objection: the sim does NOT model a late, low-T, oxidizing supergene coat. It fires greenockite at step 21 as a HOT (~160°C), REDUCING (O2=0.25), PRIMARY phase co-nucleating with galena/acanthite/barite — 4 discrete events, outnumbering the ZnS (wurtzite 1 + sphalerite 1). The engine's OWN docstring (js/41:942 "Forms by sphalerite oxidation releasing Cd") describes a supergene genesis, yet the gate `if(O2>0.5)return 0` fires it only under reducing conditions — the exact opposite redox regime. Presence-of-greenockite does not license primary reducing greenockite outnumbering ZnS.

(B) "Cd=2 is defensible / co-nucleation isn't a sequence claim." The Cd broth value IS realistic (Cd/Zn = 2/150 ≈ 1.3%, a normal fluid ratio). But the objection is about PHASE PARTITION and ABUNDANCE, not the value or the order. Per the boss drift doctrine the broth is not the lever to indict — the ENGINE is: js/41 supersaturation_greenockite uses a 1.5 ppm reference so 2 ppm Cd reads as sigma≈6 (I traced: (2/1.5)*(120/30)*1.12), beating sphalerite's sigma≈1.8 from Zn=150. And there is NO Cd→ZnS partition sink — greenockite and sphalerite each check independent supersaturation, so the trace metal spawns its own mineral instead of camouflaging into the 75×-more-abundant Zn as Cd-bearing (yellow) sphalerite, which is what real chalcophile Cd does (typ. hundreds–thousands ppm Cd hosted in ZnS).

The defense does not survive. The sim mis-allocates a trace metal into a discrete primary reducing phase, in the wrong redox regime (contradicting its own genesis comment), at an abundance (4× > ZnS) that inverts nature. This is a systemic gate looseness affecting every trace-Cd + high-S scenario (mvt-analog brine), not a one-off. The doctrine-compliant fix is a growth-condition/gate lever: add a Cd→sphalerite partition sink and/or raise the greenockite reference and gate discrete CdS on high Cd/Zn OR oxidizing (supergene) conditions — never delete it from the scoreboard (it isn't in expects_species anyway; it's a spurious surprise).

confirmed.claim: halite (NaCl) nucleates at step 21 and fires 12 events — the single most-fired species in the run — in a Pb-Zn-Ba MVT vug
confirmed.objection: Halite crystallizes only from a fluid at NaCl saturation (~26 wt% NaCl, ~360 g/L Cl). The sim's own environment trajectory reports salinity pinned at 4.724 psu (~0.47 wt%) with a concentration factor of 0.984 (i.e. no evaporation, slightly diluted), and the broth carries only Na 250 / Cl 800 ppm. That fluid is undersaturated with respect to halite by roughly four orders of magnitude; halite cannot grow as a free vug phase from it. Worse, this contradicts the scenario's OWN cited data (Alderton & Bevins 1996: 20 wt% NaCl brine) in the opposite direction — the broth is far too fresh — while still somehow precipitating salt. Even a true 20 wt% Cumbrian brine is undersaturated w.r.t. halite; halite in these veins appears only as fluid-inclusion daughter crystals on cooling, never as vug gangue. 12 events is confabulated abundance for a species that should never nucleate.
confirmed.evidence: Halite solubility ≈ 26.4 wt% NaCl at 25°C, rising only mildly with T (textbook NaCl-H2O phase relations); a 4.7 psu / ~800 ppm Cl fluid is nowhere near this. The card's cited brine (Alderton & Bevins 1996, JGS 153:91-104) is 20 wt% NaCl eq — itself below halite saturation. Card's environment block: salinity 4.724 psu constant, concentration 0.984× constant.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Replace whatever halite nucleation trigger is firing (a naive absolute-Cl or Na×Cl threshold) with a genuine halite-saturation gate keyed to the NaCl activity product vs Ksp(T), or to an evaporative concentration factor >> 1. At concentration 0.984 and ~800 ppm Cl the gate then never opens. Separately reconcile the salinity narrator: if the intent is a real Cumbrian MVT brine, raise broth Na/Cl to the cited ~20 wt% (which lifts the 4.7 psu narrator to ~200 psu) while keeping halite gated at ~26 wt% saturation so it still stays absent.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest defense I could build, and why it fails:

(1) "Does the deposit host halite?" — Caldbeck Fells / North Pennines Pb-Zn-Ba MVT veins carry Na-Cl-rich brines (Alderton & Bevins: ~20 wt% NaCl eq), so halite IS present — but only as fluid-inclusion daughter crystals nucleating inside sealed inclusions on cooling. It is never free vug gangue in these veins. The sim grows it as an open-cavity phase. Defense fails on phase, not presence.

(2) "Is the environment value defensible?" — No. The halite gate reads Na/Cl ppm directly; the broth sets Na 250 / Cl 800 (≈0.13 wt% NaCl) with concentration pinned at 0.984 (no evaporation). σ_halite = (250/100)(800/500)(0.984²)×0.7 = 2.71, well over sigma_crit 1.0. Yet real halite saturation needs ~160,000 ppm Cl — the fluid is ~200× undersaturated. Even the cited 20 wt% brine (~120,000 ppm Cl) is below halite saturation (26 wt% needed). No reading of this fluid is halite-saturated.

(3) "Misread order/abundance?" — No. The v226 strip archive confirms 12 halite nucleation events (first step 21), the single most-fired species. Confabulated dominance is real, not a paragenetic misread.

Root cause (smoking gun): halite's evaporite siblings all enforce a hard evaporation gate — borax, mirabilite, thenardite each have `if (c < 1.5) return 0;`. Halite (and sylvite) have NO concentration floor; the code just multiplies by c². Halite's own comment claims it "stays dormant at scenario baseline (concentration=1)," but nothing enforces that — dormancy silently assumes scenarios keep (Na/100)(Cl/500) < 1 at c=1. The tn457 broth (Na·Cl = 200,000 → base product 4.0) breaks the invariant, so halite fires with zero evaporation. The nucleation narrator even prints "bathtub-ring NaCl precipitating from the evaporating brine" while concentration never leaves 0.984. This is a general engine bug: a real 20 wt% brine (Cl ~120,000) would fire the gate catastrophically in any scenario.

The defense collapses at every angle. The fix is a pure growth-condition lever that INCREASES causal control (add `if (c < 1.5) return 0;` to supersaturation_halite/sylvite, mirroring borax/mirabilite/thenardite), so halite fires only on a vadose concentration spike — never in a submerged MVT vug. No scoreboard edit needed. Objection stands.

confirmed.claim: willemite (Zn2SiO4) nucleates late (first step 71, 3 events) as the oxidized zinc phase, under rising Eh (-31 → +77 mV) and O2 (0.16 → 0.39) at T ~106°C
confirmed.objection: This is the wrong oxidized-Zn mineral for a Cumbria/North Pennines carbonate-hosted MVT deposit. The documented supergene/late zinc sinks in this exact orefield (Nenthead, Cumbria) are hydrozincite and smithsonite (± hemimorphite) — the card itself expects blue/white UV attributed to 'late hydrozincite'. Willemite is diagnostic of a DIFFERENT deposit class: Franklin/Sterling Hill metamorphic Zn, or the silica-rich, sulfide-buffered-away, strongly oxidizing hypogene non-sulfide Zn deposits (Vazante, Beltana, Berg Aukas, Otavi/Kabwe). Growing willemite here is a locality inversion. It is also self-refuting on the specimen's own datum: willemite fluoresces bright GREEN under UV, so if it were really present the specimen would glow green, contradicting the observed/expected blue-white hydrozincite signature. The engine appears to have 'justified' willemite from T (106°C is within the 100-250°C hydrothermal willemite window) + rising Eh + free Si (the same dissolved-Si pool that grew the surprise quartz) — but ignored that the carbonate limestone wall buffers the system toward Zn-carbonate, not Zn-silicate.
confirmed.evidence: Nenthead (Cumbria) secondary-mineral study: principal zinc sinks are hydrozincite + smithsonite, with secondary calcite/aragonite (Geological Society SP 198:241). Brugger & McPhail 2003 (Econ. Geol. 98:819): hydrothermal willemite forms 100-250°C under oxidizing conditions specifically in non-sulfide Zn deposits (Vazante >150°C; Beltana/Berg Aukas/Kabwe low metamorphic grade). Franklin willemite is metamorphic. Willemite's classic bright-green UV vs the card's stated blue/white (hydrozincite) UV.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Route the late oxidized residual Zn to a Zn-carbonate sink (smithsonite/hydrozincite) whenever carbonate activity is high — the limestone wall is explicitly releasing CO3 each acidic pulse — and T is low. Gate willemite nucleation behind carbonate-poor + silica-rich + strongly-oxidizing conditions that this carbonate-buffered basinal brine never reaches (i.e. require low CO3 activity, not just T + Eh + Si). Adding the hydrozincite/smithsonite oxidation pathway both fixes the wrong species and delivers the expected blue/white UV the card already anticipates.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest defense I could build for the sim: hydrothermal willemite in the 100–250°C oxidizing window is a genuine, literature-backed phase (Brugger & McPhail 2003), and this scenario's late fluid really is hot (106°C), strongly oxidizing (Eh +77 mV, O2 0.39), and silica-rich (SiO2 >=100, the same free-Si pool that grew the surprise quartz). The willemite gate opened on in-range conditions, and the engine even self-labels it "SUPERGENE nonsulfide (Skorpion)." One could also argue willemite is only a "surprise," not an expects_species headline (which lists just sphalerite + barite), so the primary coin-stack claim is untouched.

That defense fails on four independent grounds. (1) Locality/deposit-class inversion: hydrothermal/Skorpion-style willemite belongs to dolostone-hosted, sulfide-buffered-away, silica-dominated hypogene non-sulfide Zn systems (Vazante/Beltana/Berg Aukas/Skorpion). This scenario is the opposite — a sulfide MVT where sphalerite is the primary substrate phase (abundant reduced S), limestone-hosted, Mn-pink-barite coin-stack style, i.e. the Nenthead/North Pennines assemblage. Web + primary lit (Geol. Soc. SP 198, the reviewer's own cite) confirm the actual late Zn sinks here are hydrozincite + smithsonite; willemite is unreported in the orefield. (2) Carbonate buffering is ignored in code: the engine gives hemimorphite a discriminator (`if CO3 > SiO2 return 0` → smithsonite wins, js/39:913) but gives willemite NO carbonate-suppression term at all — a genuine, asymmetric gap. In a limestone-buffered fluid oxidized Zn must route to carbonate, not silicate. (3) Thermal-regime mismatch: smithsonite (<~80°C), hydrozincite (cool), and hemimorphite (T_max 50°C) only open in the cold supergene window; the scenario never cools below 106°C, leaving willemite (T_min 50, window to 700°C) as the only Zn-oxidized gate that can fire hot — so the willemite is an artifact of the scenario staying hydrothermal while turning oxidizing, not a modeled supergene phase. (4) Specimen self-refutation: the card's real datum is blue/white UV (hydrozincite); the engine's own hydrozincite comment says "pale-blue SW-UV... NOT Mn-activator like willemite," and its willemite log prints "UV-fluorescent green." Present willemite ⇒ green glow ⇒ contradicts the specimen — and terminal verification against real specimens is the load-bearing test.

The objection therefore stands. Crucially the fix is a proper growth-condition lever that increases causal control (satisfies the drift doctrine): mirror hemimorphite's `CO3 > SiO2 → 0` carbonate discriminator onto `supersaturation_willemite()`, and/or add a genuine cold supergene overprint stage to the scenario (drop T into the <50–80°C window) so the smithsonite/hydrozincite gates open and produce the blue-white-UV phase the specimen actually shows. Never delete willemite from a scoreboard — it isn't in expects_species anyway; the correct move is chemistry/T-trajectory + a nucleation-gate threshold.

confirmed.claim: native_silver (surprise species) crystallizes at step 27 and coexists in the final assemblage with native_tellurium (6 events from step 0) and hessite.
confirmed.objection: Native metallic silver and native tellurium mark opposite extremes of the same tellurium-fugacity buffer and cannot coexist. Native Te records the HIGHEST fTe2 in the system (it is the residual 'relic of excess Te' — the mineral's own minerals.json description says it forms only after every telluride metal, Ag included, is satisfied). Native Ag, by contrast, is stable only at the LOWEST fTe2, after Te is exhausted, together with low fS2. In any fluid where native Te is stable, silver is tellurized to hessite (2Ag + Te -> Ag2Te); metallic Ag + metallic Te would react out. The native_silver supersaturation gate (js/36-supersat-native.ts, fluid_min Ag 1.0, O2_max 0.3) carries NO tellurium suppressor whatsoever, so it fires at Ag=15 while Te is still abundant and native Te is actively growing. The assemblage therefore simultaneously asserts maximal and minimal tellurium fugacity.
confirmed.evidence: Cooke & McPhail / Grundler and the gold-(silver)-telluride reviews establish that telluride paragenesis follows monotonically DECREASING fTe2 (calaverite -> petzite -> hessite), and that native silver requires low fTe2 + low fS2 + low Eh — a geochemical regime distinct from and later than hessite. Native Te fixes the high-fTe2 end of that same buffer. Cross-checked against the Understanding gold-(silver)-telluride-(selenide) deposits review and the Springer Ag-Te-O phase-diagram study (link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00269-017-0889-y): with excess Te present the stable Ag phase is Ag2Te/AgTe, not Ag metal. At Cripple Creek the silver is carried in hessite/petzite/sylvanite; native silver is not a characteristic primary vein phase there.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Add a tellurium-fugacity (or explicit hessite-competition) suppressor to supersaturation_native_silver so that when fluid.Te is high enough to stabilize native_tellurium/hessite, native_silver sigma collapses toward zero — mirroring the ag_suppr term already on native_tellurium but in the opposite direction. Native silver should only be reachable at the tail of the run after Te is locally exhausted (a Te-depletion gate), never concurrently with native Te. Do NOT simply drop native_silver from the scoreboard — the gate is missing a real ligand control.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest defense I could build, and why it fails.

DEFENSE A — "disequilibrium vug / Path C local-vs-bulk: native Te (Te-excess relic) and native Ag (S-depletion relic) are spatially separate grains from different micro-domains; the bulk fluid is an accounting fiction, the two natives never touch or re-equilibrate." This is the sim's own documented rationale and is the only defense with real force. It FAILS on the evidence. I ran the scenario (seed 42, 180 steps) and the bulk equator-ring fluid the σ engine reads is COMPLETELY STATIC: Ag=15.00, Te=3.000, S=8.00, Au=0.40, O2=0.05 for all 180 steps. Nothing depletes — so there is no modeled Te-exhausted micro-domain for native silver to occupy. native_silver fires (σ climbs 1.6→3.0, events at steps 27/29/33/39) purely because falling T enters its 100-200°C T_factor sweet spot, while native_tellurium is still actively growing from the SAME Te=3 fluid (σ≈1.15, events at steps 0/2/7/9/80/173 — i.e. AFTER and concurrent with native_Ag). The gate supersaturation_native_silver (js/36) has literally no Te or fTe2 term. So the sim asserts one homogeneous fluid state that is simultaneously native-Te-saturated (max fTe2) and native-Ag-saturated (min fTe2) — exactly the reviewer's indictment.

DEFENSE B — "supergene overprint: native Te AND native Ag both occur as oxidation products of tellurides, so they co-occur in the deposit." True in nature (Boulder belt, Bambolla/Moctezuma), but inapplicable: the sim grows native_silver under REDUCING (O2≤0.3, nativeRedoxAnoxic) HOT (220-237°C) hypogene conditions, not the oxidizing low-T weathering zone where that association lives.

DEFENSE C — "co-nucleation ≠ strict sequence." Doesn't land; the objection is about COEXISTENCE of two mutually-exclusive-fTe2 phases in the final assemblage, and the sim does assert both concurrent growth and joint final presence.

Thermodynamics independently confirmed: Ag-Te phase relations (Springer 10.1007/s00269-017-0889-y; Ag-Te system studies) place native Te at the high-fTe2 end coexisting with the most Te-rich telluride, native Ag at the low-fTe2 end coexisting with hessite — no shared tie-line; in a native-Te-stable fluid Ag is tellurized to Ag2Te. Locality confirmed (ROM/turnstone, jsjgeology): at Cripple Creek silver partitions into hessite/petzite/sylvanite/krennerite as fluids deplete Au and enrich Ag; native silver is not a primary vein phase. The sim also contradicts its OWN model — native_tellurium's minerals.json description and engine comment say native Te forms only after every telluride metal, Ag included, is satisfied into tellurides, yet native_silver (needing abundant free Ag) fires in the same run from Ag=15.

The objection stands. It is also doctrine-actionable via a growth-condition lever, not a scoreboard patch: add a soft Te suppressor to supersaturation_native_silver mirroring native_tellurium's existing soft Ag suppressor, so native Ag only clears threshold where Te is locally exhausted — which INCREASES causal control, the boss's own test.

Severity nuance: I nudge the reviewer's "high" to medium. The defect is real and categorical (a forbidden max/min-fTe2 coexistence in the flagship telluride scenario, self-inconsistent with the sim's own native_Te story), but native_silver is a SURPRISE species, NOT in expects_species — the scored deliverable (calaverite + sylvanite + hessite + native_tellurium + gangue) is thermodynamically self-consistent and intact. native silver is a genuine district/telluride-oxidation species, so this is mis-conditioning (one gate missing its fTe2 coupling), not an alien phase, and it arises from the documented systemic bulk-view Path C approximation shared across all native-element engines (As/Bi/Cu/Te/Ag) with a clean, low-risk targeted fix. Real and worth fixing, but scenario-noise rather than scenario-invalidating.

confirmed.claim: albite (NaAlSi3O8) grows as a surprise species with 3 events (first step 0), MORE events than the K-feldspar it accompanies (generic 'feldspar' = KAlSi3O8, 2 events), as vein gangue.
confirmed.objection: This is an alkalic-volcanic-hosted low-sulfidation (adularia-type) epithermal vein whose diagnostic alteration is POTASSIC: adularia + sericite/roscoelite. Sodic (albite) alteration is not a Cripple Creek vein assemblage. The broth is emphatically K-dominated (K=200 vs Na=60), which should route feldspar crystallization to adularia (K-feldspar) — which the sim in fact already does via the generic 'feldspar' species. Growing albite from the same fluid, and at higher frequency than the K-feldspar, contradicts the potassic fingerprint the scenario's own notes call 'the diagnostic wallrock fingerprint of alkalic-volcanic-hosted epithermal.' Albite and adularia are competing for the same dissolved Al + SiO2 budget, and in a K>>Na fluid the potassium phase wins; the sim lets both fire independently because the albite gate keys only on Na>=10 with no K/Na partition.
confirmed.evidence: mindat Cripple Creek locality + jsjgeology.net Cripple Creek gold-ore description: gangue is quartz-pyrite-fluorite with adularia (in the disseminated gold-pyrite-adularia breccia ore) plus roscoelite, ankerite, calcite, fluorite. Albite is absent from the documented district mineralogy. The scenario notes themselves specify 'adularia gangue' and 'K-alkaline fluid'. No literature places Na-feldspar in Cripple Creek veins.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Lower broth Na (Cripple Creek fluids are K>>Na; Na=60 is high enough to clear albite's Na>=10 gate — drop it well below that or below the potassic dominance), and/or add a K/Na competition on the shared Al budget so the dominant alkali claims the feldspar (potassic broth -> adularia only). Keep the generic K-feldspar firing; suppress albite via the broth Na lever, not by editing expects_species.

refutation.reasoning:
STRONGEST DEFENSE I could build, and where it breaks:

(1) "Albite is a real Cripple Creek mineral." TRUE and it partly rebuts the reviewer. The reviewer's evidentiary claim that "albite is absent from the documented district mineralogy / no literature places Na-feldspar in Cripple Creek" is factually WRONG: the host phonolite is peralkaline (K+Na>Al) and sodic, and albite is a documented primary igneous phase (Pb-isotope studies separate "K feldspar and albite" from the igneous rocks; the suite also carries nepheline, sodalite, analcime, aegirine). So the sim is not hallucinating a phase absent from the district.

(2) "The sim keeps the potassic fingerprint." TRUE. K-feldspar's supersaturation (~4.9) is ~3x albite's (~1.68), so the sim routes the DOMINANT feldspar crystallization to adularia. The 3-vs-2 event count is pure RNG-cascade noise (both nucleate at step 0; re-nucleation is a coin flip with feldspar at HIGHER σ, HIGHER p=0.3, HIGHER cap=6) — the "albite beats K-feldspar" framing is an artifact of one seed, not a design signal.

(3) Neither albite nor feldspar is in expects_species — both are already logged as "surprises," so the scenario is not ASSERTING albite as a Cripple Creek gangue phase.

WHERE THE DEFENSE FAILS: the defense rescues albite's presence in the DISTRICT (host rock) but not its crystallization as HYDROTHERMAL VEIN GANGUE from the vein fluid, which is exactly what the sim does (albite nucleates "on feldspar / vug wall" in the cavity). The documented vein assemblage is adularia + quartz + fluorite + roscoelite + carbonate — potassic metasomatism, no vein albite. Critically, adularia precipitates even from Na-rich epithermal fluids via the boiling/pH-rise mechanism, so nature routes vein feldspar to K-feldspar regardless of Na content.
