The sim's albite gate keys ONLY on Na>=10 with no aK/aNa competition (verified in supersaturation_albite / MINERAL_GATES_albite — K is ignored entirely), so a K>>Na broth wrongly grows both feldspars independently. That mechanistic gap the reviewer identified is real and verified. Under the drift doctrine the fix is a legitimate growth-condition lever (an aK/aNa partition / competitive routing in the feldspar-vs-albite gate, benefiting all peralkaline scenarios), not a scoreboard patch — which confirms it is a genuine model issue rather than a baseline-hiding artifact.

NET: the objection survives on substance (sim grows an undocumented hydrothermal vein-gangue phase via a cation-blind gate), but its severity is overstated. Downgrade to LOW because: albite is a real district phase, K-feldspar correctly dominates, both feldspars are already unclaimed surprises, and the load-bearing "more events than K-feldspar" detail is RNG noise.

confirmed.claim: ZnS grew as wurtzite (step 0, 1 event) and both sphalerite AND chalcopyrite are no-shows, despite being in expects_species.
confirmed.objection: Stage III (massive galena-SPHALERITE-CHALCOPYRITE-bornite) is the volumetric heart of Sunnyside — ~40% of the entire ore. A biography of this deposit that grows galena but neither sphalerite nor chalcopyrite has omitted the single most diagnostic and abundant ore stage. Worse, the Zn that did precipitate crystallized as wurtzite at 277 °C, where the hexagonal polymorph is thermodynamically out of bounds. A geologist reads a wurtzite-instead-of-sphalerite, no-chalcopyrite assemblage as NOT this deposit.
confirmed.evidence: Casadevall & Ohmoto 1977 (Econ. Geol. 72:1285): Stage III galena-sphalerite-chalcopyrite-bornite-hematite = ~40% of ore; Stage-I–V T = 250–320 °C. Barton & Toulmin / sphalerite-wurtzite equilibria (ScienceDirect 001670377290049X) + the 2023 GCA surface-energetics paper: above ~250 °C the wurtzite field 'lies well outside the normal sulfidation state encountered in ore-forming environments'; wurtzite is favored only at low T in highly reducing (low fS2) fluids. The sim's Eh is a very reducing −150 mV at step 0 — that low-fS2 broth is exactly what tips ZnS to wurtzite and starves chalcopyrite.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Raise the early-stage sulfidation/oxidation of the broth (Eh out of −150 mV toward the intermediate-sulfidation window; higher fS2) at 260–280 °C so the stable cubic sphalerite field is entered and Cu partitions into chalcopyrite; simultaneously cut early As/Sb activity so Cu is not scavenged into tetrahedrite/tennantite before chalcopyrite can nucleate.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest defense I could build, and where it breaks:

DEFENSE 1 — "This scenario is a deliberately late-stage carbonate-fluoride SHOWCASE; the author's own notes say Stages I–IV are compressed to a token step-0 burst because the nucleation cap won't reward fine sulfide sequencing. So the base-metal ore is intentionally out of focus." This is true and legitimately reframes the OMISSION, but it cannot rescue the sim: (a) sphalerite + chalcopyrite are still in expects_species, and Sunnyside genuinely hosts them (Casadevall & Ohmoto Stage III ≈ 40% of ore); (b) far worse than omission, the sim made an AFFIRMATIVE wrong statement — it grew ZnS as WURTZITE. A compression scope-cut could grow no ZnS; growing the wrong polymorph is not defensible as scope.

DEFENSE 2 — "Wurtzite is real ZnS and the broth is reducing + Fe-bearing + acidic (pH 4.5), the conditions literature ties to metastable wurtzite; Sunnyside sphalerite is Fe-rich marmatite, and Fe/low-aS2 expand the wurtzite field." This rescues the plausibility of TRACE wurtzite existing in nature, but it does not rescue the sim, and in fact indicts it. I checked the engines directly: the sim's wurtzite-vs-sphalerite selection is NOT driven by aS2/Fe/acidity at all — it is a naive temperature switch. supersaturation_sphalerite decays as 2.0·exp(−0.01·T) above 95°C (σ=0.30 at 277°C), while supersaturation_wurtzite gets a flat T_factor=1.4 across 150–300°C (σ=3.36 at 277°C) — an 11× inversion on identical Zn/S. So the sim produces wurtzite-over-sphalerite at 277°C in ANY reducing sulfide broth, regardless of sulfur activity. That inverts the real thermodynamics: sphalerite is the stable ZnS to the ~1020°C dry-system transition, and wurtzite is the LOW-T kinetic/metastable phase — precisely what the engine's OWN cited source (Murowchick & Barnes 1986) describes. Nature gives dominant sphalerite ± subordinate wurtzite intergrowth; the sim gives one wurtzite and ZERO sphalerite, and since the run's minimum T is 150.6°C, sphalerite's T_factor never recovers — it is structurally impossible to grow at any point in this scenario.

DEFENSE 3 — "co-nucleation at step 0 isn't a sequence claim." Irrelevant; the objection is about wrong/absent phases, not order.

DRIFT-DOCTRINE TEST: the doctrine says a real finding's fix is a growth-condition lever. Chalcopyrite's absence IS partly tunable (its porphyry-scale denominators Cu/80·Fe/50 starve it at epithermal Cu=25/Fe=10, and fahlores win the Cu — raising Fe/Cu could clear it), so on its own that prong is a medium broth-undercalibration. But the wurtzite inversion is NOT tunable by any broth lever: no Zn/S/pH/Eh setting yields sphalerite at 277°C. That the model cannot be moved back on purpose by changing growth conditions is the doctrine's own signature of a genuine ENGINE defect, not a scenario quibble — and it fires system-wide (every scenario forming ZnS above ~150°C would render hexagonal wurtzite where cubic sphalerite belongs). The defense fails.

confirmed.claim: native_gold (expects_species; the deposit's namesake and a boss calibration specimen — 'native gold in quartz') never nucleated.
confirmed.objection: Sunnyside is literally the 'gold and base metal' deposit of the Casadevall & Ohmoto title; Stage IV is Au-Te-quartz. The reason gold precipitates in these systems is boiling: Period VI fluids BOILED at ~240 °C, dumping H2S and destabilizing the Au(HS)2⁻ complex. This sim never boils — CO2aq actually RISES from 49 to ~142 mg/L (the opposite of degassing) and Eh stays reducing (−150 → −46 mV) the whole run, so aqueous gold is never forced out of solution. The single most famous product of the vein is absent for a mechanistic reason the scenario didn't model.
confirmed.evidence: Casadevall & Ohmoto 1977: Period VI boiled at ~240 °C; Stage IV = Au-Te-quartz. Guerra-Barreto et al. 2024, Mineralium Deposita 'Polymetallic vein formation through fluid FLASHING at the Sunnyside intermediate-sulfidation epithermal deposit' — flashing/boiling is the named ore-forming trigger. Gold-bisulfide solubility drops sharply on H2S loss during boiling (Seward, Williams-Jones).
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Add a Period-VI boiling/flashing event around 240 °C (a CO2/H2S-degassing pulse that drops dissolved sulfide and steps Eh), rather than the current monotonic CO2 rise; that same event drives the gold-saturation ledge and precipitates native gold in the quartz phase.

refutation.reasoning:
Empirically the card records native_gold under "No-shows," and the code shows this is structural, not stochastic. native_gold's σ (js/36:411-425) is au_f·s_f·T_factor with au_f=min(Au/1.0,4.0), s_f=max(0.2,1−S/200), σ_crit=1.0 and no substrate discount (js/86:137). The broth seeds Au=0.5 — exactly the fluid_min floor — so au_f caps at 0.5; even with total desulfidation (s_f→1.0) and optimal T, σ_max=0.5 < 1.0. Gold cannot nucleate under ANY trajectory this scenario can reach. Cross-check confirms the author's own calibration sense: at scenarios.json5:1490 Au=0.3 is annotated "sub-threshold; no native gold nucleation expected," and the two gold-growing scenarios seed Au=2 and 3. 0.5 is in the dead band. So a headline expected species — the deposit's namesake (Casadevall & Ohmoto "gold and base metal"), a physical boss calibration specimen ("native gold in quartz"), and an explicit expects_species entry — is impossible to grow.

Strongest defense I could mount, and why it fails: (1) The reviewer's MECHANISM is slightly off-target — the sim is NOT blind to gold desulfidation. The s_f=1−S/200 term is precisely a bisulfide-complex destabilization proxy: high S keeps Au(HS)2⁻ in solution, dropping S precipitates gold. So "this sim never forces gold out of solution" is imprecise; the lever exists. But this doesn't rescue the sim — it only shows the fix is a legitimate growth-condition lever, not a missing physics module. Even a modeled boiling/H2S-dump event would be necessary-but-insufficient here, because au_f is pinned at 0.5 by the low seed; you must ALSO raise broth Au. (2) One could argue gold-in-quartz is Stage IV ore, a different paragenetic phase from the late carbonate-fluoride pocket, i.e. expects_species inflation. This fails three ways: the scenario explicitly compresses and models Stages I-IV at steps 1-30 and names the Au-quartz specimen as a calibration target; a real specimen exists; and the drift doctrine expressly forbids "delete the species from expects_species" as the remedy. The honest fix is a growth-condition change (raise seeded Au to ~2 ppm, and/or add a Stage IV boiling/desulfidation event that sharply drops S), which is exactly what the doctrine sanctions. The objection's conclusion — the vein's single most famous product is absent for a modelable reason the scenario didn't wire in — survives every defense.

confirmed.claim: Paragenetic order: rhodochrosite (Stage V) nucleates at step 20, but quartz at step 49 and pyrite (Stage I) at step 111 — a Stage-I sulfide appearing 90 steps AFTER a Stage-V carbonate.
confirmed.objection: This inverts the deposit's paragenesis and the scenario's own stated design. Casadevall & Ohmoto's Stage I is pyrite-quartz — the earliest, most pervasive gangue+sulfide; Mn-carbonate (Stage V) overgrows the quartz-sulfide banding, never precedes it. The author's note claims Stages I–IV were 'compressed into steps 1-30,' but the testimony shows pyrite at 111, quartz at 49, arsenopyrite at 83 — the compression the note asserts did not happen. Real specimens show rhodochrosite rhombs perched ON quartz-sulfide, not buried under later pyrite.
confirmed.evidence: Casadevall & Ohmoto 1977 stage order: I pyrite-quartz (5%) → II banded quartz-sulfide (30%) → III base metals (40%) → IV Au-Te → V Mn carbonate (20%) → VI quartz-fluorite-carbonate. Pyrite + quartz are the paragenetic floor of essentially every epithermal vein.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Front-load silica and Fe-sulfide saturation so quartz and pyrite reach SI>0 in the opening steps (higher initial dissolved SiO2 and a sufficient early fS2), and gate the Mn-carbonate driver behind a scenario event (delay the Mn²⁺ enrichment / supersaturation window to step ~30+) so rhodochrosite cannot nucleate before the primary quartz-pyrite framework.

refutation.reasoning:
STRONGEST DEFENSE I could mount, on four fronts:

(1) Single-episode-model defense: vugg models one continuous cooling fill, not six discrete fracture-reopening events spanning ~1.5 Myr. The real I–VI paragenesis was set by tectonic reopening pulses the engine physically cannot encode, so the emergent order shouldn't be read as a paragenetic CLAIM. — Survives only partially: within a single monotonic cooling run, thermodynamics STILL predict pyrite (Fe-S, hot) early and rhodochrosite (Mn-CO3, cool, CO2-degassed) late. The sim inverts even that, so the inversion is a broth miscalibration, not an unavoidable compression artifact.

(2) Quartz-is-Stage-VI defense: the reviewer lumps quartz-at-49 in as proof compression failed, but Casadevall Stage VI is literally "quartz-fluorite-carbonate," which POSTDATES Stage V rhodochrosite. So late quartz (49) and fluorite (109) after rhodochrosite (20) are actually CORRECT ordering. — This genuinely lands: it weakens the reviewer's quartz evidence and shows the scenario's SHOWCASE paragenesis (rhodochrosite 20 → fluorite 109 → manganocalcite cap) is correctly sequenced. But it does not save pyrite: the sim also has NO early Stage-I quartz, and pyrite is the diagnostic floor.

(3) Late-pyrite-is-real defense: epithermal veins do host late pyrite generations. — Fails on the specific charge: the sim has ZERO early pyrite. Casadevall Stage I is by definition "pyrite-quartz," the most pervasive assemblage; a Sunnyside sim whose pyrite first appears at step 111, buried OVER the rhodochrosite instead of under it, contradicts every real specimen (rhombs perch ON quartz-pyrite).

(4) Early-ore-is-represented defense: galena, tetrahedrite, argentite, wurtzite, tennantite all fire at step 0, so the primary stage isn't absent. — True and mitigating, but pyrite specifically — the paragenetic floor — is the one missing, and the scenario's own event handler NARRATES "pyrite + galena + sphalerite + chalcopyrite solidify" at the Stage I→V transition while pyrite hadn't nucleated. The narration lies about its own crystal record; the author note's "compressed into steps 1-30" is falsified (pyrite 111, arsenopyrite 83).

NET: the core objection survives — a Stage-I sulfide first nucleating 90 steps after a Stage-V carbonate is a genuine, specimen-falsifiable inversion. Crucially it is FIXABLE via a growth-condition lever, exactly per doctrine: the handler already drops Fe*0.15 assuming pyrite consumed it early, but the initial broth never clears pyrite's sigma_crit at high T (corroborated by SI_siderite only reaching saturation at run's end — Fe/S delivered late). Front-loading initial Fe/S so pyrite nucleates at step 0-5 restores the floor without touching expects_species or the baseline. That makes this a real finding, not a scoreboard artifact.

I correct severity HIGH→MEDIUM because the defense partially holds: the scenario's actual scientific payload (the late carbonate-fluoride display assemblage — the boss's real specimens) is correctly ordered, the reviewer over-reads quartz (Stage VI quartz legitimately postdates rhodochrosite), the author explicitly flagged the primary-phase compression, and other early sulfides carry the primary stage. The inversion is a confirmed defect in a deliberately-compressed, de-emphasized phase, not a corruption of what the scenario is for.

confirmed.claim: calcite (expects_species) and the headline Stage-VI manganocalcite cap never nucleated; SI_calcite peaks at only −0.504 (never reaches saturation).
confirmed.objection: The 'cauliflower botryoidal manganocalcite cap' is one of the two headline specimen types the scenario is calibrated to reproduce (boss: 'brilliant salmon fluorescence, much brighter than most'). It never grows because the fluid never reaches calcite saturation — pH caps at 6.6 and, with an inert wall (reactivity 0) and no CO2 degassing, nothing pushes SI_calcite to ≥0. Real manganocalcite caps form precisely when late boiling/CO2 loss spikes pH above 7 and slams carbonate saturation up.
confirmed.evidence: Casadevall & Ohmoto 1977 Stage VI carbonate; boiling at ~240 °C. CO2 degassing on boiling raises pH and is the classic driver of late vein calcite/manganocalcite saturation (Simmons & Christenson, epithermal calc). The strip shows CO2aq climbing 49→142 mg/L and SI_calcite ceiling −0.504 — the fluid is being loaded with CO2, not degassed.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: The same Period-VI boiling/CO2-degassing event: model CO2 loss (not gain) so pH rises past 7 and SI_calcite crosses 0 in the final steps, letting the Mn²⁺-bright manganocalcite cap nucleate over the fluorite. Do NOT simply lower the calcite SI threshold — fix the CO2 trajectory.

refutation.reasoning:
STRONGEST DEFENSE I could mount (steelman): (a) Manganocalcite is genuinely rare at Sunnyside — the boss owns 1 cap among ~20 Silverton pieces — while the dominant assemblage the locality is known for (pale-pink rhodochrosite 15/20 + octahedral REE-fluorite) IS reproduced (rhodochrosite nucleates step 20, fluorite step 109). (b) A prior builder explicitly probed this exact "bump CO3 to fire the manganocalcite cap" item and REJECTED it (2026-05-27), arguing peak calcite omega was 1.045 — thermodynamically marginal, right at the noise floor and below the honest heterogeneous-nucleation barrier (sigma_crit 1.5) — so calcite marginally-not-firing was "working as designed" and matched the rare-specimen evidence. (c) Rhodochrosite carries the Mn-carbonate stage.

The defense DOES NOT SURVIVE. I ran the nucleation-gate probe through the real engine path (conditions.supersaturation_calcite()) at HEAD/v226: peak calcite omega = 0.228 (step 160, T=164°C, Ca=220, CO3=150), first gate-clear NEVER, 0 calcite crystals — corroborating the claim card's SI_calcite max −0.504 (omega≈0.31). This falsifies every plank of the defense: (1) "Rare, not forbidden" is dead — omega 0.228 (SI ~−0.5 to −0.64) is DEEPLY undersaturated, and chemistry is deterministic across seeds (RNG only gates nucleation attempts, not the omega ceiling), so NO seed can ever nucleate manganocalcite. "Rare" would require omega to occasionally poke above ~1.5; 0.228 forbids it everywhere. The prior rejection's omega=1.045 is STALE — the scenario silently drifted ~4.4× further from saturation since ~v157, and the softened test hid it (precisely the erosion the doctrine warns about; "verify before asserting state" applies). (2) Rhodochrosite (MnCO3) ≠ manganocalcite ((Ca,Mn)CO3); the boss specifically flags the manganocalcite's "brilliant salmon fluorescence, much brighter than most" as a distinct headline, and calcite is independently in expects_species — the scenario itself asserts it must appear. (3) The reviewer's mechanistic diagnosis is exactly right and is a real modeling error, not a defensible environment value: js/70p-sunnyside.ts handlers ADD to the DIC field (c.fluid.CO3 += 30/35/40, mislabeled "CO2 degassing") while capping pH at 6.65 — the chemical OPPOSITE of degassing, which removes DIC and spikes pH. The scenario note promises "late-stage neutralization comes from CO₂ degassing," yet the broth loads carbon (reviewer's CO2aq 49→142 mg/L). At pH 6.6 the CO3²⁻ activity is ~4 orders below DIC, so calcite cannot saturate regardless of DIC; textbook epithermal Stage VI manganocalcite forms via boiling/CO2-loss raising pH toward 7.5–8+ (Simmons & Christenson; Casadevall & Ohmoto Stage VI). Thermal-pulse reheating (T rebounds 164→248–262°C) also prevents the cool low-supersaturation cap window from being held. Decisively for the doctrine, the fix IS a growth-condition lever — a proper degassing/boiling event (drop total DIC, raise pH >7, hold cool cap T, thermal_pulses:false) pushes SI_calcite over sigma_crit on purpose — whereas the prior resolution (soften the test, reinterpret calcite as opportunistic) is the scoreboard-patch the doctrine explicitly forbids. The objection is both correct and actionable.

confirmed.claim: A silver-sulfosalt suite dominates the primary assemblage: tetrahedrite 8 events (the single most abundant phase in the run), plus argentite (4), proustite (2), tennantite (1) — none in expects_species.
confirmed.objection: Sunnyside is a gold + base-metal (Zn-Pb-Cu) vein; silver is subordinate and carried mainly IN tetrahedrite, not as a proustite-argentite-tetrahedrite dominant suite. Casadevall & Ohmoto's six stages list no silver-sulfosalt stage at all. Making tetrahedrite the most abundant mineral and adding argentite + proustite reskins the biography as a low-sulfidation silver vein (a Freibergite/proustite Ag district), the opposite of what the base-metal-gold Sunnyside is. This is confabulated abundance, and it is the Cu/Ag sink that starves chalcopyrite.
confirmed.evidence: Casadevall & Ohmoto 1977: ore-stage volumetrics are pyrite-quartz 5 / banded 30 / base metals 40 / Au-Te 1–2 / Mn 20 / late gangue 5 — no silver-sulfosalt-dominant stage; Ag is a minor tetrahedrite-hosted component of an intermediate-sulfidation base-metal system.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Lower Ag, As and Sb activities in the early broth so the Cu budget crystallizes as chalcopyrite and Zn as sphalerite; reserve a small tetrahedrite/proustite bloom for a discrete, minor late-Ag pulse rather than letting sulfosalts dominate the primary phase from step 0.

refutation.reasoning:
STRONGEST DEFENSE I could mount, and where it breaks:

(1) "The species are real, not confabulated." This part of the defense HOLDS and it dents the objection. Mindat types Sunnyside "Au-Pb-Mn-Ag-Zn-Cu" (Ag is a named commodity) and explicitly documents acanthite AND tetrahedrite-subgroup minerals at the Sunnyside Mine group, plus native silver. Casadevall's Stage III fahlore is the district's principal Ag carrier. So the reviewer's framing — "reskins the biography as a low-sulfidation silver vein, the opposite of Sunnyside" / "confabulated abundance" — OVERSTATES: these are genuine Sunnyside phases, and the scenario notes + test deliberately model Ag-sulfosalts as part of the (explicitly compressed, non-finely-sequenced) primary ore.

(2) "The cited mechanism is wrong." This part of the defense fully SUCCEEDS. I traced the metal budget across all 200 steps: Cu holds flat at 25.0 ppm the entire run — it never depletes. There is no "Cu/Ag sink." Chalcopyrite fails on its OWN low driving force: σ_cpy ≈ 0.18 at start even at full Cu, because Fe=10 is low and T≈278°C is below its 300-500°C porphyry sweet spot; at step 29 Fe drops to 1.5 (below the Fe≥5 gate) → σ_cpy=0; even when Fe recovers to 20-40 later, σ_cpy only reaches ~0.2-0.39, never competitive with galena (6.6) or tetrahedrite (1-2). So "This is the Cu/Ag sink that starves chalcopyrite" is factually refuted.

WHERE THE DEFENSE BREAKS (why the objection still survives): the load-bearing claim is the PROPORTIONS, and the sim is not defensibly correct there. The run makes tetrahedrite the single most abundant phase and yields 15 silver-sulfosalt events (8 tet + 4 Ag2S + 2 proustite + 1 tennantite) against only 5 base-metal-sulfide events (4 galena + 1 wurtzite) with ZERO sphalerite and ZERO chalcopyrite — despite Zn=120 being the highest metal in the broth. The base-metal signature (sphalerite/marmatite + chalcopyrite) that DEFINES Casadevall's Stage III and the deposit type is missing, contradicting the volumetrics (base metals ~40%, no Ag-sulfosalt stage). Even reading vugg "events" as nucleation-slot counts rather than volume %, a faithful Sunnyside primary ore must nucleate sphalerite and some chalcopyrite; their total absence while Ag2S posts the highest σ in the system (Ag=8 ppm maxes the engine's ag_f cap at 6.25) is an inversion, not a defensible rendering. The root cause is a miscalibrated scenario broth (Ag too high, Fe too low), and the doctrine-correct fix is a growth-condition lever — lower Ag to ~1-2 and raise Fe to ~30-40 — not a scoreboard/expects_species edit. The engine tracking the broth faithfully is a mark in the engine's favor, but the scenario as authored over-represents silver-sulfosalts and drops the defining base metals. Objection's conclusion stands; two of its supporting claims (confabulation framing; Cu-sink mechanism) are wrong/overstated.

confirmed.claim: realgar nucleates at step 0 with 6 events — the joint-most-abundant mineral in the whole run — with orpiment (step 0) as a co-primary arsenic phase
confirmed.objection: Realgar (As4S4) and orpiment (As2S3) are low-temperature hot-spring / epithermal / fumarolic arsenic sulfides. The documented Roughton Gill primary assemblage is galena + chalcopyrite + sphalerite (± pyrite, tetrahedrite/tennantite) in quartz-carbonate-baryte gangue; realgar/orpiment appear in NONE of the Russell Society or Cooper & Stanley species lists for the mine. Having realgar be the most abundant phase in a 127 °C Na-Ca-Cl basinal-brine Pb-Cu-Zn vein is both a wrong-species and a confabulated-abundance error — a geologist reads bulk realgar as a Carlin/hot-spring signature, not a Caldbeck fissure vein.
confirmed.evidence: Russell Society review (Bridges et al. 2011 / Green et al. 2008): 'The primary mineralisation at Roughton Gill comprises galena, chalcopyrite and sphalerite in a quartz-carbonate gangue with some baryte'; ~50 supergene species are described and realgar/orpiment are absent from them. Realgar/orpiment genesis is textbook low-T (geology.com, mindat min-3375): 'low-temperature hydrothermal veins, hot spring deposits, and fumarole sublimates.' The As budget of this deposit is carried by the Cu-As sulfosalts (tennantite/tetrahedrite, both present) and by supergene mimetite/olivenite/mottramite — not by free As-S.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Do not let dissolved As precipitate as free realgar/orpiment at the reducing 127 °C primary stage: (a) add a temperature-ceiling gate so realgar/orpiment only nucleate below ~120 °C AND only when base-metal (Cu+Pb+Zn) activity is low, and (b) route the primary-stage As into the Cu-As sulfosalt sink via a metal:As co-precipitation gate feeding tennantite/tetrahedrite, so this Cu-Pb-Zn-rich hot broth grows sulfosalts, not bulk arsenic sulfide.

refutation.reasoning: Strongest defense assembled: (1) the As budget is genuinely present at Roughton Gill, so the sim mis-speciates a real element rather than inventing one, and it does correctly grow the true As hosts (tennantite, tetrahedrite at step 0; mimetite/pyromorphite/mottramite in the supergene); (2) 127 C is inside realgar's stability field, so temperature alone isn't disqualifying; (3) the objection slightly overstates rank — mottramite fires 7x vs realgar's 6x, so realgar is tied-second, not strictly the joint-most-abundant phase. None of this survives. Bulk free As-sulfide is a deposit-TYPE signature (Carlin / hot-spring / low-sulfidation epithermal); Roughton Gill is a Pb-Cu-Zn base-metal fissure vein that documents its arsenic ONLY as Cu-sulfosalts (tetrahedrite-tennantite) and oxidized supergene phases (mimetite, bayldonite, olivenite, even claudetite As2O3) — never reduced free As-S. Verified against the very sources the card cites: Russell Society Part 3 gives the primary assemblage as galena+chalcopyrite+sphalerite in quartz-carbonate-baryte, ~50 supergene species with As in arsenates; the ~160-species Caldbeck district list names its As novelties (claudetite) but contains NO realgar or orpiment anywhere in the district. The mechanism is transparent: the step-0 broth carries free As=12 in a high-sulfide (S=200) reducing (Eh -149 mV) fluid, and the realgar/orpiment gates in js/91-nucleation-sulfide.ts fire on As+S supersaturation with no deposit-type appropriateness gate, so the surplus As the Cu-sulfosalts don't consume dumps into realgar (6x, tied-2nd abundance) + orpiment + proustite + arsenopyrite. A grown specimen would show abundant orange realgar/golden orpiment/ruby proustite — a spectacularly wrong look versus the azure-linarite + green-pyromorphite + campylite that defines Roughton Gill; a geologist reads that as Allchar/Getchell, not Caldbeck. This is both a wrong-species and a confabulated-abundance error. It also passes the doctrine test: the fix is a clean growth-condition lever (lower initial fluid.As from 12 so the Cu-As sulfosalts fully sequester it, retaining the step-70 pyrite-oxidation As pulse to feed supergene mimetite), not a scoreboard/expects_species patch.

confirmed.claim: chalcocite nucleates at step 0 (Eh −150 mV, pre-oxidation) as a primary phase, while chalcopyrite — expected — never nucleates at all (no-show)
confirmed.objection: This inverts the paragenesis: at Roughton Gill chalcopyrite IS the primary hypogene copper mineral, and chalcocite (+ covellite) are its SUPERGENE enrichment products. Growing chalcocite at step 0 under reducing conditions, before the oxidation pulse, with zero chalcopyrite present, produces the daughter without the parent — an order real paragenesis forbids. It also leaves the model with no primary Cu source for the whole downstream supergene Cu-sulfate cascade (linarite/caledonite/brochantite) to have oxidized FROM.
confirmed.evidence: Russell Society review: primary mineralisation = galena + chalcopyrite + sphalerite. Standard supergene theory (e.g. USGS SIR 2010-5070 Ch.12): chalcocite/covellite form in the enrichment blanket by replacement of chalcopyrite below the water table as Eh rises — they are not step-0 hypogene phases in a low-T Pb-Cu vein.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Seed the reducing step-0 primary stage with chalcopyrite (Cu+Fe+S co-precipitation at Eh −150 mV), and gate chalcocite/covellite nucleation behind BOTH the Eh rise at the oxidation pulse (step ~69) AND the presence of a pre-existing Cu-sulfide substrate, so they grow as replacement products of chalcopyrite rather than firing at t=0.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest defense I could mount, and where it holds vs. fails:

(1) "The cascade is orphaned / no primary Cu source" — this limb of the objection is FALSE and I can refute it cleanly. Tetrahedrite (Cu12Sb4S13) and tennantite (Cu12As4S13) both nucleate at step 0 as primary Cu-sulfosalts — both documented Roughton Gill primaries — and dissolved Cu=40 sits in the broth feeding linarite/caledonite/brochantite directly. The downstream Cu-sulfate cascade has legitimate Cu parents; it is not left "producing the daughter without a parent" in the starvation sense the reviewer implies. The chalcocite itself is even an extra Cu reservoir to oxidize from.

(2) "Chalcocite is fabricated / alien to the locality" — partially defensible: chalcocite is a real member of the Caldbeck supergene Cu suite and the engine's own gate note labels it "supergene Cu-enrichment-blanket signature." The scoreboard already flags it as a *surprise* (not in expects_species) and chalcopyrite as a *no-show* — nothing is hidden.

But the CORE objection survives. The code shows chalcocite nucleates at step 0 at 130 °C, Eh −150 mV, pre-oxidation (O2=0.05), i.e. in the explicitly hypogene stage (galena/pyrite/tetrahedrite/tennantite all co-nucleate; the step-25 event is named "Primary Stage Closes"). That is primary Cu2S. Meanwhile chalcopyrite — the documented primary Cu mineral at Roughton Gill per the very Russell Society review the card cites, and an expects_species entry — never nucleates: its supersaturation is porphyry-calibrated (T_factor 0.2 below 180 °C, T_optimal 400, sigma_crit 1.0), so at this genuinely low-T (110–130 °C) vein it lands at σ≈0.125 and no-shows. So the sim substitutes primary chalcocite for the expected primary chalcopyrite. That inverts the documented paragenesis: chalcocite is a supergene enrichment product of chalcopyrite, not a step-0 reducing-stage phase. Web check confirms the literature: primary = galena+chalcopyrite+sphalerite; chalcocite/covellite are supergene. The internal inconsistency clinches it — covellite's redox *tent* correctly keeps it out of the strongly-reducing primary stage (fires step 69 ✓), while chalcocite's linear redox factor has no reducing-side penalty and fires at −150 mV. This is not a real phenomenon the reviewer missed; it is a gate-calibration gap.

Drift-doctrine read: this is exactly a growth-condition lever, so it is an actionable finding, not a scoreboard-edit temptation. Fix = give chalcocite a reducing-side redox penalty (copy covellite's tent) so it only fires in the mildly-reducing supergene window, and/or lower chalcopyrite's porphyry T-floor so low-T vein chalcopyrite can nucleate. The objection stands.

confirmed.claim: mimetite (expected) never nucleates — diagnostic no-show; and proustite Ag3AsS3 grows as a step-0 primary
confirmed.objection: Mimetite is a WIDESPREAD and ABUNDANT diagnostic species at Roughton Gill (the iconic red-barrel 'campylite'), so its complete absence is a diagnostic no-show. Separately, proustite (ruby silver) as a step-0 primary is a confabulation: the documented Ag at Roughton Gill is native 'lattice' silver liberated from galena during oxidation (± acanthite), not a primary Ag-As sulfosalt — so the sim over-produces exotic As-Ag sulfosalts at the primary stage (the same syndrome as the realgar finding).
confirmed.evidence: Russell Society review: 'pyromorphite, mimetite, cerussite ... widespread and abundant'; native silver is listed among first-reported species, proustite is not. The author's own notes already flag mimetite as a likely seed-42 miss (needs As(V)>3 + Cl>2) and identify the native-silver Ag reservoir (838 ppm Ag-in-galena) — proustite is not part of that story.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: For mimetite: nudge the pyrite-oxidation As(V) pulse and Cl so the mimetite gate (As(V)>3, Cl>2) clears at seed 42 — a vugg-tune-scenario broth tweak the author already scoped. For proustite: route the Ag reservoir to native silver on the Eh rise (as intended) instead of to a primary Ag-As sulfosalt by gating proustite nucleation out of the reducing step-0 window.

refutation.reasoning:
Best defense I could mount, and whether it survives:

PRONG 1 (mimetite no-show). Defense: the sim's chemistry is actually RIGHT — the broth carries As=12 and Cl=25, comfortably above the mimetite gate (As(V)>3 + Cl>2), so this is a purely stochastic seed-42 nucleation miss, not a claim that mimetite doesn't belong. The sim "knows" mimetite is a Roughton Gill species; it just didn't roll it at this seed. Under the drift doctrine the fix is a clean growth-condition lever (nudge As(V)/Cl or the nucleation gate), never a scoreboard edit. VERDICT: does not survive as a refutation. Mimetite/campylite is the single most iconic supergene species at Roughton Gill ("widespread and abundant", the red-barrel campylite), so an abundant diagnostic species being entirely absent is a genuine no-show the reviewer correctly flags — the author admits it in the card. It is tuning-grade, but it is real.

PRONG 2 (proustite as step-0 primary). Defense: the deposit is genuinely As-rich AND Ag-rich — primary tennantite (Cu-As sulfosalt) is in expects_species, "Silver Gill" is a named part of the complex with a documented medieval silver-lead working, and 838 ppm Ag sits in the galena. Ruby silver co-precipitating with tetrahedrite-tennantite from a reducing As-Ag primary brine is not thermodynamically absurd, and co-nucleation at step 0 is not a claim of strict paragenetic sequence. So the objection's "pure confabulation" rhetoric is slightly overstated FOR proustite specifically (it is the most defensible of the As surprises; realgar/orpiment are worse). VERDICT: does not survive. The documented Ag expression at Roughton Gill is SUPERGENE native silver (± acanthite) — and native_silver is itself a no-show in this very run (line 58). So the sim got the Ag mineralogy wrong in both directions at once: it invented an undocumented primary Ag-As sulfosalt (proustite) while failing to produce the actually-diagnostic native silver. Proustite is not among the locality's reported species, and it arrives inside a clear As-overproduction cluster (realgar 6 events, orpiment, arsenopyrite all at/near step 0) — the same "realgar syndrome" the objection names, and realgar/orpiment as step-0 primaries are hard to justify at a 110-130°C mesothermal Pb-Cu vein. The proustite is an unintended byproduct of an over-hot As sub-model, not causal control gained from better science.

Both prongs of the objection hold; my strongest defense softens the framing but does not overturn either. The sim contradicts the documented Roughton Gill paragenesis.

confirmed.claim: aragonite grows as carbonate gangue at step 141 with T ~159°C (in expects_species, SI_aragonite 0.63)
confirmed.objection: Aragonite precipitation is favored only below ~90°C; at 150-160°C calcite is the stable and precipitating CaCO3 polymorph and any aragonite inverts to calcite on vein timescales. A sealed hydrothermal vein closing at ~150°C should grow calcite (± dolomite/ankerite) — aragonite is not the five-element gangue carbonate and would not survive as a nucleating primary phase at this temperature. Growing calcite AND aragonite simultaneously at 159°C asserts a polymorph that the thermal window forbids.
confirmed.evidence: CaCO3 polymorph study (Springer, Discover Civil Engineering 2025): aragonite appears 50-85°C, above ~90°C it transforms to calcite. Experimental diagenesis (Copernicus Biogeosciences 14:1461, 2017): hydrothermal aragonite→calcite replacement proceeds at ≥162-175°C. Wittichen gangue is carbonate + quartz ± baryte ± fluorite (Scharrer 2019), the carbonate being calcite/dolomite.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Add a T-ceiling to aragonite's nucleation gate (~90-100°C) so this hot carbonate stage grows calcite-only — the exact mirror of the erythrite demotion already accepted in the notes (erythrite gated OUT above T≤50°C; aragonite should be gated out above ~90°C), keeping the fix a growth-window threshold, not an expects_species edit.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest defense I could build: (1) The reviewer's stated MECHANISM is partly inverted — aragonite PRECIPITATION is kinetically FAVORED at higher T (Burton & Walter 1987, a primary experimental paper the sim actually cites; warm water precipitates aragonite), not suppressed above 90°C; the ~90°C figure is about metastable persistence/inversion, not nucleation. (2) 159°C sits BELOW the reviewer's own cited aragonite→calcite replacement threshold (≥162-175°C), so it is metastably viable on lab timescales. (3) Co-nucleation with calcite at one late step is not a strict-sequence violation — disequilibrium carbonate fluids commonly drop both polymorphs, the metastable one as a minor phase. (4) Empirically decisive-looking: Wittichen genuinely HOSTS aragonite — cobalt-bearing aragonite is a mindat-documented species there — and a Co²⁺-rich five-element brine (skutterudite/safflorite/nickeline in this very scenario) plausibly routes CaCO3 to aragonite because Co²⁺, like Mg²⁺, poisons calcite steps and substitutes into the orthorhombic lattice. So "aragonite is not a Wittichen mineral / thermally forbidden" overreaches on two counts.

Why the defense does not survive to "refuted": the documented Wittichen aragonite is a LOW-T SECONDARY (supergene cobalt-bloom weathering) phase, not a ~159°C primary vein gangue co-precipitating with calcite — the sim grows the right species at the wrong temperature and wrong paragenetic stage. More damning, the project's OWN record convicts this output: v192 explicitly "correctly retired" hot aragonite at wittichen as a speciation-flattening artifact that "never belonged at 150–700°C," and its reappearance at SI 0.63/159°C is a known mixed-fidelity artifact — the [0,90]°C Ksp clamp freezing the retrograde curvature so a positive SI leaks into the 159°C step — flagged in BACKLOG pending the Ksp-analytic upgrade (v194 notes). The firing is driven by that frozen-Ksp omega × the T-favorability sigmoid, not by Co, so it has no honest causal control (fails the boss's drift doctrine). The reviewer's conclusion therefore stands even though the path to it is imperfect: a nucleating primary aragonite at ~159°C is not what this sealed vein produces.

Severity, however, should drop from medium to low: aragonite is a single-event trace phase at the run's tail; calcite — the correct dominant gangue — also nucleates at the same step and correctly out-saturates it (SI_calcite 0.756 > SI_aragonite 0.63); the species is real to the locality (not fabricated, just mis-timed/mis-heated); and it is an already-documented, low-footprint artifact with a known fix path (the honest lever is a growth-condition fix — cool the late meteoric-carbonate sealing beat below ~90°C, which would legitimize both the aragonite and the "meteoric seal" narrative — or land the Ksp-analytic upgrade — never a scoreboard edit). The reviewer's "medium" partly rests on the two overstatements the Co-aragonite occurrence and precipitation kinetics refute.

confirmed.claim: proustite (Ag3AsS3) nucleates at step 133 and acanthite (Ag2S) at step 140, in the oxidizing meteoric tail (Eh +68 to +72 mV)
confirmed.objection: Reduced Ag-As-S sulfosalts need reduced sulfide, not stable at +70 mV (pH 6.6, 160°C) — that Eh sits squarely in the sulfate field, which is exactly why barite co-precipitates in the same beat. In real five-element veins the ruby-silver sulfosalts form in the REDUCED native-metal stage from residual reduced S, BEFORE the oxidizing meteoric overprint. Bundling primary proustite into the same oxidizing meteoric event as sulfate barite is a redox co-occurrence tension. (Acanthite as a supergene TARNISH on native silver under near-surface oxidation is defensible and is explicitly NOT challenged — only primary proustite forming at +70 mV.)
confirmed.evidence: Scharrer, Kreissl & Markl 2019 (Ore Geol. Rev.): sulfarsenides/sulfosalts form when small S enters the reduced arsenide stage; base-metal sulfosalts are a reduced-stage product, not a meteoric-oxidation product. Staude/Markl 2012: Wittichen Ag mineralization sits in reduced Ni-As-Bi / Co-As-Bi sub-stages, not the oxidizing seal.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Shift proustite's growth window to sit on/just after the reducing Eh pulse (steps ~90-115), decoupled from the late meteoric sulfate/carbonate events, so the ruby silver forms under reduced conditions and only the tarnish (acanthite) + barite ride the +70 mV oxidizing tail.

refutation.reasoning: Strongest defense I could mount: (a) proustite is a genuine Wittichen/Schwarzwald five-element-vein species — not fabricated; (b) the sim does NOT let it form oxidizing-blind — the proustite gate consumes residual reduced sulfide (fluid.S) and calls a redox gate, matching the reviewer's own "residual reduced S" mechanism; (c) barite + sulfosalt co-occurrence is a real fluid-mixing signature in many deposits; (d) co-nucleation at one step is not a strict claim of redox identity. That defense collapses on the numbers. The proustite gate (js/41:1191) passes when Eh <= ehFromO2(1.5) = +290 mV — a loose O2-anchored bridge. But the sim's OWN sulfur redox couple (REDOX_COUPLES.S, js/20c:85) puts the sulfate=sulfide crossover at pH 6.6 at -190 mV; at the recorded +67.7 mV the reduced-HS- fraction is ~1e-24, i.e. there is no reduced sulfide to build Ag3AsS3. Proustite fires only because the gate threshold is ~260 mV too oxidizing relative to the sim's own chemistry — and js/20c:580-581 carries a TODO conceding the gate isn't yet bound to a sulfide-consistent Eh threshold. The recorded v226 strip is decisive: proustite nucleates at step 133 with Eh +67.7 mV while SI_barite is already 1.57 (needs oxidized SO4) at that same step; the true reduced native-metal stage (CH4 pulse, Eh -277 mV, step ~90) sits 40 steps earlier and holds no proustite. So the sim has compressed the reduced sulfidation stage into the oxidizing meteoric barite beat, contradicting both Scharrer/Kreissl/Markl 2019 + Staude/Markl 2012 and its own Nernst chemistry. The final hand-specimen assemblage (ruby silver + tarnished Ag + barite + carbonate) is accurate-to-life, which keeps this from being high severity, but the challenged claim — primary proustite forming at +70 mV in the same event as sulfate barite — is a real redox co-occurrence tension. It is also fixable with a growth-condition lever (tighten the sulfosalt redox gate toward the -190 mV sulfide line, or move the residual-S sulfidation beat into the reduced pulse window ~step 90), so it is actionable drift, not a scoreboard edit. Acanthite-as-supergene-tarnish is not challenged and is fine.

confirmed.claim: spodumene (step 0) and morganite (step 0) grow as the 'pegmatite country' primary assemblage, driven by broth Li=15 + Be=10 (comment: 'narrator reads these as Erzgebirge country — beryl, spodumene').
confirmed.objection: Spodumene and morganite (gem Cs-Mn beryl) are diagnostic LCT rare-element PEGMATITE index minerals (Pala, Minas Gerais, Zimbabwe). The Erzgebirge granites are S-type, tin-tungsten (Sn-W) greisen-specialized; their lithium lives in ZINNWALDITE/lepidolite MICA in greisen, not in spodumene, and beryl is minor aquamarine, not gem morganite. Schneeberg itself is a Fünfelement HYDROTHERMAL VEIN, not an LCT pegmatite. Neither phase is reported here — they misrepresent the whole deposit class as a lithium pegmatite.
confirmed.evidence: Web-verified: Erzgebirge/Krušné Hory Li is mica-hosted zinnwaldite (Econ. Geol. 120, 2025; Cínovec Li-Sn-W, J. Geochem. Explor.); 'spodumene does not appear to be a significant mineral' in these systems — host is quartz+albite+zinnwaldite+topaz+cassiterite. Schneeberg's granite/greisen signature is Sn-W (correctly captured by the scenario's cassiterite+topaz), NOT LCT.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Cut the melt-trace Li (15→~1) and Be (10→~1) in the INITIAL broth so the spodumene/morganite nucleation gates never open; if a lithian/beryllian flavour is wanted, route it to a zinnwaldite (Li-mica) or aquamarine channel. The Sn=60 / topaz / cassiterite greisen signature is the correct one and stays. This is a broth-composition lever, not a scoreboard edit.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest defense I could mount, and where each strand fails:

(1) "The broth ions are defensible." This one HOLDS: the Erzgebirge Li-F granites (Zinnwald/Cínovec, Altenberg, Ehrenfriedersdorf) genuinely are Li- and Be-enriched, so Li=15 + Be=10 in the broth is fine. But this does not rescue the species — it isolates the error to the mineral SINK the sim chose, not the chemistry.

(2) "The district really does host these phases." FAILS. Erzgebirge lithium is mica-hosted zinnwaldite/lepidolite in greisen (Cínovec Li-Sn-W; the Zinnwald project's whole economic premise is ADAPTING the spodumene extraction process to zinnwaldite precisely BECAUSE there is no spodumene to mine). "Spodumene does not appear to be a significant mineral" in these systems is confirmed. Beryl here is aquamarine (Fe), not morganite — morganite is Cs-Mn pink beryl that requires extreme LCT fractionation (Pala, Minas Gerais, Afghanistan, Madagascar), a regime absent at Schneeberg. Web search returned zero Saxon morganite.

(3) "Co-nucleation at step 0 isn't a strict-sequence claim." Irrelevant — the objection is about the species existing at all as the primary assemblage, not their order.

(4) "Catalog lacks zinnwaldite, so spodumene/morganite are proxies." This is the real explanation, but under the drift doctrine it is exactly a growth-condition/catalog lever, not an exoneration: the correct fix is to add a greisen-mica (zinnwaldite) Li sink and route Li→mica, and render Be as aquamarine (or drop it) — a science-INCREASING move. Firing two diagnostically-LCT species instead is a genuine false-positive.

Notably the scenario's own notes ("pegmatite country — beryl, spodumene"; "uranium-pegmatite vein system") bake the LCT/pegmatite conflation into the framing, whereas Schneeberg is a Fünfelement (Ag-Bi-Co-Ni-U-As) hydrothermal vein in Sn-W greisen country. The objection's mineralogy is correct; it survives.

Where the objection OVERREACHES: "misrepresent the whole deposit class as a lithium pegmatite." The scenario actually models the deposit correctly overall — cassiterite+topaz capture the Sn-W greisen, the native_bismuth/native_arsenic/cobaltite/nickeline/erythrite/annabergite/proustite set captures the five-element vein, and the uranyl cation-fork (torbernite→zeunerite→autunite→uranospinite) — the scenario's actual scientific payload — is intact and unaffected. Spodumene and morganite are two single-event backdrop primaries (each 1 event), not even in expects_species. So it is a real, glaring, confirmable garnish error that a mineralogist would flag, but it does not reclassify or break the scenario. That overstated blast radius, plus the defensible broth chemistry and the clean localized fix, is why I correct severity from high down to medium.

confirmed.claim: No-show: siegenite / chalcopyrite / fletcherite (Cu-Ni-Co suite) — the assemblage grows galena+sphalerite+marcasite+pyrite but zero Cu-Ni-Co phases at a Viburnum-Trend anchor.
confirmed.objection: The Magmont-Sweetwater segment of the Viburnum Trend is quite literally characterized as 'Cu-Ni-Co-rich Pb-Zn MVT': cobalt is carried by siegenite (dominant), with fletcherite/carrollite/polydymite and chalcopyrite. Siegenite ± chalcopyrite are the accessory minerals that distinguish the Viburnum Trend from every other MVT district. A crystal biography that claims to BE Sweetwater and omits the one thing that makes Sweetwater special is not modelling Sweetwater — it is modelling a generic Pb-Zn MVT.
confirmed.evidence: SSRN: 'Trace element characteristics of sulfides from the Cu-Ni-Co-rich Pb-Zn MVT mineralization of the Magmont Mine of the Viburnum Trend'; MO DNR PUB2893 (Co occurs 'primarily as siegenite, with fletcherite... and rare carrollite'). Published paragenesis: chalcopyrite (falling fO2) → siegenite → then sphalerite+galena (rising pH). Repo check: reactive_wall broth in data/scenarios.json5 has NO Cu/Ni/Co (only Fe,Mn,Zn,Pb,Ba,S,F,Mg,Na,K,Cl,Ag,Sr); minerals.json has millerite but NO siegenite/thiospinel engine — so the suite cannot nucleate.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Add Cu≈15 / Ni≈5 / Co≈3 ppm to the reactive_wall broth and author chalcopyrite + siegenite (thiospinel) engines with nucleation gates sequenced chalcopyrite→siegenite AHEAD of the sphalerite/galena gates (falling-fO2 then rising-pH ladder). Broth chemistry + a new engine — never 'accept the no-show'.

refutation.reasoning: Strongest defense I could build: (1) Locality specificity — the reviewer's headline SSRN evidence is about the MAGMONT mine, and Cu-Ni-Co abundance in the Viburnum Trend really does peak in the central Trend (Magmont/Buick); the Ni/Co ratio decreases from Fletcher southward TO Sweetwater. So one might argue the sim anchors on the Cu-Ni-Co-poorer south end and is right to omit the suite. (2) Volumetric triviality — Co/Ni are "present in small amounts," siegenite crystals only to ~0.2 cm, so a biography of the dominant vug assemblage could skip a trace accessory. (3) Engine scope — there is genuinely no thiospinel engine, so this is a build-new, not a tune.\n\nEvery branch fails. vugg is a CRYSTAL BIOGRAPHY — it depicts the collectible crystals that grow in a vug, and by that exact measure Sweetwater is the PREMIER siegenite locality on Earth: web sources state siegenite is "most common… especially in the Sweetwater Mine" and that most siegenite specimens for sale were collected there, occurring as sharp sparkling octahedral crystals to 0.2 cm "with minor marcasite and dolomite in association." Marcasite and dolomite are both phases the sim already grows in reactive_wall, so there is no environmental barrier — siegenite belongs in the very same vug on the very same associates. The volumetric-triviality defense also fails internally: the sim already grows trace phases (acanthite Ag2S, celestine, rhodochrosite), and it deliberately added Ag and Sr to the broth for the stated purpose of distinguishing Viburnum from Tri-State — yet omits siegenite, the single most iconic Viburnum-vs-everywhere accessory, best-crystallized at the anchor mine itself. That is a self-contradiction, not a scope choice. The scope-creep defense inverts under the boss doctrine: the reactive_wall broth carries zero Cu/Ni/Co (default 0), and minerals.json has no siegenite/thiospinel engine, so the suite physically cannot nucleate — but the fix is a textbook growth-condition lever (add Cu/Ni/Co to the broth + author a thiospinel engine), and the Cu/Ni/Co FluidChemistry fields plus sibling Cu-Ni-Co engines (chalcopyrite, bornite, nickeline, cobaltite) already exist. The objection stands: the sim brands itself Sweetwater/Viburnum and omits the one thing that makes Sweetwater special.

confirmed.claim: Selenite (gypsum, CaSO4·2H2O) nucleates twice at ~79-95 °C (first step 39).
confirmed.objection: Gypsum is thermodynamically the WRONG calcium sulfate at these temperatures. The gypsum→anhydrite transition sits at ~42 °C in a NaCl brine (40-60 °C range); above ~80 °C anhydrite is the stable phase and gypsum does not nucleate as a primary precipitate. A 79-140 °C basinal-brine vug that grows selenite is telling a low-temperature evaporite/supergene story in a mesothermal setting. (Compounding: the recorded SI_selenite is negative for the entire run [-0.945,-0.63], as is SI_anhydrite — so no Ca-sulfate is even saturated in the bulk fluid, yet selenite grows.)
confirmed.evidence: Frontiers Nucl. Eng. 2023 re-assessment + Springer Mater.&Struct. 2024: CaSO4-H2O transition 40-60 °C, ~42 °C ±1; in the CaSO4-NaCl-H2O ternary the transition is 42 °C and 'below 80 °C gypsum is the sole primary phase... anhydrite appeared >80 °C'. Strip T-series (archive/strips/v226): selenite events fall at steps ~39-95 where T = 79-95 °C, i.e. above the transition. Higher salinity (an MVT bittern brine) only pushes gypsum stability LOWER.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Put a T-ceiling on selenite's nucleation gate (~50 °C, salinity-shifted per the 42 °C brine transition) so gypsum can only nucleate in genuinely low-T evaporite/supergene scenarios; at 80-140 °C route the Ca+SO4 pool to anhydrite (already tracked as SI_anhydrite). A T-gate threshold, not a scoreboard edit.

refutation.reasoning: Strongest defense I could build, and why each strand snaps: (a) "It's a late low-T/supergene overprint" — the two selenite events fire at 70.9 °C and 79.7 °C (strip v226), driven by acid-pulse S spikes in js/70c-reactive-wall.ts; no cooling or supergene event is injected, so this is squarely the 70–80 °C hydrothermal ore stage, not near-surface weathering. Dead. (b) "Metastable gypsum can nucleate above the anhydrite boundary" — the only genuinely hot-gypsum literature (Naica ~58 °C, Van Driessche 2016, which the engine comment itself cites) tops out near 60 °C, well below 79 °C; and metastable nucleation still requires supersaturation w.r.t. gypsum. Here SI_selenite is NEGATIVE at both events (−0.63, −0.69): the sim's own Ksp engine says the fluid is undersaturated in gypsum. You cannot precipitate gypsum from a gypsum-undersaturated fluid at ANY temperature, so this defense is dispositively killed by the sim's own thermodynamics. (c) "Salinity stabilizes gypsum to higher T" — backwards: higher salinity LOWERS the gypsum→anhydrite transition; and the recorded 18 psu is near-seawater-dilute (itself implausibly low for an MVT basinal brine), which puts the boundary at the ~55–60 °C pure-water value — 79 °C still exceeds it. (d) "Co-nucleation ≠ sequence" — irrelevant; the objection is about phase-vs-temperature, not order. Root cause confirmed in code: the nucleation gate (js/85b → supersaturation_selenite in js/40-supersat-sulfate.ts) uses a kinetic sigma = (Ca/60)(S/50)·redox that reaches ~13 when the acid-into-carbonate mechanic pumps Ca≈300/S≈130 ppm; the soft exp(-0.06·(T−60)) decay only knocks it to ~3–4, still clearing sigma_crit=1.0. The gate never reads SI_selenite — the SI chips are diagnostic-only (js/40b) and were never wired to the gate. So the sim grows the WRONG Ca-sulfate polymorph for the temperature (anhydrite is stable >55–60 °C) AND does so from a thermodynamically undersaturated fluid. The objection stands on both counts. This is a real loss of causal control (an SI-blind kinetic gate that fires for any hot Ca/S-rich brine), fixable via a proper growth-condition lever — steepen/hard-gate the >60 °C selenite decay to encode the phase boundary and/or require SI_selenite>0 (or >SI_anhydrite) — not a scoreboard edit. Objection survives.

confirmed.claim: Acanthite (Ag2S) is the second mineral to form (step 7), precedes sphalerite (25) and galena (39), and fires 4 events — more than galena (1) or sphalerite (1).
confirmed.objection: In MVT ores silver does not crystallize as a free early silver-sulfide crystal — it rides in galena as solid solution (matildite) or as acanthite MICROINCLUSIONS within galena, i.e. paragenetically with or after the galena host, and typically late/trace. Having Ag2S be the first sulfide, before any base-metal sulfide exists to enclose it, and more abundant than the galena it is supposed to be included in, inverts the paragenesis and confabulates abundance. The sim's own acanthite description places it in 'low-sulfidation epithermal veins... and supergene enrichment zones' — not the primary MVT stage.
confirmed.evidence: Galena/silver literature (mineralexpert.org; Springer Geol. Ore Deposits 2025): 'silver presence in galena is caused by solid solution of matildite... and, less often, by acanthite (Ag2S) microinclusions in it' — an IN-galena habit, not a pre-galena crystal. Card paragenesis table: acanthite first_step 7, sphalerite 25, galena 39; acanthite 4 events vs galena 1. minerals.json acanthite: T_range 50-170, epithermal/supergene habit, max_nucleation_count 4. The author's note legitimately defends Ag PRESENCE (Leach 2010 byproduct-Ag credit) — that is respected — but does not defend an early discrete Ag2S phase.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Add a paragenetic gate so acanthite cannot nucleate until a galena framework exists (Ag2S as inclusion/late phase), or drop broth Ag below its self-nucleation floor so it stays in galena solid-solution. A nucleation-gate threshold + broth-Ag lever, not deleting acanthite.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest defense assembled and tested: (1) Thermodynamic-order defense — Ag2S has the lowest Ksp of any sulfide (~10^-50 vs PbS ~10^-28), so "silver sulfide reaches saturation before the base-metal sulfides" is real, and the sim's supersaturation math faithfully reproduces it: in the reactive_wall broth acanthite sigma ≈ 3.4 (ag_f 1.2 x s_f 2.4 x T_factor 1.2) vs galena ≈ 0.45, ~7x higher, so acanthite fires first and hits its nucleation cap. (2) The Ag=3 budget is well-calibrated — it matches the measured 3.1 ppm Ag in Viburnum platy galena (MDPI 2018) and honors the Leach 2010 byproduct-Ag credit. Both of these survive.

But they defend only saturation ORDER, not the discrete-phase nucleation, the early paragenetic position, or the abundance. Every empirical source contradicts the sim: Missouri DNR PUB2896 states Viburnum silver "is contained in the crystal lattice of sphalerite (ZnS)" (argentiferous galena belongs to the separate Silver Mines area of Madison Co.), and the MDPI platy-galena study puts Ag at 3.1 ppm in solid solution. Sweetwater's mindat species list shows no discrete acanthite/argentite/native silver. So nature hosts the 3 ppm Ag as lattice-bound trace in the base-metal sulfides — it never nucleates a free Ag2S crystal here. The sim has no solid-solution sink, so it dumps trace Ag into a discrete phase and, because acanthite's sigma formula gets most of its magnitude from S (S/25) with the Ag factor already saturated at 3 ppm, that phase becomes the SECOND mineral overall and the single most prolific sulfide (4 crystals vs galena 1, sphalerite 1). Galena's max_nucleation_count is also 4, so the 4-vs-1 is genuinely sigma-driven, not a cap artifact — a true abundance inversion of a trace element over the ore.

Rescue attempts fail: the acid-pulse/supergene angle doesn't apply because acanthite fires at step 7, before the first acid pulse (step 15), in a reducing Eh-25 mV system — it is a primary early phase, not a late supergene overprint (a supergene overprint would put it LATE, which cuts the other way). Co-nucleation doesn't apply either: 7 -> 25 -> 39 is an 18-32 step lead, an explicit first-among-sulfides ordering. The objection stands: the sim inverts the paragenesis (discrete Ag2S before any base-metal sulfide exists to host it) and confabulates abundance. The fix is a clean growth-condition lever consistent with the drift doctrine — raise the discrete-acanthite Ag threshold or gate discrete Ag2S behind an Ag-enrichment / supergene condition (epithermal Ag-vein levels), so trace MVT-level Ag never clears sigma for a free phase — not deleting the species and not touching the correctly-calibrated Ag=3 broth.

Severity: corrected to medium rather than high. The scenario's backbone assemblage (sphalerite, galena, marcasite, pyrite, barite, dolomite) is all present and correctly ordered relative to each other, all four expects_species are satisfied, and the broth chemistry (Ag=3) is accurate; the defect is confined to one trace surprise-species' nucleation gate rendering it as a discrete over-abundant early crystal instead of lattice-bound trace. A case for high is defensible given how viscerally wrong "more silver sulfide than galena in a Pb-Zn mine" reads and the v195 Tri-State de-confabulation precedent, but the blast radius is one species' gate, not the scenario's core paragenesis.

confirmed.claim: Sulfate/Sr gangue dominates the ore: barite 11 events + celestine 6 vs galena 1 and sphalerite 1; justified by a 'high-Ba endmember' claim cited to Stoffell et al. 2008.
confirmed.objection: This paints Sweetwater — the largest lead district in the United States — as a barite-celestine vug carrying trace galena, which inverts the deposit's actual character (galena-dominant ore, dolomite/calcite the principal gangue, barite subordinate/late). The cited authority does not support the premise: Stoffell et al. 2008 analysed the TRI-STATE and Northern Arkansas districts, not a Tri-State-versus-Viburnum barium comparison, so 'Viburnum = high-Ba endmember' is unsupported by that reference. Celestine also grows 6× while its recorded SI stays negative throughout [-0.44,-0.19].
confirmed.evidence: Stoffell, Appold, Wilkinson, McClean & Jeffries 2008, Econ. Geol. 103(7):1411-1435 — title 'Mineralizing Brines from the Tri-State and Northern Arkansas Districts' (Viburnum is a SEPARATE fluid-inclusion literature, e.g. the 'Composition of Ore Fluid Inclusions from the Viburnum Trend' paper). Card event counts: barite 11, celestine 6, acanthite 4 vs galena 1, sphalerite 1. The O2=0.25 gap-fill unlocked the Ba=25 pool and SI_barite stays strongly oversaturated (1.58-2.46) the whole run, driving repeat nucleation.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Lit-check whether Viburnum is truly high-Ba; if not, lower broth Ba from 25 toward a dolomite-dominant gangue. Independently, raise Zn/Pb/S-driven sulfide nucleation frequency (or damp the barite gate the O2 gap-fill over-opened) so galena/sphalerite out-number the sulfate gangue. Broth-composition + gate-frequency lever.

refutation.reasoning:
I mounted three defenses and none fully survives.

DEFENSE 1 (event count ≠ mass; late-vug framing): The strongest refutation is that "events" count nucleation/growth-bursts, not volume. A galena-dominant deposit can still produce a specimen where one big galena cube (1 event) is over-banded by many thin barite blades (11 events) — exactly the reactive-wall banding mechanic. And Sweetwater specimens genuinely carry barite, fluorite, and calcite drusy over galena, so a late sealed carbonate vug CAN be gangue-dominated. This is real and it softens the "sulfate gangue dominates the ORE" phrasing — it is a proportion error, not a phantom-mineral error (every phase produced is a plausible Viburnum associate). BUT it does not rescue the scenario: the scenario's OWN description says "Sphalerite-galena-marcasite ± barite" (barite subordinate), and the output inverts that; galena is absent from expects_species for the largest lead district in the US and nucleates only once alongside sphalerite once, while celestine (6) and acanthite (4) — neither a notable Sweetwater phase — outrank both ore sulfides. Character is genuinely skewed.

DEFENSE 2 (the citation): One could lean on the other real sources (Hanor 1994 Sr/Ba tracers; Anderson & Macqueen 1982) for the general "basinal brines carry Ba/Sr" premise. But the specific written claim — "Viburnum is the high-Ba MVT endmember per Stoffell et al. 2008" — is confabulated: web-verified, that paper's data is Tri-State + Northern Arkansas, not a Viburnum-vs-Tri-State Ba comparison. Worse, the premise is itself dubious (Tri-State is the barite/jasperoid-rich district; Viburnum is famed for galena, not barium), so the citation is both misattributed and pointing the wrong way. Under vugg's own citation discipline (v139 adamite fabricated-citation lesson, twin-law-check tooling), this is a first-class defect. Defense fails.

DEFENSE 3 (celestine while SI<0): The best physics rescue is the barite–celestine (BaSO4–SrSO4) solid solution: Sr can coprecipitate as a barytocelestine intermediate even when pure-endmember celestine SI is mildly negative, because mixing lowers the effective solubility (Lippmann thermodynamics), and the nuc note literally sites it "on barite (celestobarite–barytocelestine pair)." This makes the growth not thermodynamically impossible. BUT the code does NOT implement solid-solution thermo — the gate is the legacy heuristic supersaturation_celestine() clearing on raw Sr/S availability, which is blind to the negative Ksp-based SI chip the sim itself reports. So the phase grows for a reason the sim's own better instrument contradicts, and it is labeled discrete "celestine" rather than minor Sr-in-barite. Per the drift doctrine ("if we understand why the model moved we should be able to move it again by changing growth conditions"), the causal lever (SI) and the actual gate diverge — decreased causal control, a real internal inconsistency, not better science.

Net: all three prongs of the objection stand. The fix is a clean growth-condition lever (dial O2 back toward the sulfide-dominant Eh window / trim the Ba–Sr pool or add H2S buffering so sulfides lead and barite/celestine fall subordinate; correct the citation to a paper that actually studied Viburnum), never a scoreboard edit — so the finding is doctrine-useful. Objection confirmed.

confirmed.claim: Mimetite (Pb5(AsO4)3Cl) nucleates at step 84 at T≈310°C within the hypogene porphyry assemblage.
confirmed.objection: Mimetite is a supergene oxidation-zone lead arsenate produced by weathering of galena in cool (<~100°C), oxidizing groundwater; it cannot crystallize at 310°C in a magmatic-hydrothermal brine, where arsenic stays reduced — indeed As is already fixed here as arsenopyrite (step 3) and tennantite (step 24). Oxidized arsenate coexisting with reduced arsenide/sulfosalt in the same 310°C pocket is a redox co-occurrence violation.
confirmed.evidence: The mineral's OWN minerals.json entry: 'Supergene product of galena oxidation in As-bearing groundwater,' scenarios:[supergene_oxidation]. Every classic mimetite locality (Tsumeb, Cumbria campylite) is a <100°C oxidation zone. The step-85 'oxidation/meteoric' event raises Eh (→323 mV) and O2 (→1.8) but leaves T at 310°C, so an oxidized phase fires at magmatic-hydrothermal temperature.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: The oxidation event injects O2 without collapsing T. Gate supergene arsenate nucleation on T<~100°C (or make the meteoric-infiltration event also drop temperature), deferring mimetite to the future supergene-blanket arc the author's own notes reserve — rather than leaking it into the 310°C hypogene stage.

refutation.reasoning:
STRONGEST DEFENSE (and it partly lands): the sim is NOT committing the crude redox blunder the reviewer describes. Mimetite is correctly gated behind arsenic oxidation via arsenateAvailablePpm/arsenateRedoxAvailable. I instrumented porphyry@seed42: at steps 80-83 mimetite sigma is EXACTLY 0.000, because S=122(>50) and O2=0.3 force arsenicOxidizedFraction→0 (thioarsenite As(III) lock). It only nucleates at step 84 (event step 85), the exact instant the oxidation event sets O2=1.8, Eh=44→322 mV and begins dissolving arsenopyrite #10/12/14/16 to AsO₄³⁻ — the textbook scorodite/mimetite supergene cascade. So the engine does NOT co-precipitate oxidized arsenate with reduced arsenide in one reduced 310°C brine: at the firing instant As is being actively oxidized, and the arsenopyrite is being CONSUMED to feed it. Relict primary tennantite (step 24, never dissolved) sitting beside secondary mimetite is a perfectly normal incomplete-oxidation texture in real oxidation zones. On those grounds the reviewer's literal "redox co-occurrence violation" framing is largely REFUTED — the mechanism they invoke ("arsenic stays reduced") is inverted at the actual firing step. The habit even resolves to "campylite barrel," the correct Cumbria supergene form.

WHERE THE DEFENSE FAILS — temperature, which the reviewer also explicitly flagged: the oxidation event drops T only 40°C, so the entire meteoric-oxidation overprint is modeled at 308°C. That is physically impossible. Meteoric/supergene oxidation is a post-uplift, near-surface, <~100°C process; you cannot have hot(308°C)+oxidizing+meteoric simultaneously in nature. Mimetite here nucleates 128°C above its OWN declared T_range_C ceiling (180) and ~190°C above its T_optimum ceiling (120). Its engine's only thermal brake is a soft exp damp above 150°C (factor ≈0.094 at 308°C), which the huge raw product (Pb55 · As(V)15 · Cl600) blows straight through to sigma 1.56. Damningly, unlike ALL FIVE sibling arsenates (austinite/legrandite/koettigite/duftite/bayldonite), whose MINERAL_GATES carry hard T_max=35-60°C, MINERAL_GATES_mimetite has NO T_min/T_max at all — a genuine missing-gate oversight, not a design choice. The fix is a clean growth-condition lever per the drift doctrine: drop the oxidation event's T to <100°C (model true post-uplift meteoric infiltration) and/or add the hard T_max that every sibling arsenate already has. The objection's CONCLUSION (mimetite at ~310°C is unphysical) survives; only its stated redox-coexistence RATIONALE is blunted.

confirmed.claim: Sylvanite (step 0) and calaverite (step 4) nucleate at T≈398°C, before the Cu ore (chalcopyrite step 24, bornite step 38).
confirmed.objection: Au-Ag tellurides are late, shallow, epithermal-cap phases (~200–300°C) that overprint a porphyry after it has cooled; at Bingham they sit in the distal/upper epithermal apron, not the deep 400°C potassic core. Growing them FIRST and HOTTEST — before the Cu-Fe sulfides — inverts the porphyry→epithermal-cap paragenetic and thermal sequence.
confirmed.evidence: The sim's own gates (js/41-supersat-sulfide.ts): calaverite T_optimal 275°C, sylvanite T_optimal 225°C — yet both fire at ~398°C, sylvanite right at its T_max 400. The author's broth comment itself: 'Te=2: epithermal-cap trace... Bingham's upper levels carry tellurides (calaverite, sylvanite)... porphyry-distal apron.'
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Te is delivered from step 0 with a permissive T_max, so tellurides fire in the hottest early window. Withhold Te until a late epithermal-cap event (deliver a Te pulse only once T<300°C) and/or tighten calaverite/sylvanite T_max toward ~325°C, so the tellurides crystallize late and cool as they actually do at Bingham.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest defense I could build, and why it fails:

DEFENSE A — "Bingham gold is genuinely high-T." True: Landtwing 2010 / Redmond & Einaudi 2010 put Cu-Au ore deposition at ~350-425 C, gold co-precipitating with bornite-chalcopyrite in the potassic core. So a hot gold phase is defensible — and indeed native_gold firing at step 0 is fine. But this rescues NATIVE gold, not the telluride mineralogy. The objection is specifically about calaverite/sylvanite and the ordering, which this does not touch.

DEFENSE B — "co-nucleation at one step is not a paragenetic claim." Fails. The tellurides fire at steps 0 and 4; chalcopyrite/pyrite at 24, bornite at 38. That is a genuine ~20-38 step lead, and it is hotter (398 C vs the post-cooling Cu stage). Worse, the ordering is baked in by design: Cu is withheld (Cu=0 initially, injected at step 25) while Te/Au/Ag sit in the initial 400 C brine — so the sim GUARANTEES tellurides precede the Cu-Fe sulfides. Nature does the reverse: web-confirmed that in telescoped porphyry systems telluride veins CROSSCUT (postdate) the earlier quartz-pyrite-molybdenite veins.

DEFENSE C — "the gate T_max permits it, so it's within spec." Fails hardest. Web-confirmed sylvanite incongruently melts at 354±5 C; the sim grows it at 398 C — ~45 C above its stability ceiling. The gate's own T_max:400 is unphysical for sylvanite, and its own T_optimal (sylvanite 225, calaverite 275) plus the author's own broth comment ("Te=2: epithermal-cap trace... porphyry-distal apron") show the model INTENDED these as late, cool, distal phases. The sim contradicts its own stated intent and basic Au-Ag-Te thermodynamics.

None of the defenses survive. The objection is correct on three independent grounds: paragenetic inversion (tellurides before Cu-Fe sulfides), thermal inversion (398 C vs the <300 C telluride window), and an outright stability violation for sylvanite (above its 354 C melting point).

Doctrine check: the fix is a clean growth-condition lever, not a scoreboard edit — either (1) deliver Te via a LATE cooling/epithermal-cap event after the system drops below ~300 C (steps 85/95), so tellurides form in the cool distal stage the author describes, and/or (2) lower the telluride gate T_max to real stability limits (sylvanite ~350 C) and steepen the hot-side T_factor so it zeros out instead of flooring at 0.4. Both move the model on purpose by changing conditions/gates. Causal control is understood, so this is exactly the actionable finding the review wants.

Severity: medium is right. It does not break any expects_species (chalcopyrite, pyrite, molybdenite all fire correctly) — the headline Cu-Mo-Au paragenesis is intact and the tellurides are non-expects trace surprises. So not high. But it is a visible, checkable inversion of the deposit class's defining porphyry->epithermal sequence, with an independent thermodynamic-impossibility kicker for sylvanite and a direct contradiction of the author's own note — well above low.

confirmed.claim: Paragenetic order of the centerpiece cascade: chrysocolla first-nucleates at step 71 and malachite at step 73, while azurite does not appear until step 179 — i.e. chrysocolla and malachite grow ~100 steps BEFORE azurite.
confirmed.objection: This inverts both the scenario's own stated centerpiece (azurite dominates at high pCO2, drops to malachite, then late silica → chrysocolla) AND real Bisbee paragenesis. Azurite is the EARLY high-pCO2 carbonate; malachite is its lower-pCO2 alteration product; chrysocolla is the LATEST supergene Cu phase (needs accumulated silica). Growing chrysocolla first and azurite last is a full reversal of the diagnostic sequence.
confirmed.evidence: Bisbee's diagnostic specimens are malachite-after-azurite pseudomorphs — the famous 'Roman sword' clusters from the Junction, Cole and Campbell mines — proving azurite crystallizes first and is replaced by malachite as pCO2 falls (researchgate 'Connoisseur's Choice: Malachite Pseudomorphs after Azurite — Part 1: Bisbee'; Vink 1986, the card's own cited pCO2-thermodynamics source). Chrysocolla is universally the last Cu-oxide phase. Root cause is visible in the broth: SiO2=500 ppm sits in the INITIAL fluid, so chrysocolla saturates the instant oxidation starts (step 71) instead of waiting for the bisbee_silica_seep event at step 265, and malachite fires off ambient Cu+CO3 at step 73 rather than following the azurite_peak(180)→co2_drop(225) beats.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Withhold silica from the initial broth (drop initial SiO2 from 500 to a low trace) and inject it ONLY via the bisbee_silica_seep event at step 265, so chrysocolla cannot saturate at step 71; and gate malachite's nucleation behind the azurite_peak→co2_drop sequence (raise its CO3/pCO2-drop threshold, or route it as an azurite-alteration product) so azurite's high-CO3 step-180 window precedes it. This restores azurite→malachite→chrysocolla by CHEMISTRY TIMING, not by reordering the scoreboard.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest defense I could build, and where each piece lands:

(1) MALACHITE HALF — REFUTED. The objection bundles "malachite fires at 73 before azurite = inversion," but malachite-first is defensible geology. Malachite precipitates across a wide pCO2 range; azurite needs elevated pCO2 (sim gate effectiveCO3≥120, delivered only by the azurite_peak monsoon, CO3=260). The famous Bisbee malachite-after-azurite "Roman swords" are the REVERSION of the azurite generation — and the sim models that correctly via the in-place azurite→malachite paramorph on co2_drop (step 225), NOT via the single step-73 nucleation. So direct early malachite + later azurite bloom + azurite reversion is a sound polyphase story. This half of the objection is not a defect.

(2) DESIGNED CASCADE — WORKS. The v226 nucleation events show azurite blooming at 179/183/184/198 (gated on the CO3=260 monsoon floor) and a SECOND chrysocolla at step 265 landing on ring 1 cell 64 — the exact site azurite occupied at 179/184. The intended azurite→chrysocolla-pseudomorph centerpiece executes in the right order. The reviewer's "full reversal" framing overstates: the diagnostic cascade IS present and correctly sequenced.

(3) MAGNITUDE — MITIGATED. The early chrysocolla is only 2 crystals (steps 71,72). grow_chrysocolla needs σ≥1 (SiO2≥20); SiO2 hits 0 by step 74 and there's no dissolution at pH 6.2/24°C, so those crystals grow ~3 steps then freeze small. Not a dominant early generation.

WHERE THE DEFENSE FAILS — the chrysocolla FIRST-APPEARANCE is genuinely inverted, and the reviewer's root cause is exactly right. The strip confirms it: SiO2 = 531→177→118→59→0 over steps 64–74. The primary 500 ppm SiO2 is drawn down by quartz but not before a ~10-step transient where the fluid is simultaneously cold, re-oxidized, pH-recovered, and still silica-rich — so chrysocolla nucleates at step 71, ahead of cuprite (73), native copper (126), and azurite (179). Chrysocolla predating the more-reduced cuprite/native-copper phases and the azurite bloom violates the established Bisbee paragenesis (chrysocolla is universally the latest Cu-oxide, silica-limited phase, which at a limestone-hosted deposit must wait for the porphyry silica seep the scenario itself schedules at step 265). The paragenetic table the claim card reports therefore lists chrysocolla first, actively misrepresenting the diagnostic order. The fix is a clean growth-condition lever squarely in the doctrine's wheelhouse — deplete/strip dissolved SiO2 on cooling (e.g. an uplift-step SiO2 drawdown or lower carried-over silica) so chrysocolla waits for the seep, or tighten its silica/redox gate — never a scoreboard edit. Because early chrysocolla is real, on the centerpiece, and traceable to a genuine broth-modeling gap, the core objection survives; it is just overstated on the malachite half and on magnitude.

confirmed.claim: Ruby is the first and only Al-phase to nucleate (step 19), taking all the alumina, from a broth deliberately loaded with Mg (fluid Mg=120 ppm; wall_Mg=15000 ppm 'dolomite-grade'); spinel never grows and is not in expects_species.
confirmed.objection: At Mogok the alumina-partitioning switch is Mg. With Al+Cr+Mg all present, MgAl2O4 spinel is the stable Al phase and forms PREFERENTIALLY; ruby only nucleates in micro-domains where Mg is locally exhausted. Red spinel is in fact MORE abundant than ruby at Mogok. Growing ruby directly from a high-Mg broth inverts the real causal control — the sim lets Mg sit idle while ruby takes alumina that nature routes into spinel first.
confirmed.evidence: Lotus/GIA Mogok geology primer + MDPI Minerals 2020 (U-Pb zircon/zirconolite in Mogok ruby & spinel): 'When Al and Cr join together with magnesium, the result is spinel. Only when the Mg is exhausted does ruby grow. This explains why far more red spinels than rubies are found at Mogok.' Primary occurrence = spinel-forsterite marble + phlogopite-graphite marble (Garnier et al. 2008).
