The inconsistency is real and confirmable — it just isn't the tropical-band nature-contradiction the reviewer framed; it's a hydrothermal-floor artifact and a metadata/causal-control wart. Fix is a legitimate growth-condition lever (make the ambient_cooling 25 °C floor scenario/cave-aware so air-mode cold-cave scenarios honor their declared T), never a scoreboard patch.

Net: core inconsistency stands (confirmed), but the specific evidence (23.622) is a quantization artifact of a physically-fine 25.0, the aragonite-compounding rationale is refuted, and the sim's output does not contradict nature — so severity drops from medium to low.

confirmed.claim: fluorite grew as a surprise (1 event, first step 0) in a meteoric karst dripstone cave (Frasassi/Carlsbad/Reed Flute composite)
confirmed.objection: Fluorite (CaF2) is not a karst speleothem. Cave drip water carries only trace F (<0.3 ppm) and is vastly undersaturated in CaF2; fluorite is a hydrothermal/MVT vein mineral forming at 50-150 C from F-rich fluids — the opposite of a cool meteoric drip. None of the four cited references (Frisia 2002, Sherwin 2011, Railsback 1994, Spotl 2007) reports fluorite at these caves; its presence is a confabulated species-that-should-not-be-there.
confirmed.evidence: Code-traced: scenarios.json5 (~L3386-3406) never lists F, but FluidChemistry defaults `this.F = opts.F ?? 10.0` (js/20-chemistry-fluid.ts:28). supersaturation_fluorite (js/33-supersat-halide.ts:58) then passes fluid_min (Ca>=10, F>=5) and computes product=(Ca/200)*(F/20)=(2000/200)*(10/20)=5, x T_factor(23.6C->0.47)=sigma~2.36 (floor Ca=3500 -> sigma~4.1), clearing sigma_crit=1.2 (_nuc_fluorite js/83:16). CaF2 mass action: at realistic cave Ca~80 ppm and F~0.3 ppm the IAP (~5e-13) sits far below Ksp (~3.9e-11) -> undersaturated. Standard karst hydrochemistry (Fairchild & Baker, Speleothem Science) confirms drip waters are dilute and F-poor.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Set the scenario broth F explicitly to a realistic cave-drip value (~0.1-0.3 ppm, below fluorite fluid_min.F=5) so supersaturation_fluorite returns 0. The defect is the leaked F=10 default; the scenario never intended any fluoride. Do not patch the scoreboard — close the gate by fixing the broth ion.

refutation.reasoning:
STRONGEST DEFENSE I could build, and why each prong fails:

(1) "Fluorite is a documented cave mineral (Hill & Forti, Cave Minerals of the World), so it's not impossible." FAILS: documented cave fluorite occurs in F-rich/hydrothermal host settings, not cool meteoric dripstone. Web check confirms fluorite forms hydrothermally and is "not commonly found in cave speleothems formed by drip water"; speleothems are calcite/aragonite/gypsum. None of the three anchor caves (Frasassi, Carlsbad, Reed Flute) is a documented fluorite locality, and none of the four cited papers (Frisia 2002, Sherwin 2011, Railsback 1994, Spotl 2007) mentions fluorite — they are all carbonate-speleothem references.

(2) "Frasassi/Carlsbad are hypogene sulfuric-acid caves fed by rising deep fluids that could carry F." FAILS: the scenario explicitly models cool meteoric drip (T=23.6C frozen, notes describe percolation/re-precipitation), and F is NOT authored as any thermal input — it is the generic global default. No F-bearing thermal fluid is modeled, and fluorite is still undocumented at these caves.

(3) "The sim inflates all ions ~30x to force nucleation in 150 steps, so F=10 is just inflated trace F." BACKFIRES: CaF2 has a third-order IAP [Ca][F]^2, so uniform ~30x inflation raises IAP ~30^3 (~+4.4 log units) and flips genuinely undersaturated cave water (SI ~ -2 to -3) into spurious supersaturation. The inflation convention is the artifact-generator, not a defense.

MECHANISM (confirmed by code trace): scenarios.json5 (~L3383-3407) never authors F, so it falls through to FluidChemistry's global default this.F = opts.F ?? 10.0 (js/20:28). That 10 ppm clears the fluorite gate's low fluid_min (F>=5, js/33:16), and combined with the deliberately high carbonate-demo Ca (2000 global / 3500 floor) at the cool T_factor (23.6C -> 0.472), product=(Ca/200)*(F/20)=5-8.75, sigma~2.36-4.13 > sigma_crit 1.2 (js/83-halide:16) -> nucleates at step 0, BEFORE even calcite/aragonite. A confabulated hydrothermal/MVT halide appears first in a meteoric karst cave, purely from an un-authored plumbing default. Realistic karst drip water carries F <~0.3 ppm and is CaF2-undersaturated; the reviewer's mass-action check is sound.

DOCTRINE: the fix is a clean growth-condition lever — author F at a realistic karst value (~0.1-0.3 ppm, or 0) in initial.fluid. At F<5 the fluid_min gate fails, supersaturation_fluorite returns 0, and fluorite vanishes with no scoreboard patch (no delete from expects_species, no baseline edit). This INCREASES causal control (F becomes explicit and physical), which is exactly what a real finding looks like. The objection stands and is directly actionable. Note: the same F=10 default likely trips fluorite in any other high-Ca carbonate scenario lacking an F override — a systemic smell worth a broader audit.

confirmed.claim: environment/saturation trajectory: SI_calcite +2.7, SI_aragonite +2.6, SI_dolomite +5.7 log Omega from broth Ca=2000, Mg=1500, CO3=2200 ppm; realized T=23.6 C (stated initial 18 C)
confirmed.objection: These are brine, not cave-drip, values and they contradict the cited localities' measured hydrochemistry. Real karst drip water (Frisia 2002; Fairchild & Baker) runs Ca ~40-120 ppm, Mg a few-40 ppm, HCO3 ~150-350 ppm, with SI_calcite ~+0.3 to +1.3 (Omega 2-20). Log Omega +2.7 = Omega ~500 (calcite) and ~5e5 (dolomite) are 1.5-2+ orders of magnitude too supersaturated. The realized 23.6 C also exceeds both the stated 18 C and real cave temperatures (Frasassi ~14 C, Carlsbad ~13 C, Reed Flute ~20 C), and warmer/over-saturated conditions are exactly what push fluorite and dolomite into their nucleation fields. This over-concentrated brith is the shared root cause of both preceding findings.
confirmed.evidence: Strip chips: SI_calcite 2.709, SI_aragonite 2.583, SI_dolomite 5.669 (log Omega), Ca 2001.97, CO3 2196.85, T 23.622; scenarios.json5 initial.fluid Ca=2000/Mg=1500/CO3=2200 and initial.temperature_C=18 (equator-mapping overrides it to 23.6). Contrast with standard cave drip-water datasets which sit near SI_calcite +1.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Reset the broth to realistic cave-drip concentrations and T ~13-18 C; drive supersaturation with a CO2-degassing (pCO2 drop) or mild-evaporation event so calcite/aragonite still cross their sigma_crit at cave-realistic SI while dolomite and fluorite remain undersaturated. Reconcile the equator-mapping temperature with the stated 18 C initial.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest defense I could mount: (1) this is an explicit MECHANISM DEMO (PROPOSAL-CAVITY-MESH Tranche 6) for per-vertex nucleation, not a hydrochemistry-fidelity model — the load-bearing physics is the floor/ceiling Mg/Ca RATIO contrast (0.009 vs 4.4) that drives the calcite-floor/aragonite-ceiling sort, and that ratio physics is correct; the author inflated absolute concentrations only to get enough nucleation events in 150 steps. (2) The surprises aren't all phantoms — HMC and even dolomite are genuine Mg-rich cave carbonates (Frasassi has dolomitic host rock; huntite/hydromagnesite/dolomite cave associations are documented), so dolomite isn't an impossible phase per se.

Why the defense fails. The challenged claim is specifically the environment/saturation TRAJECTORY, and the objection is factually right: Ca=2000/Mg=1500/CO3=2200 ppm are brine values ~20-50x above measured karst drip water (Ca ~40-120, HCO3 ~150-350; Frisia 2002, Fairchild & Baker), and SI_calcite +2.7 (Omega ~500) sits ~1.5-2 orders above the real cave band (SI +0.3 to +1.3, Omega 2-20). Two smoking guns tie the over-concentration causally to the surprises: (a) FLUORITE — the engine gate gives sigma 2.36 at Ca=2000 vs 0.094 at a realistic Ca=80, on only the default 10 ppm background F with NO geological F source at any cited locality; fluorite is a pure artifact of Ca inflation. (b) SI_dolomite +5.7 = Omega ~5e5 is non-physical for any low-T cave. Crucially, the inflation is NOT forced by engine calibration: the v144 SI carbonate engine uses textbook omega with calcite sigma_crit=1.5, which realistic cave Omega 2-20 clears — so dialing absolute Ca/Mg/CO3/F down to real drip-water values while preserving the floor/ceiling Mg/Ca contrast would keep the calcite/aragonite demo working and remove both spurious phases. That is a clean growth-condition lever, exactly what the drift doctrine wants, and its existence means the current broth is an effect-hack, not defensible bedrock. The temperature point (23.6 vs 18) is the objection's weakest limb — only +5.6 C and subtropically plausible for Reed Flute — but it doesn't rescue the sim.

confirmed.claim: magnetite (in expects_species and named as the stage-1 serpentinization byproduct) is a NO-SHOW, while awaruite grows with 3 events from step 0
confirmed.objection: Magnetite is the single most diagnostic, near-ubiquitous product of serpentinization: Fe(II) silicates + water -> serpentine + magnetite + H2, with the Fe3+ supplied by reduction of WATER, not by an oxidizing fluid. It forms under strongly reducing, H2-generating conditions — the exact regime that also stabilizes awaruite. Awaruite (Ni-Fe alloy) is downstream of, and far rarer than, the magnetite-forming reaction, so an assemblage that contains awaruite but no magnetite is impossible in a real serpentinite. The scenario grows the rare reduced alloy and omits the index oxide — backwards.
confirmed.evidence: Real mineralogy (web: PNAS 2004 H2-rich serpentinization fluids; Wiley 2021 Schwarzenbach awaruite; ScienceDirect 2023 alloys-serpentinisation): serpentinites = serpentine + brucite + magnetite +/- FeNi alloy; H2 from olivine hydration makes them among Earth's most reduced rocks and stabilizes Ni-Fe alloys AT THE SAME TIME as magnetite. Code root cause: MINERAL_GATES_magnetite in js/37-supersat-oxide.ts has O2_min 0.1 / O2_max 1.0 (comment 'Fe2+/Fe3+ both required') and oxideRedoxTent peak 0.4-1.5 with note 'T sweet spot 300-600C skarn/magmatic' — i.e. it is modeled for a mildly OXIDIZING hydrothermal/skarn magnetite, not reducing serpentinization magnetite. The strip runs O2 0.118 (steps 0-23) then 0.039 (step 24 on) and Eh -74 to -150 mV; after step 24 O2 falls below the 0.1 window floor so supersaturation_magnetite() returns 0 outright, and before that the tent floor (0.4) x fe_f(0.83) x T_factor(1.0) = sigma ~0.33 < sigma_crit 1.0. Magnetite can never nucleate here.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Give the magnetite supersaturation model a reducing-side serpentinization branch: a low-O2 / strongly-negative-Eh stability window keyed to the H2-generating serpentinization reaction (mirroring awaruite's existing O2<0.3, pH>=9, Ni>=50 gate), so Fe2+ + H2O precipitates magnetite under reducing conditions. This increases causal control (magnetite becomes drivable by the serpentinization redox trajectory). NOT a global sigma_crit drop and NOT a baseline/expects_species patch.

refutation.reasoning:
STRONGEST DEFENSE ATTEMPTED, THEN TESTED.

First, I corrected the reviewer's mechanism: EH_DYNAMIC_ENABLED=true (js/20c:52), so supersaturation_magnetite does NOT return 0 on "O2<0.1 floor". The active path maps the gate to an Eh window [ehFromO2(0.1)=-75, ehFromO2(1.0)=+220] mV. The strip's step-0 Eh (-74.016) actually CLEARS the window by ~1 mV; it only closes once Eh falls (~step 24). This gave me the best refutation lever: "it's a supersaturation shortfall, not a redox exclusion — the gate isn't categorically wrong, and magnetite is reachable through the EXISTING engine by raising the Fe budget." I recomputed the cleared-step sigma anyway: fe_f(0.833) x oxideRedoxTent(o2eq≈0.101 → 0.55) x T_factor(1.0) ≈ 0.46 < sigma_crit 1.0. Magnetite still can't nucleate.

That defense collapses on inspection. The tent PEAKS at o2eq=0.4 (≈Eh +75 mV) with the note "T sweet spot 300-600°C skarn/magmatic" — it REWARDS mild oxidation and penalizes reduction. This is the v17 oxidizing-hydrothermal/skarn magnetite. But serpentinization magnetite forms by Fe(II) oxidation coupled to WATER reduction (2H2O+2e→H2), i.e. in the most reduced, H2-generating fluids on Earth (Eh -300 to -600 mV). So making the scenario MORE accurate (truly strongly reducing, as its own notes claim) would push Eh far below the -75 window floor and close the gate ENTIRELY. Accuracy makes it worse — the definitive tell that the engine models the wrong phenomenon. Forcing sigma over 1.0 by cranking Fe would be a scoreboard hack via the oxidizing pathway, not the real reducing one.

Second defense (Frost 1985, cited in-scenario): "extreme reduction can suppress magnetite in awaruite-stable niches." Fails three ways: (i) in every real awaruite-bearing serpentinite (Josephine, Trinity, Red Mountain, Jeffrey itself) magnetite is a MAJOR phase and awaruite a microscopic accessory — magnetite >> awaruite; awaruite-WITHOUT-magnetite is not observed at whole-rock scale; the sim grows 3 awaruite events and 0 magnetite, exactly backwards. (ii) The strip Eh (-74 to -150 mV) is only "Fe-reducing/ferruginous" per the code's own scale — nowhere near the extreme reduction that could suppress magnetite. (iii) The scenario's OWN intent lists magnetite in expects_species and its stage-1 narrative says "chrysotile + brucite + magnetite + awaruite fire" — chrysotile, brucite, and awaruite all fire; magnetite is the lone dropout.

Third defense ("rodingite cabinet story, magnetite is just matrix"): fails — the scenario explicitly models a serpentinization-onset stage, puts magnetite in expects_species, and _nuc_awaruite (js/86:166-168) literally offers "with magnetite — serpentinization Fe-Ni pair" as a substrate, so the awaruite engine expects magnetite as its co-phase and never gets it.

Root cause confirmed exactly as the reviewer diagnosed: magnetite has only the single v17 oxidizing/skarn engine (grep confirms no native/serpentinization path), while its siblings brucite (v114) and awaruite got serpentinization-specific gates. The fix is a growth-condition lever fully compliant with the drift doctrine: give magnetite a reducing serpentinization mode (fires at low Eh / hyperalkaline / high-Mg, Fe3+ generated internally by water reduction), mirroring brucite's v114 treatment — NOT deleting magnetite from expects_species. Every refutation I could muster fails. The objection stands.

confirmed.claim: fluorite (CaF2) nucleates at step 59
confirmed.objection: Fluorite is not part of the Ca-Al-silicate rodingite paragenesis and is not a documented Jeffrey / ophiolite-rodingite species. Rodingitizing and serpentinizing fluids are effectively fluorine-free — there is no F source anywhere in the deposit model (dike plagioclase/cpx alteration, serpentinization, chromite trace all supply Ca/Al/Si/Cr/Ni, not F). A fluorite crystal in this assemblage is a chemistry the environment cannot supply.
confirmed.evidence: The Jeffrey broth in data/scenarios.json5 sets no F field at all (only Cl 5). FluidChemistry (js/20-chemistry-fluid.ts line 28) defaults this.F = opts.F ?? 10.0, so an unset scenario silently inherits 10 ppm fluorine. supersaturation_fluorite() (js/33-supersat-halide.ts) gates only on Ca>=min and F>=min; rodingite's very high Ca (350) x the leaked 10 ppm F x T_factor 1.0-1.2 in the 250-350C window clears sigma_crit. Real mineralogy: published Jeffrey rodingite assemblages are grossular-andradite + diopside + vesuvianite + prehnite + pectolite + zoisite + calcite (mindat loc-581; Rocks & Minerals 55(5); researchgate 'The Jeffrey mine') — fluorite is not reported.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Set F explicitly to ~0 (<=0.5 ppm) in the Jeffrey broth — rodingite/serpentinizing fluids carry negligible fluorine — which starves the fluorite gate. Systemic note: any scenario that omits F inherits the 10 ppm default, so the leaked default is a repeat confabulation vector worth auditing across F-poor scenarios.

refutation.reasoning:
I ran the current build (v226, seed 42) to hunt for a refutation and instead confirmed the firing: fluorite nucleates deterministically at step 59 (sigma 1.294 > sigma_crit 1.2; Ca=570 after the step-60 dike event, F=10, T=314.6°C, pH=10.17), and `_nuc_fluorite` (js/83) has NO RNG gate, so it fires every run (max sigma 1.36 at step 162). `initial fluid.F = 10` is confirmed as the FluidChemistry default (`this.F = opts.F ?? 10.0`, js/20:28) leaking into a broth that sets no F.

The strongest defenses I could build, and why each fails: (A) "fluorite is a rare documented Jeffrey species" — mindat loc-581 / ResearchGate give the rodingite assemblage as grossular-diopside-vesuvianite-prehnite-zoisite-calcite; fluorite never surfaces. Jeffrey's F is hosted by fluorapophyllite-(K)/fluorapatite, NOT fluorite. (B) "10 ppm F is realistic" — self-refuting: serpentinite whole-rock F (6-31 ppm) is locked in hydrous silicates; free fluid-F is far lower and apatite/apophyllite-buffered. If 10 ppm free-F truly sat in a Ca-570 fluid, real rodingites (extremely Ca-rich) WOULD precipitate fluorite — they don't, proving effective free-F is well below CaF2 saturation. (C) "it's an authored growth condition" — no; the broth sets no F, and the author's own notes enumerate anticipated surprises (tourmaline) but never F/fluorite. This is the drift doctrine's central sin: the model moved off a scoreboard-invisible default, not a growth-condition lever, so it can't be "moved again on purpose" — there is no F lever in the scenario. (D) "one event isn't a sequence claim" — irrelevant; the objection is about a foreign phase existing at all. Every defense fails; the objection stands.

The fix is doctrine-clean and a pure growth-condition lever: author `F` explicitly in the Jeffrey broth (F: 0, or a realistic sub-saturation trace like F: 1) so the F-poor rodingite fluid is modeled honestly — no expects_species edit, no baseline patch. Fluorite is the least defensible of the run's surprises (actinolite/titanite/dolomite/siderite/albite all have real Ca-Mg-Fe-Na-Ti-CO3 sources in the modeled broth; fluorite alone rides a phantom ion).

confirmed.claim: dolomite (step 0), siderite (step 139), and rhodochrosite (step 163) all grow; SI_dolomite reaches 4.09 and SI_siderite 3.84 across the run
confirmed.objection: Two problems. (1) Assemblage context: Mg/Fe/Mn carbonates belong to serpentinite CARBONATION (ophicarbonate / listvenite), a metasomatic process distinct from rodingitization; the Ca-Al-silicate rodingite proper carries only minor late calcite, not a dolomite-siderite-rhodochrosite suite. (2) Mass balance: they precipitate from a fluid with essentially no dissolved carbon — DIC is 0 for the entire run and CO3 is 0.3-0.9 mg/L then 0 after step 73 — so their high saturation indices are an artifact of hyperalkaline pH inflating carbonate-ion activity on a near-zero carbon pool, not a real carbonate-precipitating fluid. Siderite is a double tell: it is precisely the Fe that magnetite (Finding 1) failed to consume.
confirmed.evidence: Strip v226: DIC array all 0; CO3_2 array 0.315-0.932 then 0 from step 73; yet SI_dolomite 2.96-4.09 and SI_siderite 3.28-3.84 sustained. Real mineralogy (Wikipedia Rodingite; ScienceDirect Um Rashid rodingite; Canadian Mineralogist Koziakas): rodingite = grossular-andradite + calcic pyroxene + vesuvianite + epidote/prehnite + calcite; dolomite/siderite/magnesite are products of a SEPARATE carbonation process. Mn=1 ppm is in the broth (consistent with the documented manganoan/violet vesuvianite), so rhodochrosite has a nominal Mn source but no carbon budget to build MnCO3.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Make carbonate saturation honor the DIC mass balance — with DIC ~0 the dolomite/siderite/rhodochrosite gates should not clear regardless of pH-driven CO3^2- activity — rather than letting hyperalkaline pH alone inflate SI. Combined with the Finding-1 magnetite fix consuming the Fe2+ budget, the residual carbonate output collapses to the single minor late calcite that is actually documented at Jeffrey. This is a growth-condition (carbon-budget) lever, not a scoreboard edit.

refutation.reasoning:
STRONGEST DEFENSE, and how far it survives.

Defense hit 1 (lands cleanly): the reviewer's headline evidence is a strip-quantization artifact. The DIC chip (99j:435) reads fluid.CO3 on a 0-4500 mg/L scale; stripQuantize (85f:156) gives step 4500/254 ≈ 17.7 mg/L, so a real ~2-5 ppm DIC floors to byte 0 ("DIC all 0"). The CO3_2 chip's 0-80 scale has step 0.315 mg/L, and the reviewer's own "CO3_2 = 0.315-0.932" are exactly bytes 1-3 — the tell-tale quantization ladder proving carbon is present, not zero. So "precipitate from a fluid with essentially no dissolved carbon / DIC is 0" is literally false.

Defense hit 2 (lands): mass balance IS active. MINERAL_STOICHIOMETRY debits dolomite CO3:2, siderite CO3:1, rhodochrosite CO3:1; the broth (70r) starts CO3=5, floors at 2, never replenishes, and growth draws it down (matching CO3_2→0 at step 73). These fire once each — trace phases, not bulk. High SI on a small carbon pool is correct intensive thermodynamics (SI is a potential; precipitable mass is what's carbon-limited), not a bug.

Defense hit 3 (partial): the assemblage is not fabricated. Primary sources confirm the Jeffrey/JM Asbestos locality hosts calcite (canonical rodingite phase — correctly in expects_species) and dolomite (documented, 60+ minerals).

WHERE THE DEFENSE FAILS. The rodingite-hosted fluid-inclusion study at this exact mine (PMC2174467) states "Carbon dioxide was not detected in any of the samples," with CH4 >90 mol% of the carbonic species — a reducing, methane-dominated fluid. This is decisive: the sim carries the small carbon pool as OXIDIZED DIC and applies full hyperalkaline Bjerrum amplification (effectiveCO3 lets a 2-ppm fluid clear the CO3≥20 gate; observer SI hits log Ω 4.09/3.84), but in a strongly reducing serpentinization fluid (Eh -74 to -150 mV) that carbon is dominantly CH4 and largely unavailable to build carbonate. The sim lacks carbon-redox speciation, so it over-oxidizes the budget and lets trace Mg/Fe/Mn carbonates nucleate from the rodingitizing fluid. The reviewer's Part 1 is essentially right in kind: carbonates at these settings are a separate, later, cooler, more-oxidized carbonation overprint (ophicarbonate/listvenite), not products of the high-T reducing CO2-free rodingite fluid. Siderite/rhodochrosite are unconfirmed at the locality, and siderite is coupled to Finding 1 (the Fe magnetite failed to consume). So the mechanism critique — high carbonate SI leaning on pH-amplification of a tiny, wrongly-oxidized carbon pool — survives on the primary literature even though the "DIC=0 / mass-balance-violation" framing was a misread.

NET: the objection's core stands (sim precipitates carbonates from the wrong fluid/redox stage), but its severity is overstated. The clean fix is a growth-condition lever, not a scoreboard patch: partition carbon into CH4 under low Eh (suppress the carbonate-available fraction), and/or move carbonate nucleation into a late cooler oxidized carbonation stage — fully consistent with the drift doctrine.

confirmed.claim: rhodochrosite (pure MnCO₃) nucleates at step 0 alongside pure siderite (FeCO₃) from a single homogeneous fluid that is Fe-dominated (Fe 59.8 ppm vs Mn 7.87 ppm; Fe/Mn molar ≈ 7.5)
confirmed.objection: Rhodochrosite and siderite form a COMPLETE solid solution — (Mn,Fe)CO₃ is continuous end-to-end. From one homogeneous fluid you crystallize a single intermediate carbonate whose composition is fixed by the fluid's Fe/Mn, not two separate near-end-member phases. At step 0 the fluid is Fe/Mn ≈ 7.5, so the only carbonate that can grow is a manganoan SIDERITE; a pure-Mn end-member (rhodochrosite) crystallizing in the same generation from that Fe-rich fluid is impossible. A specimen showing pure rhodochrosite crystals next to pure siderite crystals of the same generation would be read as two different fluids, which contradicts the single step-0 broth.
confirmed.evidence: Verified: a complete solid-solution series exists between rhodochrosite (MnCO₃) and siderite (FeCO₃); the (Mn,Fe)CO₃ join is continuously miscible (older mineralogy confirms 'complete isomorphous miscibility' of rhodochrosite with siderite). Sources consulted: link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12932-021-00075-1 (calcite-rhodochrosite s.s.) and sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0883292701001184 (rhodochrosite/siderite solubility, anaerobic) plus minsocam AM27 rhodochrosite-from-Butte. Broth (strip v226): Fe 59.8 ppm, Mn 7.87 ppm at step 0; both minerals fire at step 0.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Gate rhodochrosite nucleation behind the Mn pulse instead of firing it at step 0: require Mn above a threshold only reached after the step-8 event (Mn 7.9→37.8 ppm). Then the Fe-rich early fluid grows a siderite generation and the post-pulse Mn-rich fluid grows a distinct later Mn-carbonate generation — a temporally zoned sequence, which is how real Mn-Fe carbonate generations record a fluid-chemistry shift, and it also reinforces the dim-early/bright-late lesson. This is a nucleation-gate-threshold lever, not an expects_species edit.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest defense I could build for the sim, and where it breaks:

(1) "Both phases are independently supersaturated, so nucleation is thermodynamically permitted." True in the sim's own math — I verified rhodochrosite sigma ≈ 1.43 (>1.0) and siderite ≈ 2.46 at step 0, each below its own Ksp. But being individually supersaturated is exactly the setup the reviewer addresses: for a COMPLETE solid solution the equilibrium product of one homogeneous fluid is a single (Mn,Fe)CO₃ whose composition tracks the fluid, not two coexisting near-end-members. Two pure phases are metastable relative to the solid solution.

(2) "Disequilibrium/kinetic co-precipitation is real; discrete Mn- and Fe-carbonates do co-occur." Genuinely mitigating, and Rds+Sd do occur together in nature — but they occur as INTERMEDIATE/zoned compositions or as separate GENERATIONS from an evolving fluid (the "two fluids" reading the reviewer flags), not as pure end-members from one homogeneous broth. The fatal point survives: at Fe/Mn ≈ 7.5 even a kinetically-partitioned Mn-carbonate would be strongly ferroan; a PURE MnCO₃ crystal is chemically forbidden by this fluid. The sim carries no Fe content on the rhodochrosite crystal (unlike HMC's mg_content), so it emits pure MnCO₃ — the one thing the fluid cannot make. That's the sim's apex test ("would a real rock agree?") failing.

(3) "It's a tutorial scaffold; rhodochrosite/siderite aren't in expects_species and don't touch the taught lesson." This is the real severity discount, not a refutation. The lesson is calcite fluorescence; the carbonates are incidental surprises. Notably the author's own event comment claims Mn≈38 is "below the rhodochrosite supersaturation requirement," yet Mn 7.87 already clears it at sigma 1.43 — confirming the firing is an unmodeled artifact, not an intended feature.

Why it's confirmed, not refuted or uncertain: the objection's core mineralogy (Mn-Fe carbonate = complete R‑3c solid solution; Fe-dominated fluid → manganoan siderite, not pure rhodochrosite) is well established, and the sim structurally contradicts it — siderite and rhodochrosite have NO Fe/Mn competition term, so any anoxic broth with Fe≥10 and Mn≥5 fires both as pure phases. Crucially the fix is a clean growth-condition lever, fully in the drift doctrine's spirit and matching the sim's own established pattern: a cation-fraction gate (rhodochrosite requires Mn-dominant / Fe not swamping it; siderite the Fe-dominant branch), exactly analogous to the existing rosasite (Cu-fraction ≥0.5) vs aurichalcite (Zn-fraction ≥0.5) branches — no scoreboard editing, no deleting from expects_species.

Corrected severity: low rather than medium. It's a confirmed nature-contradiction with a clean fix, but scoped to THIS scenario the stakes are low — tutorial scaffold, species not taught and not in expects_species, both phases genuinely supersaturated in-engine (a solid-solution/competition simplification, not a broken SI calc), lesson unaffected. The underlying missing Fe–Mn competition gate is systemic (affects any Fe+Mn anoxic carbonate broth) and would justify medium if filed sim-wide, but as raised against tutorial_mn_calcite specifically it is low-impact.

confirmed.claim: halite (NaCl) nucleates 8× starting step 21 in a 70 °C travertine hot-spring pool (Mammoth Hot Springs), the most abundant 'surprise' species
confirmed.objection: Halite is an evaporite that requires the fluid to concentrate to roughly 10× seawater (halite saturation ≈ 26 wt% NaCl, Na ≈ 100,000 / Cl ≈ 155,000 ppm). This broth is 6.3 psu — under one-fifth seawater — with Na 200 / Cl 300 ppm and 'concentration' flat at 0.984× (NO evaporation), i.e. ~500× below halite saturation. Mammoth travertine waters are dilute Ca-HCO₃-SO₄ type and halite is never found there; an NaCl crust plating a degassing carbonate pool is thermodynamically impossible.
confirmed.evidence: NaCl solubility ~360 g/L → saturated brine Na≈101,000 ppm; seawater (35 psu) is already ~10× undersaturated and halite precipitates only after ~90% evaporation of seawater (standard evaporite sequence, Warren 2006). The gate math is the smoking gun: js/33-supersat-halide.ts supersaturation_halite = (Na/100)·(Cl/500)·c² = (200/100)·(300/500)·0.984² ≈ 1.16 > σ_crit 1.0. The normalization treats Na=100 ppm / Cl=500 ppm as 'saturation' — ~1000× too dilute — so ordinary brackish spring water clears the gate. The code comment claims it 'stays dormant at scenario baseline (c=1)', but that is false for any broth with Na≳170 & Cl≳300 ppm.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Re-scale the halite supersaturation normalization in js/33-supersat-halide.ts so σ keys to real NaCl solubility: require an evaporative/vadose concentration event to drive concentration to ~10× (salinity ≳ 300 psu, Na → ~10⁵ ppm) before σ clears 1.0; at the tutorial's 6 psu / c≈1 the gate must return σ≪1. This is a nucleation-gate-threshold fix (explicitly allowed) and protects every dilute scenario. Do NOT lower the broth Na/Cl — 200/300 ppm is realistic Mammoth spring water; the gate normalization is the culprit.

refutation.reasoning:
Best defense assembled and tested: (1) The other two "surprises" ARE defensible — quartz reads as Yellowstone silica sinter (opal/chalcedony) and aragonite is a genuine warm, high-Mg travertine polymorph — so I hoped halite was the reviewer over-reaching on a broadly-plausible assemblage. (2) I checked whether the activity correction (ACTIVITY_CORRECTED_SUPERSAT=true, which the reviewer omitted) might suppress the raw σ below threshold. (3) I checked whether Mammoth waters or any event could supply Cl or evaporative concentration.

All three fail. The activity correction is negligible here: I(broth) ≈ 0.049 mol/kg → damped Na⁺Cl⁻ factor ≈ 0.956, so final σ ≈ 1.161 × 0.956 ≈ 1.11, still > σ_crit 1.0 — consistent with the card's own paragenesis (halite first at step 21, 8 events, the most abundant surprise). The co2_degas events move only DIC/pH/T and T never crosses 100 °C, so Na=200/Cl=300 and c=0.984 are fixed for the whole run — no evaporative spike exists. And Mammoth vent water is a dilute Ca-Mg-HCO₃-SO₄ type; halite is not a Mammoth phase. Halite saturation needs ~26 wt% NaCl (Cl≈155,000 ppm); this broth is ~500× undersaturated in Cl. An NaCl crust plating a degassing dilute carbonate pool is thermodynamically impossible, exactly as stated.

The root cause is precisely the reviewer's: js/33 line 97 normalizes σ_halite to Na=100/Cl=500 ppm as "saturation," ~1000× too dilute. The code's own claim that halite "stays dormant at scenario baseline (c=1)" is false — the concentration-independent product Na·Cl = 60,000 already clears the implicit 50,000 dormancy threshold before c² applies, so any brackish broth (Na≳170 & Cl≳300) trips the gate with no evaporation. Sylvite (K/200·Cl/800) carries the identical structural flaw, so this is a systemic engine bug surfacing in the flagship geological-accuracy tutorial, not a one-off. The remedy — recalibrate the halide-gate normalization to true NaCl/KCl saturation, or require a real evaporative-concentration event — is a growth-condition lever, squarely within the drift doctrine (no expects_species deletion or baseline-hiding needed). The objection stands.

confirmed.claim: quartz (SiO₂) nucleates at step 0 and crusts the pool at 67–70 °C alongside the travertine calcite
confirmed.objection: Quartz does not nucleate directly from low-T aqueous solution. Below ~150–180 °C silica precipitates only as amorphous opal-A (siliceous sinter / geyserite), which itself needs SiO₂ above amorphous-silica solubility (~120–140 ppm at 70 °C). The broth carries only 50 ppm SiO₂ — below opal-A saturation — so in reality NO silica phase forms at all. Furthermore Mammoth is specifically the CARBONATE (silica-poor) hot-spring system; siliceous sinter occurs at the rhyolite-hosted geyser basins (Norris, Upper), never at carbonate-hosted Mammoth. A silicate crust on a travertine tutorial is both a kinetic impossibility and a locality mismatch.
confirmed.evidence: The sim's OWN silica_polymorph() (js/25-chemistry-conditions.ts:181) returns 'opal' below 100 °C — the model already knows the 70 °C polymorph is amorphous opal, not quartz — but js/59-engines-silicate.ts:101 uses that result only to set the crystal's habit label (crystal._polymorph), not to select the species, so nucleation still records 'quartz'. The solubility table gives ~27 ppm at 70 °C → σ = 50/27 ≈ 1.85 > σ_crit 1.2, and the quartz T-window (T_min 50) admits 70 °C. Rimstidt & Barnes 1980 (GCA 44:1683) quartz precipitation kinetics: direct quartz growth is negligibly slow below ~150 °C.
confirmed.growth_condition_lever: Two levers. Scenario: drop the tutorial broth SiO₂ to a trace (~0) — Mammoth is silica-poor carbonate spring water — so σ_silica never clears. Engine (generalizable): have silica_polymorph() select the nucleating SPECIES (opal-A / chalcedony vs quartz) and require SiO₂ to exceed amorphous-silica solubility (~130 ppm at 70 °C) before any silica phase precipitates, so 50 ppm at 70 °C yields nothing. This wires the already-computed polymorph science into nucleation — not a scoreboard edit.

refutation.reasoning: Strongest defense: (a) grow_quartz relabels the crystal's display to 'opal' at T<100°C (js/59-engines-silicate.ts:101-102,130-135), so one could argue the sim is really modeling opal-A sinter, a real Yellowstone phase; (b) ~50 ppm SiO2 is a defensible dissolved load for hot-spring water; (c) co-nucleation at step 0 is not a strict-sequence claim. All three collapse. The killer is the sim's OWN opal engine: supersaturation_opal() (js/39-supersat-silicate.ts:823-841) gates on SiO2 >= 200 ppm (Fournier 1977 amorphous-silica solubility) and returns 0 for this broth (SiO2=50) — the physically-correct silica engine is SILENT here, confirming no silica phase should precipitate. The quartz path fires only because supersaturation_quartz() (js/39:486-499) references quartz solubility (~27 ppm at 70°C from the js/25 table, interpolated between [50,15] and [75,30]) giving sigma≈1.85 > sigma_crit 1.0, with no low-T kinetic floor — exactly the Rimstidt & Barnes 1980 error (natural waters are ubiquitously quartz-supersaturated but quartz cannot nucleate below ~150°C). silica_polymorph() (js/25:181-188) even KNOWS the 70°C phase is amorphous ('opal'), but that result only sets the habit label, never gates the species. Locality is also wrong: Mammoth is limestone-hosted carbonate travertine; siliceous sinter occurs only at Yellowstone's rhyolite-hosted geyser basins — the two systems are mutually exclusive. So the sim precipitates a silica phase (recorded 'quartz') that is a kinetic impossibility AND a locality mismatch, while its own correctly-calibrated opal gate proves the fluid is undersaturated w.r.t. the only phase that could form. The objection stands. Fix is a growth-condition lever (kinetic T-floor on quartz nucleation and/or route low-T silica through the amorphous-silica-gated opal engine), fully within the drift doctrine — not a scoreboard edit.

uncertain.claim: Silver paragenesis: argentite first nucleates at step 0 (T≈448°C) and proustite at step 19, but native_silver only at step 70.
uncertain.objection: Five-element paragenesis is inverted here. Native silver is the EARLY native-metal stage (co-precipitated with native Bi/As and the arsenides in the reducing primary); Ag2S (acanthite/argentite) and the ruby silvers (proustite, pyrargyrite) are the LATER, lower-temperature sulfidation/sulfosalt products. Crystallising argentite as a step-0, 448°C primary before the arsenides even appear, and pushing native silver to a late step, reverses the real sequence (and contradicts the sim's own wittichen scenario, where native Ag grows first and then sulfidises to acanthite+proustite).
uncertain.evidence: Standard five-element vein paragenesis (Kissin 1992; Markl/Burisch Schwarzwald work already cited in wittichen): native-metal stage (Ag, Bi, As + arsenides) precedes the sulfide/sulfosalt + carbonate stage. Schneeberg specimens are native silver/argentite/proustite with acanthite as a tarnish/late product — Ag-sulfide is paragenetically late, not a 450°C primary.
uncertain.growth_condition_lever: Gate argentite and proustite behind the step-70 cooling event plus a late sulfidation beat (raise effective S activity / lower T for the ruby-silver window), and let native silver co-nucleate in the reducing arsenide primary phase by keeping early free-S low. A redox/S-timing lever, not a species delete.

refutation.reasoning: Strongest defense, and it largely holds: (1) NAMING — argentite is not acanthite. The sim explicitly encodes the 173°C Ag2S polymorph transition (argentite gate T_min:173; acanthite gate T_max:173). At 448°C only cubic argentite can form; acanthite is thermodynamically forbidden. The reviewer's "(acanthite/argentite)" conflation is refuted — the sim is more sophisticated than the objection. (2) SULFUR REGIME is the real control, not a fixed sequence. Native Ag requires LOW fS2; in a sulfur-rich reducing fluid silver partitions into Ag2S. This scenario's primary is explicitly chalcopyrite (CuFeS2) + arsenopyrite (FeAsS), S=30 — sulfur-BEARING, not the sulfur-POOR arsenide vein the classic five-element "native-metal-first" model describes. So Ag2S-first is thermodynamically expected HERE. native_silver is doubly suppressed at step 0: T_max 300 (gated out at 448°C) AND s_f = 1−S/50 = 0.4 at S=30. It fires at step 70 only after cooling below 300°C and S drawdown. (3) The claimed wittichen CONTRADICTION is actually a feature of causal control: wittichen is sulfur-poor → native-Ag-first → sulfidises to acanthite+proustite; schneeberg is sulfur-rich → Ag2S-first. The two diverge BECAUSE their sulfur trajectories diverge — drift-doctrine compliant (the lever is broth S, not a scoreboard). (4) native_bismuth late (step 69) is physically MANDATED — Bi melts at 271°C (T_max 270), impossible as solid at 448°C. (5) native_silver at step 70 is defensibly supergene/cementation wire silver (gate cites Tsumeb supergene enrichment; the whole scenario is a meteoric-oxidation story). (6) argentite/proustite/native_silver are all SURPRISES — not in expects_species; the designed focus is the uranyl secondaries, the silver is incidental byproduct of the Ag=8 five-element seed. RESIDUAL that keeps this from a clean refutation: at Schneeberg SPECIFICALLY the deposit-type silver is classically primary native silver within the sulfur-poor Bi-Co-Ni-Ag-U arsenide stage; the scenario's sulfur-rich parameterization (needed to saturate its chalcopyrite/uraninite primary) does not reproduce that native-Ag-primary association, and settling whether it should would need primary paragenetic literature/specimen. The defense strongly weakens the objection (medium→low) and shows the output is causally honest and thermodynamically grounded, but cannot fully close the deposit-specific empirical question, so genuinely uncertain rather than cleanly refuted.

refuted.claim: aragonite nucleates at step 17 (~153 C) in a low-Mg (Mg/Ca 0.05-0.1) basinal brine
refuted.objection: Aragonite is the metastable CaCO3 polymorph; it is favoured by HIGH Mg/Ca and low T, and at ~150 C it inverts rapidly to calcite. The author's own note states 'low-Mg -> favors calcite over aragonite', and SI_aragonite is ALWAYS below SI_calcite in the strip — so both the environment and the thermodynamics say calcite, yet aragonite still nucleates. A hot, low-Mg MVT brine should not grow aragonite.
refuted.evidence: Strip: SI_aragonite (peak 0.945) tracks below SI_calcite (peak 1.008) at every step; T at step 17 ~153 C. Author scenario note: 'Mg/Ca ~0.05-0.1 (low-Mg -> favors calcite over aragonite)'. Aragonite->calcite inversion is rapid above ~100 C; aragonite in nature needs elevated Mg/Ca or low T to nucleate over calcite.
refuted.growth_condition_lever: Add an Mg/Ca-ratio + temperature VETO to aragonite's nucleation gate: aragonite should only fire when Mg/Ca is high (Mg poisoning calcite) or T is low. Given the mvt broth is explicitly low-Mg and hot, that gate stays closed and calcite wins as the author intends — a nucleation-gate-threshold fix keyed to broth Mg/Ca.

refutation.reasoning: Strongest form of the objection: at ~150°C a metastable polymorph whose SI sits permanently below calcite's, in a Mg-poor brine, should convert to calcite rather than nucleate and grow as a persistent crystal. That steelman fails on three checks. (1) The strip's SI_aragonite is raw log10(Ω) (carbonateSaturationIndex returns logIAP−logKsp). Aragonite is by definition more soluble than calcite, so SI_arag < SI_calcite is a fixed ~0.06–0.13 offset that holds EVERYWHERE aragonite has ever formed — it is not, and cannot be, a nucleation criterion. The sim correctly ignores SI ranking. (2) The firing gate uses supersaturation_aragonite() = omega × kinetic_favorability (Folk 1974 Mg pref + Burton-Walter 1987 T pref + Morse 1997 Ostwald + Sr/Pb/Ba trace boost), not the strip SI. Reconstructing step-17 from the strip (T=153.5°C, Mg/Ca=0.21, Ω_arag=3.7, Sr15/Pb40/Ba20 ppm) gives weighted sigma=1.095 > sigma_crit 1.0 — it clears, carried by T_factor≈1.0 and trace_factor=1.3, not Mg. (3) The reviewer's premise that aragonite needs LOW T is inverted: Burton & Walter 1987 (a cited source, web-confirmed) shows aragonite/calcite precipitation-rate ratio rises strongly WITH temperature; a hot brine favors aragonite nucleation. The Sr/Pb/Ba that promote aragonite metastable nucleation and inhibit its inversion (web-confirmed: Mg/Sr/Pb directly favor aragonite; Pb suppresses calcite) are precisely the ions enriched in this Pb-Zn-Ba MVT brine. The sim also models the inversion (grow_aragonite T>100°C & sigma<0.8 -> polymorph conversion) and a 400°C T_max (Carlson 1983), so the metastability physics is present; it doesn't trigger here because the trace-rich fluid holds favorability high — itself consistent with inversion-inhibition literature. Aragonite is a single-event, non-expects surprise phase. The objection as stated (should not nucleate in a hot low-Mg brine) is refuted by the exact papers cited. Residual, minor: the crystal persists and grows rather than staying a fleeting nucleus, and the no-inversion is an emergent side effect of trace_factor rather than an explicitly modeled inhibition — a size-calibration nuance to revisit via the favorability kinetics lever, not a scoreboard edit, and not grounds for the phase being spurious.

refuted.claim: dolomite never nucleates, though SI_dolomite is strongly positive (0.819 -> peak 1.323, exceeding SI_calcite) and dolomite is in the mvt nucleatable set
refuted.objection: Saddle (baroque) dolomite is THE signature gangue carbonate of MVT deposits and of Tri-State specifically (ore hosted in cherty dolomitic limestone; hydrothermal dolomitization is a defining MVT process). A supersaturated dolomite that never grows, while calcite/aragonite/siderite/rhodochrosite all do, is a diagnostic no-show for the deposit type. (Caveat: low-T dolomite kinetic inhibition — the 'dolomite problem' — is real, so a deliberate kinetic gate is partly defensible; but MVT saddle dolomite forms precisely in this hot saline window.)
refuted.evidence: Strip SI_dolomite peaks 1.323 (> calcite 1.008) yet zero events; minerals.json lists dolomite in the mvt scenario nucleatable set (so it CAN fire). Tri-State reference (KGS/mindat): 'common gangue minerals include jasperoid, dolomite, calcite'. Saddle dolomite is a classic 90-150 C saline-brine MVT phase.
refuted.growth_condition_lever: Lower dolomite's kinetic nucleation threshold within the elevated-T + high-salinity window (saline-brine catalysis overcomes the low-T dolomite problem that keeps it inhibited at surface). A T/salinity-catalysed dolomite nucleation gate lets the diagnostic saddle-dolomite gangue grow — a gate-threshold fix, not a scoreboard patch.

refutation.reasoning:
STRONGEST DEFENSE (and it holds):

1. The reviewer's headline evidence is a category error. The strip's "SI_dolomite" (peak +1.323) is a PURE thermodynamic saturation index — saturationIndex_dolomite in js/32b returns logIAP − logKsp, with NO compositional or kinetic gating. The nucleation path uses a DIFFERENT quantity, supersaturation_dolomite() (js/32 L301), which applies hard gates first: Mg≥25, Ca≥30, pH∈[6.5,10], and mg_ratio≥0.3, THEN compares an omega to sigma_crit=10. Comparing dolomite's log-scale SI to calcite's log-scale SI and concluding "dolomite should fire because calcite did" ignores that the two minerals have utterly different nucleation kinetics. Positive SI + zero nucleation is the literal definition of the "dolomite problem" — normal waters run strongly dolomite-supersaturated yet dolomite does not directly precipitate at low/moderate T. This is correct, textbook nature, not a no-show bug. The reviewer's own caveat concedes the inhibition is real.

2. Empirical confirmation (probe of seed-42 mvt, 120 steps): broth Ca=300, Mg=65 → Mg/Ca=0.217, and after the step-20 fluid_mixing dissolves limestone Ca climbs to 400, dropping Mg/Ca to 0.163. It is BELOW the 0.30 dolomite gate floor at EVERY step, so supersaturation_dolomite() returns exactly 0.000 throughout → 0 events. The blocker is fully identified and is a growth-condition lever (Mg concentration), exactly satisfying the boss drift doctrine: we know why it doesn't grow and can move it on purpose by raising Mg. The sim DEMONSTRABLY grows dolomite in sabkha_dolomitization (Mg-rich brine + Kim-2023 wet/dry cycling → f_ord ordering), proving the species is not dead — it is correctly condition-gated.

3. Geologically the low-Mg ore fluid is faithful, not an error. MVT ore brines are Ca-enriched CaCl2 brines precisely BECAUSE dolomitization of the aquifer consumed the Mg; the sim's Ca rising to 400 on limestone interaction (Mg/Ca falling further) is exactly this Ca-enrichment. Much Tri-State "dolomite" is the DOLOMITIZED HOST (cherty dolomitic limestone / jasperoid = wall-rock replacement), which is out of scope for an open-space vug-fill simulator. Dolomite is NOT in mvt's expects_species; the late open-space gangue the sim does produce (calcite) matches a common MVT paragenesis.

WHERE THE OBJECTION KEEPS A KERNEL: saddle (baroque) dolomite IS a diagnostic MVT vug-filling cement and forms in exactly mvt's 90–177°C window (above the Gregg & Sibley ~50–60°C roughening T — the repo even has a saddle-dolomite habit tag + observe tool). A real Tri-State druse often carries it. So a legitimate SCENARIO-ENRICHMENT argument exists: the flagship MVT scenario could optionally showcase saddle dolomite via a higher-Mg brine tune. But that is a tuning preference, contradicted by the low-Mg chemistry the author deliberately chose and cited, and it is NOT a "sim contradicts nature" defect — the current output is a correct rendering of a low-Mg, Mg-depleted ore-stage brine. The objection as framed ("a supersaturated phase that never grows is a diagnostic no-show") fails: that is the dolomite problem rendered correctly, with full causal control intact.

refuted.claim: Ba goes entirely to baryte (BaSO4, 11 events — the most abundant species) and NOT one grain of witherite (BaCO3) nucleates, in a CO3=250 carbonate-rich broth anchored explicitly on the North Pennine Orefield (Weardale).
refuted.objection: Witherite is the North Pennine's world-unique diagnostic mineral — 'a very rare mineral elsewhere in the world, but for reasons not fully understood it occurs in remarkable abundance in the North Pennines' (BGS). Type locality is Alston Moor (Withering 1784); Settlingstones (Northumberland, North Pennine) was the world's largest witherite producer 1873-1969. In this orefield witherite is the characteristic Ba gangue ALONGSIDE baryte. A North Pennine vein whose single most abundant phase is baryte, grown from a carbonate-rich fluid, that yields zero witherite is missing the one mineral that makes this locality famous. It is a diagnostic no-show, not an abstraction — the sim already ships a working grow_witherite engine (js/52).
refuted.evidence: BGS Earthwise / Northern Pennine Orefield + Settlingstones records: witherite occurs in 'remarkable abundance' uniquely in the North Pennines, type locality Alston Moor, Settlingstones world's largest producer, baryte+witherite are the characteristic barium gangue. Catalog description in data/minerals.json for witherite states the same and flags it should fire in the MVT-analog Ba+CO3+F+Pb+Zn assemblage. grow_witherite exists (js/52-engines-carbonate.ts:991, registered js/65-mineral-engines.ts:125) and gates on supersaturation_witherite()>=1; barite outcompetes it because BaSO4 Ksp << BaCO3 Ksp whenever sulfate is present.
refuted.growth_condition_lever: Make the diagnostic Ba-carbonate window real in the broth: witherite forms where sulfate is suppressed and carbonate dominant. The breach event (step 118) already injects CO3+130 — starve that reopening fluid of sulfate (drop residual S/SO4 in the gen-2 pulse, and/or add a mid-run sulfate-drawdown so late Ba is not consumed by baryte). With SO4 suppressed, Ba~20 and carbonate high, supersaturation_witherite clears its sigma>=1 gate and witherite grows at the reopened vents — exactly the North Pennine sulfate-suppressed mechanism. No scoreboard edit; the engine is already present.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest case FOR the objection: witherite really is the North Pennine's world-famous diagnostic Ba mineral (type locality Alston Moor, Settlingstones = world's largest producer), the sim ships a working grow_witherite engine, the broth is CO3-rich (250), and the reviewer's Ksp mechanism is textbook-correct. If "North Pennine + carbonate-rich + abundant barite" simply meant "expect witherite," the no-show would be damning. It does not survive, for two independent reasons.

(1) Zonal mineralogy — the reviewer mislocates the mineral. Dunham's Northern Pennine zonation (BGS Earthwise; Dunham 1934/1990) has a CENTRAL FLUORITE ZONE (fluorite+quartz+galena+sphalerite, over the Weardale Granite cupolas) grading outward to an OUTER BARIUM ZONE where baryte+witherite are characteristic and fluorite is ABSENT. The separation is "remarkably sharp... fluorite and barium minerals typically mutually exclusive." Settlingstones and the Alston barium veins are barium-zone; witherite's fame is a barium-zone phenomenon. This scenario is anchored on WEARDALE — the fluorite-zone TYPE CENTER, the most witherite-absent part of the entire orefield — and is explicitly fluorite-forward (F=25, both generations fluorite). A fluorite vein with no witherite is the CORRECT fluorite-zone signature, not a diagnostic miss. The reviewer conflated the whole province with the specific facies modeled.

(2) Mechanism is a clean, deterministic growth-condition outcome, not a scoreboard artifact. Initial Ba=20; the fluid_mixing event sets S=120 but never adds Ba, and no other event does either, so Ba stays ≤20 all run. supersaturation_witherite() hard-gates at fluid.Ba<30 (js/32:615), so witherite σ is pinned at 0 — a gated-out mineral, not an RNG miss. Even at the 30 floor, σ=(Ba/80)(CO3/200)·T_factor needs Ba≈64 at CO3=250 to reach σ_crit=1.2 — the broth is ~3× short. Barite's S=120 + BaSO4 Ksp ≪ BaCO3 Ksp scavenges the trace Ba, which is EXACTLY the mechanism the reviewer's own evidence cites. So the reviewer's cited chemistry predicts the sim's output.

Drift doctrine: the result is fully causal and movable — to grow witherite you raise Ba above ~30–64 and cut residual sulfate, i.e., model a barium-zone vein (Settlingstones), a different facies. The lever points at the right geology. Nothing in the model needs fixing; the current growth conditions correctly produce no witherite. Residual nit (out of scope of this objection): barite firing 11 events as the single most abundant phase from a Ba=20 pool reads slightly generous for a fluorite-forward vein, but that is a barite/fluorite count-calibration question, not the witherite claim, and if anything it reinforces barite scavenging the Ba.

refuted.claim: sylvanite ((Au,Ag)Te2) first-grows at step 0 (T=277 C), BEFORE calaverite (AuTe2) at step 4, in a system whose stated mechanic is 'at T>250 C calaverite (Au-rich end-member) wins; as Ag rises and T drops... sylvanite takes over.'
refuted.objection: The paragenetic order is backwards relative to both the standard decreasing-fTe2 telluride sequence and the scenario's OWN documented mechanic. Real gold-telluride paragenesis nucleates the Au-rich, high-fTe2, high-T phase (calaverite/krennerite) first, then progressively Ag-richer phases (sylvanite -> petzite -> hessite) as fTe2 and T fall. Here the intermediate Ag-bearing sylvanite leads and the Au end-member follows. The cause is a broth artifact: Ag is pinned at 15 and Au at 0.4 from step 0 (Ag/Au ~37), so the Au:Ag fork is already deep in sylvanite territory before any cooling has occurred — the fluid never passes through an early Au-dominant window.
refuted.evidence: The gold-(silver)-telluride deposit reviews describe telluride precipitation 'initially as calaverite followed by increasingly silver-bearing petzite and hessite' along a falling-fTe2 path; sylvanite is an intermediate, not a leadoff, phase. The scenario's own notes assert calaverite wins at T>250 C — the run is at 277 C at step 0, so calaverite, not sylvanite, should lead there.
refuted.growth_condition_lever: Start the broth Au-dominant (lower initial Ag, e.g. front-load Au relative to Ag) and RAMP Ag upward via the existing step-110 pulse (whose description already says 'Ag rises faster than Au, biasing toward sylvanite+hessite'). That makes calaverite nucleate first in the hot early window and the Ag-tellurides follow as the pulse enriches Ag — reproducing the decreasing-fTe2 sequence through a broth trajectory rather than a fixed high-Ag start.

refutation.reasoning: STRONGEST DEFENSE (and it holds on the nature-fidelity axis): The objection's central pillar — that sylvanite-before-calaverite violates "the standard decreasing-fTe2 telluride sequence" — is misapplied. Calaverite (AuTe2) and sylvanite ((Au,Ag)Te2) are BOTH ditellurides occupying the SAME fTe2 level; the decreasing-fTe2 ladder separates the ditellurides (calaverite/sylvanite/krennerite) from the lower-Te phases (petzite Ag3AuTe2, hessite Ag2Te), and does NOT order calaverite vs sylvanite relative to each other. Which ditelluride forms is set by the fluid Au:Ag ratio, not fTe2. So an Ag-rich fluid legitimately leads with sylvanite. My probe (seed 42) confirms sylvanite and calaverite co-nucleate within 4 steps (calaverite sigma=1.66 clears its 1.4 threshold from step 0 — it is eligible, just lost the 10%/step RNG coin until step 4), native tellurium leads at step 0, and the FINAL counts are near co-equal (2 calaverite : 2 sylvanite : 3 hessite) — a faithful Ag-rich gold-telluride assemblage. Web check: at least one authoritative telluride-mineralogy source reports sylvanite as the MOST abundant telluride at Cripple Creek (genuine literature disagreement with calaverite-dominant reviews), so sylvanite co-dominance/leadoff is within the documented range, not falsified. The sim therefore does NOT contradict nature; the "backwards vs fTe2 sequence / contradicts nature" headline fails.\n\nWHAT SURVIVES (lower severity, real): The objection's secondary point is correct — the scenario's OWN note-2 ("At T>250C calaverite wins; as Ag RISES and T drops... sylvanite takes over") is contradicted by the broth, which pins Ag=15 from step 0 (Ag/Au~37). Because Ag is front-loaded rather than rising, sylvanite is sigma-pinned as the winner at ALL temperatures (sigma 6.96 vs 1.66 the whole run), so the advertised temperature-driven fork never crosses. That is a genuine notes-vs-broth internal inconsistency and a pedagogical miss (the scenario is sold on a fork it never demonstrates). It is NOT a contradiction of nature, and it has a clean growth-condition lever: start Ag lower and ramp it via the existing step-30/110 fluid pulses (whose descriptions already say "Ag rises faster than Au"), which would make calaverite genuinely lead at 280C and sylvanite take over as Ag rises — matching both the fTe2 framework and the scenario's own text. Because a defensible-to-nature output stands but a real, actionable design inconsistency remains, the objection is refuted on its stated grounds (contradicts nature / violates the fTe2 sequence) while the residual issue drops to low.

refuted.claim: titanite (step 171, T ~180 °C) nucleates in a late low-temperature epithermal fluoride-carbonate vug.
refuted.objection: Ti is essentially immobile in low-temperature hydrothermal fluids and titanite is not part of the Sunnyside paragenesis. Hydrothermal titanite forms at ~250–670 °C; at <200 °C Ti mobility is restricted to sub-micron scale and titanite tends to break DOWN (to anatase + smectite), not crystallize fresh in a vug. A sphene in a cooling epithermal fluoride pulse is a species a mineralogist would not expect on any Silverton-district carbonate specimen.
refuted.evidence: Zr-in-titanite geothermometry gives hydrothermal titanite formation ~250–670 °C; titanite low-T alteration study (Clays & Clay Minerals 2005, CCMN.2005.0530110) shows Ti mobility limited to ~500 nm and titanite altering to anatase/smectite at low T. No titanite in Casadevall & Ohmoto's Sunnyside stages.
refuted.growth_condition_lever: Gate titanite nucleation behind a T ≥ ~250 °C threshold (so a cooling <200 °C fluid cannot grow it), or remove trace Ti from the late fluoride-pulse broth — Ti should not be a mobile late-stage component in this vug chemistry.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest defense of the sim, stress-tested: The objection's entire technical spine is "titanite nucleates at T ~180 °C, where Ti is immobile and titanite breaks DOWN to anatase+smectite." That temperature premise is factually wrong for the actual run. The v226 strip shows T at step 171 = 248.0 °C, not 180 °C — a thermal-pulse reheat fires at step 170 (T 150.6→248, SiO2 472→531, pH 6.61→6.5, Mn↑: the exact signature of the ambient_cooling fracture-fed hot-fluid pulse, js/85d:241-272, on by default here). This is corroborated by the σ math, which *forces* the conclusion: at the 150.6 °C trough σ_titanite ≈ 0.39, far below the σ_crit=1.0 gate, so titanite CANNOT nucleate there; it can only fire at the elevated pulse T (~248 °C, σ≈1.3). So nucleation is at ~248 °C — right at the floor of the reviewer's OWN cited hydrothermal-titanite window (250-670 °C, Zr-in-titanite), and a fresh hot silica-charged pulse is precisely where fresh titanite crystallizes rather than altering to low-T anatase. The reviewer's anatase-breakdown / 500 nm Ti-immobility citations apply to <200 °C static alteration and are inapplicable at 248 °C. Titanite is redox-insensitive, the gate correctly attenuates it to a capped trace (cap 3, only 1 crystal grew — a minor accessory footprint, not a headline phase), and it perches as a single sphene wedge on the reheated silica-supersaturated (SiO2≈531) Ca-rich (212) near-neutral fluid — geologically coherent for a titanite-bearing silicic-ignimbrite-hosted system.

Does the defense survive? Yes, on the objection as stated. Two honest residuals keep it from being airtight (hence low, not none): (1) titanite is not in Casadevall & Ohmoto's documented Sunnyside stages and epithermal ore veins are not a canonical sphene host — but the sim produces it as a transient-pulse trace, not a paragenetic stage, so "co-nucleation at one step ≠ a claimed titanite stage"; (2) the Ti that clears the gate is the UNIVERSAL FluidChemistry default (Ti=0.5, js/20:34) which exactly equals the titanite floor (Ti>=0.5, js/39:363), not a Sunnyside-modeled Ti source — so this species rides the ambient floor rather than deposit-specific causal control. If the boss wants tighter control, the clean growth-condition lever (per the drift doctrine) is to raise the titanite Ti floor above the 0.5 ambient default so it only fires where Ti is deliberately enriched (alpine clefts at Ti=1.0), which would also exclude this Sunnyside instance while preserving tormiq/grimsel — never editing expects_species or the baseline. But 0.5 ppm dissolved Ti in a hot silica-rich pulse within a titanite-bearing host is not unrealistic, so this is advisory. Net: the objection's evidentiary core (low-T breakdown at 180 °C) collapses because the real nucleation T is ~248 °C; the sim is defensibly right, with a minor unmodeled-Ti-provenance note.

refuted.claim: vanadinite Pb5(VO4)3Cl fires 6 events (tied most-abundant) beginning at step 69
refuted.objection: Vanadinite is diagnostic of arid, evaporatively-concentrated, oxidizing desert supergene zones (Mibladen, Arizona). Caldbeck is one of the wettest temperate districts in Britain, and the scenario's own broth never evaporatively concentrates (concentration flat at 0.984× the whole run) and dilutes toward meteoric (salinity 7.9→3.9 psu). Vanadinite is at best a rare, minor Caldbeck-district curiosity on OTHER veins (Silver Gill/Golden Hugh, Ingray Gill), partly resting on the discredited Kingsbury records; it is not a Roughton Gill phase and certainly not co-dominant. The genuinely Roughton-Gill-adjacent vanadate is mottramite (Brae Fell), which the sim already grows (7 events) — that one is correct.
refuted.evidence: WebSearch: vanadinite in Caldbeck is recorded only from Silver Gill (Golden Hugh level) and Ingray Gill, via Kingsbury & Hartley 1956 — and the district's Kingsbury records are noted as partly fraudulent. It is absent from the Roughton Gill species lists. Vanadinite's arid-oxidized-zone genesis is standard descloizite-group mineralogy; mottramite (Stanley et al. 1991, nickeloan mottramite) is the documented Caldbeck vanadate.
refuted.growth_condition_lever: Tighten the vanadinite nucleation gate to require evaporative concentration (a concentration/aridity threshold well above 1×) plus high Cl, which this temperate, diluting broth never reaches; with that gate the V budget partitions into mottramite (already firing) and vanadinite stays trace-to-absent, matching the district.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest defense of the sim, and it holds:

(1) PRESENCE — vanadinite IS a documented Roughton Gill / Caldbeck species. The Caldbeck literature records "yellow-brown crystals of vanadinite... on plumbogummite from higher Roughton Gill," with additional Caldbeck occurrences at Red Gill Mine and Arm O' Grain. These modern records come from the Russell Society reviews (Rumsey et al. 2008 Red Gill; Bridges et al. 2011 Part 3) and Cooper & Stanley — NOT the discredited Kingsbury 1956 Silver Gill/Ingray Gill records the objection leans on. So the reviewer's two factual anchors — "absent from the Roughton Gill species lists" and "rests on fraudulent Kingsbury records" — are both false. Bonus consistency: grow_vanadinite renders a yellow-brown/endlichite habit when As is present (crystal.habit='endlichite_yellow'), matching the real yellow-brown Roughton Gill micro, not the bright-red Mibladen desert habit.

(2) GENESIS — the "arid, evaporatively-concentrated, desert-only" premise is falsified. Vanadinite occurs at Leadhills–Wanlockhead, a temperate humid Scottish Pb district (~1500 mm/yr precipitation, ~13 C mean), as an "exceedingly rare" but real oxidation-zone phase — and that is the TYPE DISTRICT of the very linarite–caledonite–leadhillite trio this scenario is built around. The same wet-temperate Pb-supergene paragenesis provably hosts rare vanadinite. The scenario's dilute, non-concentrating broth (concentration 0.984, salinity falling) is therefore not disqualifying: the Cl for the apatite Cl-site comes from the documented basinal-brine reservoir (broth Cl=25), and the v193 O2_min gate correctly restricts vanadinite to the post-oxidation-onset window (step 69) where V(V) is mobile — paragenetically correct, not a desert requirement the sim is violating.

(3) The objection also overstates the metric: mottramite fires 7 events, vanadinite 6 — vanadinite is tied for SECOND, not "tied most-abundant," and mottramite (which the reviewer concedes is correct) actually out-ranks it, consistent with mottramite being the better-documented Caldbeck vanadate.

Where the objection retains a grain: 6 nucleation events over-ranks a phase every source calls "rare"/"exceedingly rare" — it fires 3x the headline linarite (2) and level with world-class pyromorphite (6). This traces to a real, growth-condition-shaped lever (the v193 V-leach bumped V to 14 to feed mottramite, and vanadinite's low V_min=2 / v_f cap at V=12 lets it saturate; a missing P+As-vs-V anion-competition fork lets vanadinite ignore that abundant pyromorphite/mimetite should win the apatite Cl-site at Roughton Gill). That is a legitimate down-tuning target — but it is a rare-phase-fired-too-often calibration point, NOT the "wrong mineral, contradicts nature, belongs only on other veins" contradiction the objection asserts. The objection's thesis is refuted; the sim is modeling a genuine, correctly-gated, wet-temperate supergene vanadinite that the reviewer under-weighted.

refuted.claim: turquoise CuAl6(PO4)4(OH)8·4H2O nucleates at step 90 with 4 events
refuted.objection: Turquoise is a mineral of arid, aluminous, weathered-volcanic terrains (SW USA, Iran); it is not among the ~50 documented supergene species at Roughton Gill. Its appearance here is a co-occurrence artifact — the sim has Cu (from the sulfides), Al (silicate 'basalt' wall proxy) and PO4 (the plumbogummite phosphorus) simultaneously available, so the engine assembles turquoise even though the temperate, diluting (salinity→3.9 psu), non-evaporative broth is the wrong regime and the P here is documented to express as plumbogummite/pyromorphite instead.
refuted.evidence: Russell Society / Cooper & Stanley lists for Roughton Gill: the Al-PO4 supergene phase present is plumbogummite (the type mineral, Hartley 1882; Förtsch 1967 mix-crystal) plus kintoreite/segnitite-type alunite-supergroup species — turquoise is not reported. Turquoise's arid aluminous-host genesis is standard.
refuted.growth_condition_lever: Gate turquoise nucleation behind an evaporative-concentration / higher Al-activity condition absent from this dilute temperate broth, so the co-present Cu+Al+P is routed into plumbogummite, pyromorphite and olivenite (all documented, all already firing) rather than assembled into turquoise.

refutation.reasoning:
The strongest defense: the objection's load-bearing premise — "turquoise's arid aluminous-host genesis is standard," so a temperate/diluting/non-evaporative broth is "the wrong regime" — is factually false, and the sim actually encodes the correct genetic control.

(1) GENESIS IS NOT ARID-RESTRICTED. Turquoise is documented from Cornwall, England, an explicitly humid-temperate oceanic climate (Phoenix United Mine, Gunheath/Bunny china-clay pits, Columb Major; a dedicated paper "On the Occurrence of Turquoise in Cornwall," Mineralogical Magazine v29). Saxony and Silesia (temperate central Europe) are also notable localities. Aridity in the SW-USA/Iran type deposits is a preservation/fame bias, not a genesis requirement. The reviewer's "standard arid genesis" claim collapses.

(2) THE SIM MODELS THE RIGHT CONTROL, AND IT IS THE LOCALITY'S OWN CONTROL. The turquoise supersat gate (js/38) suppresses turquoise when SiO2/CO3/Cl are high — it fires only in a carbonate/chloride/silica-DEFICIENT supergene fluid where Cu stays available for Al+PO4 rather than being scavenged into malachite/chrysocolla/atacamite. Roughton Gill's supergene chemistry is documented as carbonate-deficient (Rumsey et al. 2008) — the very reason linarite/caledonite dominate and leadhillite is rare. So turquoise fires BECAUSE of the same low-CO3 condition that defines the locality's headline sulfate paragenesis. This is causal control, not a "co-occurrence artifact": the gate is a real gated supersaturation with anion competition, not a naive Cu+Al+PO4 → precipitate.

(3) THE REVIEWER'S REGIME DESCRIPTORS FAVOR turquoise. Low salinity (3.9 psu) = low Cl = less atacamite competition = pro-turquoise. "Non-evaporative" is correct — turquoise is a weathering-zone phosphate, not an evaporite. Those descriptors disqualify an evaporite; applying them to turquoise is a category slip.

(4) INGREDIENTS + Al-PO4 CRYSTALLIZATION ARE LOCALLY ATTESTED. The reviewer concedes plumbogummite (Pb-Al-PO4) is the TYPE mineral here, so supergene Al+PO4 mobility and aluminium-phosphate crystallization is the defining locality behavior. Turquoise is the Cu A-site member of the same phosphate family (the turquoise group is an A-site Cu/Fe/Zn/vacancy family), and the deposit is Cu-rich (linarite/caledonite/brochantite/mottramite/olivenite). A Cu-Al-PO4 phase is a small, chemically-natural step from the documented Pb-Al-PO4.

(5) NO VALID GROWTH-CONDITION LEVER EXISTS to remove it: the Al that correctly makes plumbogummite makes turquoise; T/pH/O2 at step 90 are textbook supergene and in-gate (nucleation proves T∈[5,80], pH∈[6,8.5], O2>0.5). Deleting turquoise would be the scoreboard patch the doctrine forbids. And the sim never asserts turquoise as a documented Roughton Gill species — it is explicitly in the "Surprises (grown but NOT in expects_species)" list; the version notes celebrate it as accuracy-INCREASING supergene drift.

Does the SIM survive? Yes. The objection's genetic case is refuted, the "artifact" framing mischaracterizes the gate, and the environment is faithful. The only residual is a minor naturalism-of-labeling point: turquoise is not on the published Roughton Gill list, and the engine emits "turquoise s.s." rather than a humid-climate-appropriate turquoise-group/rashleighite (Fe-bearing) or planerite label, with narrator prose over-narrating "arid." That is a defensible non-scoreboard refinement (color/label dispatch + narrator), not a nature-contradiction — so it does not sustain the reviewer's medium severity.

refuted.claim: realgar (As4S4) nucleates at step 133 — grown but NOT in expects_species (the flagged surprise)
refuted.objection: Realgar is an arsenic SULFIDE that needs elevated reduced aqueous sulfide and low-redox conditions, yet this scenario's entire premise is a sulfur-STARVED brine (~3 ppm S) in which native arsenic is thermodynamically stable OVER realgar — exactly why the sim (correctly) grows native_arsenic. Worse, it nucleates in the OXIDIZING meteoric tail (Eh ~+68 mV at step 133, indexed from the strip) co-precipitating with sulfate barite: reduced As(II)-S and sulfate cannot both be stable in one fluid. The Wittichen type veins are documented sulphide-absent, so realgar has no home here at all. It is a surprise precisely because its gate is misfiring.
refuted.evidence: Staude, Bons & Markl 2012 (Mineralium Deposita) 'Multi-stage Ag-Bi-Co-Ni-U... Wittichen': ore = native elements + Co-Ni arsenides + pitchblende, 'sulphides are absent'; As goes to Co-arsenides + secondary arsenates. Scharrer, Kreissl & Markl 2019 (Ore Geol. Rev.): stability of native As over realgar/orpiment is set by reduced-sulfide concentration — in S-poor fluids As stays native. Realgar forms in low-redox microenvironments at 100-200°C requiring reduced sulfide (geologyscience.com/minerals/realgar; en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Realgar; ScienceDirect S0883292718302245).
refuted.growth_condition_lever: Tighten realgar's nucleation gate to require reducing Eh (roughly < -100 mV) AND a reduced-sulfide threshold this S-starved, late-oxidizing broth never reaches. The same low trace-S that correctly keeps native arsenic stable must also VETO its sulfide — the gate is currently firing on As abundance without a redox/sulfide check in the +70 mV tail.

refutation.reasoning:
STRONGEST DEFENSE OF THE SIM — and it survives.

The reviewer's premise ("sulfur-starved ~3 ppm S, native As stable over realgar") is true ONLY for the hot native-metal/arsenide stage (steps 0–132). I ran the scenario headless (seed 42): σ_realgar = 0.00 for ALL 133 prior steps — the S≥30 gate held realgar OFF exactly as the S-starvation premise demands, which is precisely why the sim grows 4 native_arsenic and zero realgar through the metal stage. The gate is not misfiring; it is enforcing the reviewer's own thermodynamics.

Realgar nucleates at loop-step 133 for one reason: that is the exact step the DELIBERATELY-AUTHORED meteoric influx (event_wittichen_meteoric_sulfate, js/70u line 55) raises fluid.S 3→30. The probe shows S flipping 3.0→30.0 and σ_realgar going 0.00→1.01 in the same step. This is a scenario growth-condition event, not a scoreboard artifact. Change the event and realgar moves — full causal control, exactly the drift doctrine's test.

The co-nucleation is the kill-shot: proustite (Ag3AsS3) — an EXPECTED reduced-As-S sulfosalt — nucleates at the SAME step 133 (σ=1.57), and acanthite (Ag2S, expected) follows on the same sulfur. The reviewer's argument ("no reduced sulfur here / reduced As-S impossible") would delete proustite AND acanthite from expects too. The author already committed to a late sulfide stage; realgar is the same phenomenon, and with As=120 ppm (the richest ion in the entire broth) a trace As-sulfide is arguably MORE expected than the Ag-limited ruby silver.

Redox is within tolerance, not "impossible": realgar's gate passes at Eh ≤ ehFromO2(1.2) ≈ +252 mV; the +68 mV at step 133 clears it comfortably (mildly reducing-to-mixed, the documented hot-spring realgar regime the engine cites — Sulphur Bank/Yellowstone). The "sulfate barite can't coexist with reduced As-S" claim is refuted by the barite engine itself (js/40 line 185–187): it deliberately opens at the SO4/H2S Eh boundary (~O2≈0.4, and O2 sits at 0.37 here), explicitly "allows barite + galena coexistence (MVT diagnostic)." Sulfate/sulfide coexistence at the redox boundary is textbook MVT and hot-spring mineralogy; the objection applies strict equilibrium where kinetic metastability rules.

Empirical clincher: the reviewer's OWN cited paper (Scharrer, Kreissl & Markl 2019, Ore Geol. Rev.) states that when certain conditions in native-element/arsenide systems aren't met "the result is a realgar-orpiment mineralization" — realgar is a documented member of five-element variability, with a common late sulfide/sulfosalt stage. Literature/mindat also record realgar in the Wittichen area ("an uncommon mineralization of arsenic, galena, sphalerite together with realgar").

Where the objection has a grain of truth: Wittichen's PRIMARY assemblage is genuinely sulphide-poor and realgar is a rare/atypical accessory there. But the sim reflects exactly that — ONE crystal, σ=1.01 (a hair over threshold), 0 µm growth, displacing nothing. A trace whisper is the proportionate outcome for a rare-in-district phase. The only genuine model nuance is the single fluid.S pool serving both sulfate and sulfide minerals — but that is shared infrastructure (deliberate, MVT-justified), not a wittichen defect, and any refinement would be a growth-condition lever affecting proustite/acanthite equally, never a realgar-specific patch.

The doctrine-correct response to this real, science-grounded surprise is to ADD realgar to expects_species (documenting the late As-S stage) or annotate it as a rare accessory — not to suppress it. The gate is right; the objection ("its gate is misfiring") fails.

refuted.claim: Primary Co-Ni arsenide stage is represented ONLY by nickeline + cobaltite (+ arsenopyrite); skutterudite, safflorite, and rammelsbergite are no-shows despite all three existing in the catalog and firing in the sister five-element scenario (wittichen).
refuted.objection: Schneeberg IS the type district of the Fünfelementformation; its defining primary ore is the Co-Ni-Fe di/tri-arsenide suite — skutterudite ('Speiskobalt', the principal historic cobalt ore), safflorite, rammelsbergite — intergrown with native Bi/Ag/As. Growing a monoarsenide (nickeline) + a sulfarsenide (cobaltite) but omitting the tri-arsenide skutterudite and di-arsenide safflorite guts the deposit's diagnostic mineralogy. Safflorite's TYPE LOCALITY is literally the Schneeberg district (Daniel mine, Neustädtel).
refuted.evidence: Web-verified: mindat/iRocks Schneeberg ore suite = 'skutterudite, native bismuth, cobaltite, ... rammelsbergite, safflorite, loellingite'; safflorite type locality = Daniel mine, Neustädtel, Schneeberg district; classic specimens are cobaltite perched on a SKUTTERUDITE matrix. The engines work — wittichen (S=3, As=120, 320-420°C reducing) fires skutterudite+safflorite. Schneeberg's broth (S=30, As=60) routes As into arsenopyrite+cobaltite and starves the arsenide field.
refuted.growth_condition_lever: In the pegmatite/primary reducing phase, raise As (toward the Förster-1992 100-1000 ppm arsenide-phase range already cited) and add a sulfur-starving sink (or lower free S) so metal:As favours di/tri-arsenides over sulfarsenides, holding T in the 320-420°C arsenide window (mirror the wittichen primary stage). That opens the skutterudite/safflorite/rammelsbergite nucleation gates — a T/As/S-trajectory lever, not an expects/baseline edit.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest defense of the sim: the reviewer's own diagnosis is mechanistically CORRECT ("S=30, As=60 routes As into arsenopyrite+cobaltite and starves the arsenide field") — but that mechanism is good science, not a bug. In js/41-supersat-sulfide.ts the three "no-show" phases are hard-gated off by sulfur on principle: skutterudite `if S>5 return 0 // No sulfur tolerance`, safflorite `if S>15 return 0`, rammelsbergite `if S>20 return 0` (and loellingite `if S>1`, "Sharp arsenopyrite boundary", Kretschmar & Scott 1976). Schneeberg's broth is S=30, so all four pure di/tri-arsenides are correctly suppressed. Mirror-image confirmation: the phases that DO fire are the sulfur-REQUIRING analogues — arsenopyrite gates `S<10 return 0` (needs S≥10), cobaltite is a CoAsS sulfarsenide built for the sulfidic regime, nickeline is a NiAs monoarsenide with no upper-S gate. This is the textbook sulfarsenide↔pure-arsenide fork: at high sulfur activity Co→cobaltite (not skutterudite), Fe→arsenopyrite (not loellingite). The sim is internally coherent and accurate-to-life for its declared broth.
