Crucially, S=30 is load-bearing for the scenario's ACTUAL target: the entire uranyl-supergene cascade (torbernite/zeunerite/autunite/uranospinite) derives its Cu+As from weathering a chalcopyrite + arsenopyrite primary — both sulfur-requiring. To fire skutterudite you must set S<5, which deletes chalcopyrite+arsenopyrite and collapses the whole uranium-vein paragenesis. So the reviewer's implied fix is not a clean growth-condition lever; it is a self-contradiction within one broth. The scenario is honestly scoped to the sulfidic URANIUM slice (Walpurgis Flacher / Weisser Hirsch = the uranyl-mica type localities), not the sulfur-poor five-element arsenide stage. And the arsenide suite IS represented in the sim — via the SAME engines — in the sister scenario wittichen (S=3), which fires skutterudite+safflorite. Same code, different sulfur, different assemblage: the exact drift-doctrine ideal ("we understand why the model moved and can move it again by changing growth conditions"). skutterudite/safflorite/rammelsbergite were never in schneeberg's expects_species, so this is correct scoping, not a deleted-from-scoreboard patch.

Does the defense survive? Yes. The objection frames a correct sulfur-fork as "gutting the diagnostic mineralogy," conflating district-level mineralogy (Schneeberg the district hosts multiple, chemically-incompatible formations — a sulfur-poor biconi arsenide stage AND a sulfidic uranium stage) with the single paragenetic slice a scenario can model. One broth cannot fire both the pure arsenides (need low S) and the chalcopyrite/arsenopyrite/cobaltite (need high S) — they are mutually exclusive on the sulfur axis. The sim picked the uranium slice, the pure arsenides are principled-gated off by a cited sulfur lever, and the arsenide slice lives in wittichen. The residual that survives is only a minor curation point: safflorite's type locality is the Schneeberg district, so a Schneeberg-named scenario showcasing it (a future S-poor sub-scenario) would be nice — additive scope, not a chemistry correction. That drops the severity from high to low, but does not confirm the objection.

refuted.claim: turquoise (Cu-Al phosphate) nucleates at step 90 in the supergene phase, riding the Cu + P + Al oxidation broth.
refuted.objection: Turquoise is a supergene Cu-Al phosphate that forms in ACIDIC (pH ~3-6), oxidizing, aluminous weathering profiles — it is not a reported Schneeberg species, and it directly contradicts this scenario's carbonate-buffered, near-neutral oxidation (pH pinned at 6.5). It appears only because the oxidation fluid happens to carry Cu, P and Al simultaneously; the real Schneeberg oxidized zone yields uranyl micas, the erythrite/annabergite cobalt bloom, and Cu carbonates — not turquoise.
refuted.evidence: Turquoise-group phosphates require low-pH, Al-rich supergene conditions (classic arid Cu-oxidation caps); absent from Schneeberg's near-neutral carbonate-country oxidation. The card's own pH trajectory never leaves 6.17-6.50, i.e. it never enters turquoise's stability window, yet turquoise grew — a gate that ignores the buffered pH.
refuted.growth_condition_lever: Tie turquoise's nucleation gate to pH (require pH < ~5.5); the scenario's carbonate-buffered near-neutral pH then suppresses it naturally. Alternatively drop supergene Al activity in the oxidation phase. Fix is the pH/Al growth-condition, not removing turquoise from the baseline.

refutation.reasoning: Strongest defense: The objection's load-bearing claim — that turquoise fired via "a gate that ignores the buffered pH" — is demonstrably false in the code. supersaturation_turquoise() (js/38-supersat-phosphate.ts:587) reads this.fluid.pH and penalizes outside a 6.0-8.5 window, and grow_turquoise() (js/58-engines-phosphate.ts:590) actively DISSOLVES turquoise at pH<5 ("turquoise + H+ -> Cu2+ + Al3+ + H3PO4"). The scenario pH (6.17-6.50) is inside the favorable window, so turquoise grew BECAUSE pH was favorable, precisely a pH-aware growth-condition lever, not a scoreboard artifact. Second, the reviewer's "carbonate-buffered near-neutral -> Cu carbonates" premise is contradicted by the scenario's own saturation drivers: SI_calcite -3.9 to -2.1 and SI_dolomite -7.4 to -5.0 show carbonate deeply UNDERSATURATED. The sim grew chrysocolla + brochantite + cuprite and NO malachite/azurite — the fingerprint of a low-CO3, aluminosilicate-hosted (pegmatite/greisen) Cu-oxidation zone, which is exactly the aluminous, phosphatic, oxidizing, low-T setting turquoise wants. The three cations are genuinely sourced (Cu from chalcopyrite weathering, Al from the feldspar/spodumene/topaz/morganite pegmatite, P from the uranyl-phosphate broth), so the co-occurrence is causal, not coincidental. The pH quibble is also marginal: the reviewer's own upper bound is 6, the scenario sits at 6.17-6.50 (the acidic/neutral seam), and since turquoise is soluble in hot HCl, the pH-3 end of the reviewer's window is where turquoise dissolves, not forms — the sim's "acidic transport, partially-neutralized deposition" model is defensible. Does it survive? Yes. The objection's mechanism claim and its geochemical framing both fail against the actual code and the card's own numbers. Residual valid kernel: turquoise is not a documented Schneeberg species and the alkaline edge of the window (8.5) may be generous — but turquoise is flagged as a SURPRISE (not in expects_species), and a chemically-consistent emergent phase is a feature of a generative sim, not a species to delete. No scoreboard edit is warranted; at most a broth-lever note (P is largely consumed by uranyl phosphates, so tightening supergene P slightly would suppress it if desired).

refuted.claim: Quartz first nucleates at step 84 (T≈310°C) as a single event, AFTER every sulfide, and is the second-rarest phase (1 of a possible 10).
refuted.objection: In a porphyry Cu deposit quartz is the volumetrically dominant gangue and forms the early, high-temperature A/B stockwork veins that HOST the Cu-Fe sulfides. At Bingham quartz precipitated above 400°C and even partially re-dissolved at 350–425°C, creating the very vugs the Cu-Fe sulfides then filled. Quartz being the last and nearly rarest phase contradicts the deposit's defining architecture (the scenario's own wall note calls it a 'quartz stockwork').
refuted.evidence: js/25-chemistry-conditions.ts: silica solubility is PROGRADE with SiO2≈700 ppm at 300°C; broth SiO2=700 pins σ_quartz=1.0 to exactly 300°C, only reached near run end, so quartz is starved through the whole hot ore stage. minerals.json quartz max_nucleation_count=10 but the strip records 1 event. Real geology: Landtwing et al. 2005 (EPSL 235) / 2010 (Econ. Geol. 105) quartz-dissolution-then-sulfide model at Bingham.
refuted.growth_condition_lever: Raise broth SiO2 to quartz saturation at the 400°C start (≥1200 ppm; ~1600 ppm models arrival from a hotter ~600°C source). Quartz then supersaturates from step 0 and plates continuously as the fluid cools, building the stockwork before/with the sulfides — a broth-chemistry change, not a baseline/scoreboard patch.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest defense of the objection: in a porphyry Cu deposit quartz IS the volumetrically dominant gangue and the scenario's own wall note calls it a "quartz stockwork," yet the sim makes quartz the second-rarest phase and grows it dead last, after every sulfide — apparently inverting the deposit's defining architecture. If the scenario is meant to reproduce the whole deposit, that looks fatal.

But the objection fails on the actual physics of the modeled window, and — decisively — the reviewer's own cited papers refute it. At Bingham the vein/vug paragenesis (Landtwing 2005 EPSL 235; Redmond 2004 Geology 32:217; Landtwing 2010 Econ. Geol. 105:43) is: (1) the BULK vein quartz (Q1) crystallized EARLY, above ~400 °C, forming the stockwork framework; (2) Cu-Fe sulfides precipitated LATER, at 425–350 °C, filling dissolution vugs/microfractures WITHIN that earlier quartz; (3) a SECOND, late quartz generation (Q2) "finally filled" the microfracture network AFTER the sulfides. The scenario starts at 400 °C, so the dominant early Q1 quartz forms above the modeled window and is correctly the pre-existing wall/host (the "quartz stockwork" the vug sits in) — not something that should nucleate into the open cavity. Within the 400→310 °C window the sim actually reproduces the real order: sulfides (steps 22–44), then a single minor late quartz event at step 84 / 307 °C = Q2, the genuine last-crystallizing quartz. So "quartz last and minor in the vug" is not a contradiction of nature — it is what the vug-scale paragenesis at Bingham literally does.

The reviewer's supporting evidence is also wrong on the numbers: SiO2 is not static at 700 pinning saturation to 300 °C — the strip shows it rising to ~1122 ppm mid-run, so σ_quartz crosses 1 during cooling (~step 63) and quartz nucleates after the step-85 cooling event, not merely at run-end. And "quartz starved through the hot ore stage" is not a defect: quartz SHOULD be undersaturated (net dissolving) through the 425–350 °C Cu window — that dissolution is the vug-forming mechanism the reviewer's own citations invoke. The reviewer conflated total deposit quartz volume (dominated by pre-window Q1) with vug-filling paragenetic order (where late Q2 is genuinely last). This survives as a refutation.

Honest residuals, none of which rescue the objection: the prograde-only silica_equilibrium table cannot represent the retrograde dissolution that actually carves the Bingham vugs (the sim just fails to precipitate quartz there rather than dissolving it), and SiO2=700 is an admitted non-retuned carryover whose placement of saturation near 300 °C is fortunate rather than literature-derived. Both are cavity-genesis/scope limitations, not the paragenetic inversion the objection alleged. If anything the sim is impressively faithful here. Any real fix would be a legitimate growth-condition lever (encode a retrograde-solubility branch, or anchor SiO2 to measured brine values), but the current output is defensibly correct as-is.

refuted.claim: No magnetite nucleates anywhere in the 120-step run.
refuted.objection: Bingham is a magnetite-series, Au-rich Cu porphyry whose potassic core is defined by abundant hydrothermal magnetite (the district's strong magnetic anomaly is diagnostic); the magnetite ± K-feldspar ± biotite assemblage is THE marker of potassic alteration. A total magnetite no-show removes a phase that helps define this deposit type — and this is not an unmodeled-species excuse, because the sim has a magnetite engine.
refuted.evidence: js/37-supersat-oxide.ts magnetite gate: T sweet-spot 300–600°C (the 400°C start sits in it), but requires Fe≥25 and O2 in 0.1–1.0 with a redox-tent centered ~0.4–1.5. Broth Fe=30 (fe_f=0.5) and O2=0.2 drive the redox-tent low, so σ_magnetite stays under the 1.0 gate. Real potassic-core magnetite: Redmond & Einaudi Bingham studies; Ishihara magnetite-series granitoids.
refuted.growth_condition_lever: Raise early-stage Fe and place O2 in the magnetite window (~0.4–1.0) during the potassic stage — the potassic fluid is Fe-rich and moderately oxidized — pushing σ_magnetite over 1.0 so magnetite joins the early potassic assemblage instead of being absent.

refutation.reasoning:
The run models the Cu-Mo-Au SULFIDE ore stage, not the earlier potassic-magnetite stage. The scenario's own primary source (Landtwing et al. 2010) shows Bingham Cu-sulfides precipitate on cooling through ~425 to 350C; the run starts at 400C and cools to 310C, squarely in that window. The anomaly-defining hydrothermal magnetite forms earlier and hotter (>500-550C) in the K-silicate/potassic stage (Redmond & Einaudi, the reviewer's own citation, supports this two-stage structure). So a run faithfully modeling the ore stage sits paragenetically AFTER the bulk potassic magnetite; its absence is a correct consequence of the modeled T-window, not a contradiction of nature. Chemically coherent too: in the S-rich, sulfate-bearing cooling fluid (anhydrite grows), Fe partitions to pyrite + chalcopyrite (both grow, both in expects), not to Fe-oxide. Crucially the magnetite engine is NOT broken or globally too-strict: it fires in the jeffrey_mine serpentinization scenario (Fe=50, hyperalkaline, reducing), proving the porphyry no-show is a scenario-specific, growth-condition-controlled outcome. Verified under the active EH_DYNAMIC path: the magnetite window is Eh in [-75,+220]mV so the gate is even OPEN early, yet at Fe=30 (fe_f=0.5) the max achievable sigma over the whole run is ~0.5, half the 1.0 gate. That is exactly what the drift doctrine wants: behavior set by growth-condition levers (Fe budget + Eh tent), re-movable on purpose (raise Fe to >=60, seat early Eh at the ~75mV tent peak), no scoreboard patch. Does the defense survive? Yes. Bingham's magnetite facts are true, but the objection assumes magnetite must appear in THIS run; it need not, since the modeled window is the post-magnetite sulfide stage and magnetite is not in expects_species. Residual: the notes invoke the potassic core and feldspar grows, so a trace early-stage magnetite pulse is a legitimate future enrichment, hence low not none, but it is a nicety, not a nature-contradiction, so the objection as stated does not stand.</parameter>
</invoke>

refuted.claim: dioptase nucleates at step 264 (during the silica seep) as a member of the Bisbee oxide-zone assemblage.
refuted.objection: Dioptase is a diagnostic arid-oxide-zone Cu cyclosilicate of Tsumeb / Congo-Renéville / Altyn-Tyube, requiring an Al-poor, high-silica-activity, near-neutral-to-alkaline regime. Bisbee's Cu-silicate is chrysocolla, not dioptase. Dioptase is firing on the very same silica seep that should make chrysocolla, so the sim is confabulating a Tsumeb species onto a Bisbee broth.
refuted.evidence: Web/mindat check: Arizona dioptase is documented at the Christmas Mine (Banner district, Gila Co.) and the Ray Mine (Pinal Co.) — NOT the Warren/Bisbee district, whose 325+ species suite is chrysocolla-dominated for Cu-silicates. Dioptase (Cu6Si6O18·6H2O) is Al-free and forms in specific arid, alkaline, high-silica-activity settings unlike Bisbee's limestone-buffered ~pH 6.5 monsoon.
refuted.growth_condition_lever: Add a composition/pH gate to dioptase nucleation: require Al below a low threshold and pH above what a limestone buffer reaches. Bisbee carries Al=20 and buffers near pH 6.5, so Cu+Si should partition into (Al-bearing, hydrous) chrysocolla and leave crystalline dioptase dormant — the same silica-seep chemistry that legitimately makes chrysocolla then no longer mis-fires dioptase.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest defense of the sim, and it survives clean. The objection's load-bearing factual premise — "Bisbee's Cu-silicate is chrysocolla, not dioptase" and "dioptase is documented at Christmas/Ray, NOT the Warren/Bisbee district" — is false. Dioptase (Cu6Si6O18·6H2O) is on the official Bisbee species roster (bisbeeminingandminerals.com), and Bisbee is precisely the kind of rich Cu-silicate locality where it belongs: it is the TYPE LOCALITY for shattuckite (Schaller 1915, Shattuck Mine) and bisbeeite (which Schaller noted is compositionally "identical to dioptase"). Multiple sources list dioptase among the minerals associated with Bisbee shattuckite/plancheite (malachite, chrysocolla, dioptase, quartz, azurite, cuprite, plancheite, calcite, cerussite). So dioptase genuinely IS a rare member of the Bisbee oxide-zone assemblage — exactly what the claim card asserts. The reviewer's mindat check was incomplete; Christmas/Ray are additional AZ localities, not an exclusive list.

The "firing on the very same silica seep that should make chrysocolla" complaint actually vindicates the sim rather than damning it: at Bisbee the porphyry-derived silica seep is the very fluid that produced BOTH abundant chrysocolla (Al-bearing, amorphous) AND the rare crystalline Al-free Cu-cyclosilicates (dioptase/shattuckite/plancheite). The sim produces 3 chrysocolla events (dominant) against a single dioptase crystal (1 event, rare accessory) — the correct rarity proportion. The discriminator is real and encoded as a growth-condition lever, not a scoreboard patch: dioptase's gate requires a carbonate-poor micro-fluid (scenario delivers CO3 ≤ 50 via the deepened co2_drop, v204) with high silica activity (seep +90 → 100+ ppm SiO2), Al-free, at pH 6.5 (near-neutral) — a defensible dioptase field. The v204 comments show the authors moved CO3 on purpose to let this micro-environment fire, satisfying the boss drift doctrine (causal control, not baseline editing).

The only residual nuance a reviewer could fairly raise is completeness/ordering: shattuckite is the more characteristic Bisbee type-mineral and did not fire in this seed, so dioptase appearing solo is slightly ahead of its more-expected sibling. But that is an under-production of shattuckite, not a wrong-species insertion — the challenged claim (dioptase as a member of the Bisbee assemblage) is correct. The objection does not survive.

refuted.claim: sylvite (step 311) and halite (step 320) crystallize as late oxide-zone phases, at concentration 2.99× and fixed salinity 29.921 psu (~3× seawater).
refuted.objection: Sylvite (KCl) is a bittern-stage potash evaporite that precipitates only AFTER halite from extreme evaporation of a saline basin — there is no potash-evaporite source in a copper oxidation vug. And thermodynamically neither can form here: halite saturates at roughly 10× seawater concentration and sylvite far beyond that, but the run only reaches 2.99×. The sim precipitates rock-salt and potash minerals at a fraction of their saturation concentration.
refuted.evidence: Sylvite and halite are the essential minerals of bedded evaporite (salt) deposits, precipitated from saline waters in arid basins, with sylvite occurring in the late bittern stage after halite (Britannica 'Sylvite'; ScienceDirect 'Sylvite'/'Halite'). Seawater (~35 g/L) must be concentrated ~10× before halite saturates and much further for sylvite; the strip's concentration maxes at 2.992× at a fixed 29.9 psu. Bisbee's oxide zone is a Cu-carbonate/silicate/sulfate assemblage, not an evaporite.
refuted.growth_condition_lever: Raise the halite/sylvite nucleation concentration thresholds so they cannot fire at ~3× (halite ≥ ~10×, sylvite a post-halite bittern stage). If a genuine drying efflorescence is desired, drive the bisbee_final_drying event's concentration multiplier far higher AND/OR let salinity climb from its frozen 29.9 psu — but keep sylvite gated out entirely, since no K-evaporite parent exists in this system.

refutation.reasoning: Strongest defense of the sim, and it holds. (1) The reviewer's headline thermodynamic argument — "halite needs ~10x seawater, sylvite far beyond, run only hits 2.99x" — is a scale category-error. In the sim, salinity is pinned at 29.921 psu while `concentration` independently triples to 2.992x at the step-305 `final_drying` event; the two are decoupled internal variables, so "concentration x" is a dimensionless evaporation multiplier on the scenario's baseline broth, NOT a multiple of seawater (baseline Na is 142 ppm vs seawater's ~10,800 ppm — a compressed phenomenological ppm scale). Saturation in the engine is the SI product crossing sigma_crit=1.0. I recomputed from the strip + js/33: after final_drying SI_halite ~= 10 and SI_sylvite ~= 1.8, both genuinely supersaturated in the sim's own bookkeeping, and halite's quadratic-in-c form is explicitly anchored to "fires when a vadose concentration spike kicks in" — which final_drying ("fractures seal, system locks") literally is. So "precipitates at a fraction of saturation" is false in-model. (2) Halite is accurate-to-life: web-confirmed that halite forms as an efflorescent secondary salt in arid Cu-supergene oxidation zones, mine caves and tailings; atacamite (grown in this same run) is the diagnostic phase of that arid Cu-chloride regime; Bisbee is Sonoran-Desert-arid and its mine caves host soluble efflorescences (chalcanthite, Onac 2025). The reviewer's "halite = bedded evaporite basins only" framing is too narrow. (3) Sylvite is the only real residual: it is uncharacteristic and probably undocumented at Bisbee specifically — BUT efflorescent sylvite is documented (SEM cubic + lath habit in salt efflorescences, not strictly bedded bittern), the residual porphyry brine is K-enriched from K-feldspar weathering, and a vug drying to near-dryness can deposit a trace K-salt. Both minerals are flagged as SURPRISES, not claimed in expects_species, so the sim never asserts them as characteristic Bisbee phases. The sylvite-before-halite step order (311 vs 320) is stochastic nucleation-dice timing on two independent gates, not a paragenetic-sequence claim, so "wrong order" doesn't land either. The objection as posed — both thermodynamically impossible + sourceless, medium severity — does not survive: the physics claim is a scale error, halite is defensibly correct, sylvite is a defensible-if-uncharacteristic trace. Honest residual (low, not medium): the halite gate sits near-threshold at cold baseline (SI ~1.0 at c~1) because the broth carries Na/Cl loading for atacamite/mimetite/turquoise, so a modest 3x drying trips it harder than the engine's "dormant at c=1" design intent, and sylvite-at-Bisbee is a stretch — both addressable via growth-condition levers (cap the final_drying ramp, trim baseline Na/K, or raise the halide sigma_crit), never scoreboard-editing.

refuted.claim: Surprise species albite (NaAlSi3O8) nucleated at step 0 in ring 1 — one of only three grown species — in a scenario whose stated thesis is that alkalis are kept LOW so Al goes to andalusite and it is explicitly 'NOT a pegmatite'.
refuted.objection: The scenario's whole diagnostic premise (author's note) is alkali-poverty: Al must NOT be locked into feldspar/tourmaline/mica, so andalusite is the star. Yet Na=10 sits exactly on the albite engine's floor (`if (Na < 10) return 0` does not bail at 10), so a sodic feldspar nucleates at sigma=1.13 in the innermost ring — and the sim's albite is coded as a pegmatite/perthite phase ('classic perthite association — albite on feldspar host'), importing pegmatite texture into a rock defined as not-a-pegmatite. Compounding it, pure end-member albite is a LOW-grade plagioclase: across the peristerite gap the stable plagioclase at a ~600C hornfels peak is oligoclase/andesine, not Na-albite, so albite specifically is grade-inappropriate even though minor intermediate plagioclase would be fine.
refuted.evidence: Verified: supersaturation_albite (js/39:539) gate `Na < 10 || Al < 3 || SiO2 < 200` with broth Na=10 (scenarios.json5:806) passes -> sigma=(10/35)*(45/10)*(350/400)=1.13 > crit 1.0; nucleation_events shows albite at step 0, ring 1. Substrate code js/89:72-73 ('classic perthite association') carries pegmatite semantics. Petrology: peristerite solvus separates albite (An~0-2, sub-greenschist/greenschist) from oligoclase; hornfels-grade plagioclase is intermediate, so pure albite at 600C is off-grade. K-feldspar itself is fine here (muscovite-out reaction musc+qz -> Kfs + Al2SiO5 at ~600C low P), so only the Na phase is the problem.
refuted.growth_condition_lever: Drop the broth Na below the albite floor (e.g. Na=5-8 instead of 10). supersaturation_albite then returns 0 and albite is suppressed, matching the alkali-poor / not-a-pegmatite thesis, while the legitimate K-feldspar (K=15) still fires. Pure broth-Na lever, not deleting the species from the baseline.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest case FOR the objection: at a ~600 °C hornfels peak the peristerite solvus makes the equilibrium plagioclase oligoclase/andesine (An17–30), not An~0 albite, so labelling the sodic phase "albite" is nominally too Na-rich; and the substrate comment "classic perthite association — albite on feldspar host" (js/89:72-73) carries pegmatite flavor into a rock the author calls "not a pegmatite."

Why that does not survive:

1) Metapelitic andalusite hornfels genuinely HOSTS sodic plagioclase. The textbook assemblage is andalusite + quartz + muscovite/biotite + plagioclase + K-feldspar ± cordierite. The reviewer even concedes "minor intermediate plagioclase would be fine." So the presence of a sodic feldspar is CORRECT, not a nature-contradiction.

2) Granularity, not error. The catalog has exactly two feldspar species — K-feldspar ("feldspar") and Na-feldspar ("albite"); there is no oligoclase species to nucleate. "albite" is therefore the sim's ONLY available representative of the rock's sodic plagioclase, exactly the proxy logic the scenario already invokes (wall 'pegmatite' = "inert aluminous-silicate proxy", matrix "hornfels" = render skin). Suppressing it would delete a phase the rock really has — the drift-doctrine anti-pattern (hide a species by patching a gate/broth), and strictly LESS accurate-to-life.

3) The trajectory actually validates albite specifically. The run cools 599 → 422 °C. The lowest-grade contact facies is the albite–epidote hornfels facies (~400–500 °C), where the stable plagioclase IS albite (An<~5). The peristerite argument bites at the PEAK; a late, lower-T sodic plagioclase in a cooling aureole is defensibly albitic — so "albite" is not even off-grade for the temperatures the sim ran.

4) The thesis is upheld, not broken. "Not a pegmatite" is a claim about alkali ABUNDANCE relative to Al (alkalis too low to lock Al into feldspar, leaving excess Al → andalusite the star), not exclusion of all feldspar. Output: andalusite 5 events (dominant) vs feldspar 1 and albite 1 (marginal, capped). Minor feldspars coexisting under dominant andalusite is precisely the alkali-poor-metasediment signature. K-feldspar is ALREADY in expects_species — the author accepts a feldspar here — so a co-existing minor sodic feldspar is consistent, and plagioclase is arguably MORE expected than Kfs (Kfs needs muscovite-out; plagioclase is near-ubiquitous).

5) Good causal control. Why albite appears: low-but-nonzero Na(10) + high Al(45) + high SiO2(350) → σ=1.13, barely over crit 1.0, so albite is MINOR by design. The low Na correctly keeps it a trace phase; pegmatite-level Na would make it proliferate. That is the growth-condition lever working as intended. And no pegmatite texture is imported — cleavelandite_platy needs high σ, so at σ=1.13 albite renders tabular-on-wall.

Residual: the nomenclatural gap (calling An~0 what a petrologist would call oligoclase at peak) is real but is a species-granularity limitation, not a growth-condition contradiction, and it is mitigated by both the cooling into albite-facies T and the sim's binary feldspar granularity. The "medium-severity contradiction with nature" framing fails; at most this is a low-severity labeling nit whose only proposed fix (raise the gate / drop Na below 10 to hide it) is exactly what the doctrine forbids.

refuted.claim: galena (PbS) and molybdenite (MoS2) nucleate together at step 54 as fresh growth events
refuted.objection: Both are primary hypogene sulfides, not supergene precipitates. Molybdenite is a deep (~800 m level), high-temperature phase — its own record tags it porphyry-only, T_range 150-600 C — and it is the SOURCE of the Mo that already crystallized as wulfenite (step 0), ferrimolybdite (step 3) and powellite (step 12). Growing the source sulfide AFTER its own oxidation products is a paragenesis inversion no real profile allows. Galena is 'practically insoluble' and does survive as relict cores in the gossan, but it is armored relict, not a phase meteoric water re-precipitates. Nucleating MoS2 at 24 C also violates its 150-600 C floor by an order of magnitude — a leaked gate.
refuted.evidence: Web (le-comptoir-geologique / aetherfineminerals): molybdenite + tungstenite + Ge minerals are 30th-level (~800 m) deep hypogene phases associated with the rare light-green sphalerite; galena is 'practically insoluble' in the oxidation zone (survives, not re-precipitated). minerals.json molybdenite: class sulfide, scenarios [porphyry] ONLY, T_range_C [150,600]; galena: T_range_C [50,450], scenarios [mvt, porphyry, radioactive_pegmatite]. Card: molybdenite/galena first-step 54, environment T there ~25 C, Eh dropping through ~250 mV.
refuted.growth_condition_lever: Same Eh-floor lever as the sphalerite finding — the late block (step 54: galena/molybdenite/chalcocite/covellite/cuprite) fires only because Eh has fallen into the sulfide field. Clamp the Eh floor so the gossan cell never crosses the sulfide/sulfate boundary. Additionally enforce the per-mineral T_range gate (a nucleation-gate-threshold lever, explicitly sanctioned by the drift doctrine): a 150-600 C porphyry sulfide must not nucleate in a 24 C weathering broth. If the intent is to depict Mo protore being weathered, model it as a relict/armored source that feeds Mo to the oxidized phases, not as a fresh nucleation event.

refutation.reasoning:
STRONGEST DEFENSE (and it holds): The empirical seed-42 run shows step 54 is the supergene_cu_enrichment (cementation) event — O2 collapses 2.0→0.6, Eh 340→131 mV, S climbs to ~100. In steps 50-53 (oxidizing) galena and molybdenite sigma are both exactly 0; the redox gate opens ONLY when the reducing pulse lands. The co-nucleating cohort is galena+molybdenite+chalcocite+covellite+cuprite — the classic supergene enrichment/cementation blanket: chalcocite(4) and covellite(4) are the headline secondary Cu sulfides, cuprite the supergene Cu2O, galena(1)+molybdenite(1) the trace tail. The reviewer entirely missed this — they read the whole cohort as "primary hypogene sulfides growing" when the sim is faithfully modeling secondary-sulfide precipitation where descending metal-laden oxidized solution hits the reducing zone below the water table. That is exactly what a cementation zone does.

Three of the objection's four pillars fail: (1) "leaked gate at 24C violating a 150-600C floor" misreads the architecture — scenarios[] and T_range_C are catalog METADATA, not runtime gates; the runtime gate is sulfideRedoxAnoxic (O2/Eh) + chemistry minima, and molybdenite's low-T disfavor is a smooth decay (0.287x at 25C), not a hard floor. (2) "galena is armored relict, not re-precipitated" is too strong — secondary/supergene galena re-precipitating in a reducing, S-rich, Pb-rich (Pb=100, Cl-mobilized as at arid Tsumeb) cementation blanket is documented; sigma here is a robust 1.96. (3) "paragenesis inversion no real profile allows" dissolves once you read the sim's causal model: Mo is a DISSOLVED reservoir (broth Mo, bumped by the pb_mo_pulse whose text is an "oxidizing galena+molybdenite lens" UPSLOPE), so the vug's molybdates form from that molybdate under oxidation and the step-54 MoS2 forms from the SAME dissolved reservoir when redox flips — it is not the primary source mineral growing after its own products. Redox oscillation across the water-table transition (oxidize → re-reduce), precipitating MoS2 from molybdate, is real: it is precisely how jordisite (supergene amorphous MoS2) forms.

RESIDUAL (why not severity none): the one imperfection that survives is a species-RESOLUTION nicety, not a nature-contradiction. The cold reducing supergene MoS2 is more precisely jordisite (amorphous MoS2) than crystalline 2H/3R molybdenite; the sim has no jordisite engine so the reducing-pulse MoS2 gets the molybdenite label, and it clears only at a knife-edge sigma ~1.0. This is cleanly actionable via a GROWTH-CONDITION lever (steepen molybdenite's low-T decay so it cannot clear at 25C, and/or add a jordisite engine for the supergene niche) — the doctrine-approved kind of fix, never a scoreboard edit. But it does not make the sim contradict nature; it models a genuine, well-understood phenomenon the reviewer overlooked. The high-severity objection as framed fails.

refuted.claim: chalcocite (Cu2S) + covellite (CuS) co-grow at step 54 inside the same vug as the fully oxidized gossan suite (goethite, jarosite, brochantite, scorodite, anglesite)
refuted.objection: Chalcocite-covellite are the supergene ENRICHMENT blanket — they form below the water table in a reducing environment, as descending Cu re-precipitates on primary sulfide. They are mutually exclusive by redox with the coexisting Fe(III)/sulfate oxidized assemblage (jarosite, goethite, brochantite) they are intergrown with here. At Tsumeb these are vertically separated zones, yet the anchor explicitly calls this the '1st-stage gossan (uppermost supergene zone).' Co-locating the enrichment blanket in the uppermost oxidation zone is a co-occurrence/zonation violation. (Cuprite, Cu(I) oxide, at the same step is fine — it legitimately forms near the reducing front WITHIN the oxidation zone, so the sim gets the transitional phase right; it is the Cu2S/CuS sulfides that are misplaced.)
refuted.evidence: Standard supergene model (USGS SIR 2010-5070-C ch.12; Springer 'Gossans: In Situ Supergene Base Metal Deposits'): chalcocite/covellite enrichment blanket sits below the oxidized gossan at/under the water table, reducing side of the redox front. scenarios.json5 broth comment self-documents the intent ('Cu 25->55: enough to feed chalcocite/bornite at supergene enrichment zones') — a tuning note, not a citation-backed defense of intergrowth with the oxidized suite. The card's own Eh min 129 mV shows the cell dips into the enrichment/reducing regime.
refuted.growth_condition_lever: The single falling-Eh movement (pH clampMin 3.5, Eh 355->129 mV) sweeps one cell through the whole vertical redox column, so a single vug records gossan + enrichment blanket + protore at once. Cap the Eh drop so the modeled cell stays in the oxidized zone the anchor claims. If the author wants the enrichment blanket, give it its own deeper, reducing sub-zone scenario (or a gated deep-zone event with lowered Eh/O2), rather than letting the uppermost-gossan cell drift reducing — that keeps causal control per the doctrine: you move the assemblage by moving the Eh window, not by editing the scoreboard.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest defense of the SIM, and it holds. The objection's load-bearing claim is that chalcocite/covellite are "mutually exclusive by redox with the coexisting Fe(III)/sulfate oxidized assemblage they are intergrown with here" — i.e. simultaneous intergrowth under one redox state. The mechanism shows that is not what the sim does.

Evidence from the v226 strip nucleation record + engine gates:
- Oxidized gossan suite nucleates steps 0–37 in the oxidizing acid window (O2 1.5–2.2): goethite@0, jarosite@4, scorodite@7–18, brochantite@2–17, alunite@15, ferrimolybdite@3–37, anglesite@0–4.
- The reducing Cu-sulfides (chalcocite, covellite, cuprite, galena, molybdenite) nucleate in a SEPARATE later window, steps 54–65, driven by event_supergene_cu_enrichment (step 55) which deliberately sets fluid.O2 = 0.6 and injects Cu+50/S+30/Fe+10. Its narrator is explicit and textbook-correct: descending Cu2+ meets the reducing enrichment blanket, ~5–10× ore enrichment.
- Redox gates are enforced both ways: chalcocite/covellite have hard sulfideRedoxAnoxic gates (O2 <= 1.9/2.0), while jarosite/scorodite/brochantite gate on sulfateRedoxAvailable/arsenateRedoxAvailable (oxidizing side + As(V)). At the O2=0.6 pulse the oxidized engines return 0, so those phases are RELICT, not co-growing. There is no single step at which a reducing sulfide precipitates in an oxidizing fluid — the sim respects redox exclusivity instant-by-instant.

So the objection misreads a time-separated redox oscillation as simultaneous intergrowth. The residual is only a zonation/anchoring nuance: an enrichment-blanket pulse inside a vug anchored "uppermost gossan." That survives on its merits: (1) vugg's core conceit compresses a deposit's vertical zonation into one pocket's temporal fluid trajectory — the Bisbee sibling does the identical thing with an explicit bisbee_enrichment_blanket event; (2) time-separated mixed-redox assemblages in one hand specimen are real via water-table oscillation, and Tsumeb specifically underwent multiple oxidation cycles (three supergene zones, Mesozoic–Cenozoic uplift); (3) the reviewer concedes cuprite (Cu(I) front) is legitimate at the very same reducing pulse, and chalcocite/covellite are the immediately adjacent, slightly-more-reducing microenvironments the narrator places on dissolving sulfide relics; (4) covellite/chalcocite are documented supergene phases at Tsumeb and commonly occur as oxidation-zone coatings; (5) drift-doctrine: these are fully causally controlled by a growth-condition lever (the O2-drop + Cu/S enrichment event) with hard anoxic gates — remove the event and they vanish; they are NOT in expects_species, so nothing is being propped up on the scoreboard. The fix the reviewer implies (they don't belong) would require deleting a scientifically-grounded, explicitly-narrated real process, not correcting a physics error.

The objection does not survive: the redox chemistry is correct, the co-occurrence is a genuine telescoped/overprint texture produced by a real, controllable process.

refuted.claim: K-feldspar ('feldspar', 2 events) and albite (2 events) precipitate as the earliest vein phases at step 0 (before quartz at step 4), and pH holds ~6.5 through the F-metasomatic topaz stage (topaz first at step 34).
refuted.objection: An F-rich topaz (greisen-type) vein is feldspar-DESTRUCTIVE: H+/HF metasomatism converts feldspar to topaz + quartz + sericite/kaolinite — exactly the sericitized/kaolinized alteration halo observed at Ouro Preto. Precipitating vein feldspar in that setting, and holding a near-neutral pH (~6.5; neutrality at 360°C is ~5.7, so this reads mildly alkaline) straight through topaz growth, contradicts the acidic, feldspar-consuming regime that the topaz assemblage itself requires. Feldspar preceding even quartz compounds the oddity.
refuted.evidence: Morteani et al. 2002 (the card's cited source) — Ouro Preto imperial topaz sits in hydrothermally sericitized/kaolinized (feldspar-destroyed) phyllite; the greisenization reaction feldspar + HF(aq) -> topaz + quartz + mica is standard economic-geology. Both feldspars appear in the card's own 'Surprises' list; the strip shows pH pinned at 6.504–6.614 for almost the whole run with only a brief dip to 5.567.
refuted.growth_condition_lever: pH-trajectory lever: drive pH into the acidic band (~4–5) during and after the step-35 F-pulse (HF dissociation / H+ metasomatism), making the regime feldspar-destructive. That both matches greisen chemistry and suppresses vein feldspar/albite precipitation, routing the audit-gap-fill Na=60/K=40 into sericite instead of feldspar — a broth-pH change, not editing feldspar out of the baseline.

refutation.reasoning: Strongest defense — and it holds. The objection applies a generic acidic-greisen, feldspar-destructive template, but the deposit-specific literature (including the card's own cited source) describes the opposite regime. Olsen (1972, Econ. Geol., "Origin of Topaz Deposits near Ouro Preto") explicitly states "feldspar produced concurrently with and slightly after the topaz" from later F-depleted solutions, plus "orthoclase which was later altered to kaolin" — i.e., hydrothermal feldspar (incl. K-feldspar) IS a real phase at Ouro Preto, its stability governed by F-activity (topaz when F high, feldspar when F low). Morteani et al. 2002 ("Variant B," the card's anchor) models crystallization at ~360 °C from NEAR-NEUTRAL metamorphic brines — the explicit non-greisen model — and much of the kaolinite/sericite is a late/supergene weathering overprint, not evidence the primary topaz-forming fluid was strongly acidic. At 360 °C neutral pH is ~5.7, so 6.5 is only ~0.8 units above neutral: unremarkable for a metamorphic brine and compatible with sericite/muscovite alteration. The counter-source claiming "absence of feldspar" refers specifically to PEGMATITIC feldspar (an argument against a pegmatite origin), not hydrothermal feldspar, so it does not conflict with Olsen. Mechanically the sim (supersaturation_feldspar/albite in js/39-supersat-silicate.ts, gated on K/Na/Al/SiO2 with acid penalties only below pH 4/3) precipitates feldspar+albite at step 0 during the DESIGNED F-poor interval (F=12, below the 20-ppm topaz threshold) — which is exactly the F-activity control (feldspar stable when F is low) that the reviewer missed, then topaz after the F-pulse. So the central claims — "vein feldspar shouldn't be there" and "pH must be acidic" — are both refuted by the primary literature. What survives is minor and non-contradictory: albite/Na-feldspar is on weaker footing than K-feldspar; feldspar leads quartz by ~4 steps; and the sim never fires the documented late orthoclase→kaolin overprint (its kaolinization branch needs pH<4.5, but pH floors at 5.567), so it misses a late alteration feature. These are incompletenesses/refinements, not a contradiction with nature — a growth-condition lever (a late acidic/F-depleted pulse to kaolinize some feldspar) would improve fidelity but is not required to make the current output defensible. The objection does not survive at medium; the sim is defensibly modeling a real, literature-documented F-controlled feldspar↔topaz paragenesis.

refuted.claim: uranophane [Ca(UO2)2(SiO3)2(OH)2·5H2O] grows as a co-equal primary phase — 3 events, first_step 0 — alongside the two diagnostic vanadates in a scenario anchored to the Uravan carnotite type locality.
refuted.objection: The Uravan Mineral Belt is a vanadium-EXCESS ore (V:U from 3:1 to 10:1, averaging 5:1). That vanadium surplus is precisely why dissolved uranyl is quantitatively fixed as the insoluble vanadates carnotite/tyuyamunite; uranophane (a V-FREE uranyl silicate) is not a characteristic Uravan secondary and is absent from the USGS oxidized-vanadiferous ore inventory. The sim broth inverts the ratio — V=12 < U=15 (V:U≈0.8) — so it carries 'orphan' uranyl the vanadate pathway can't capture, and because uranophane's nucleation gate has NO vanadium term, that excess uranyl precipitates as uranophane in the silica-saturated (SiO2=200) sandstone broth. This over-represents a species real V-rich Uravan ore suppresses.
refuted.evidence: USGS TEI-583 'Summary of the mineralogy of the Colorado Plateau uranium ores' — in oxidized vanadiferous ores U is fixed in carnotite and tyuyamunite; the vanadate/oxysalt list (tyuyamunite, meta-tyuyamunite, carnotite, hewettite, pascoite, rossite, metarossite, fervanite, hummerite) contains NO uranophane. V exceeds U 3:1–10:1 (avg 5:1) [USGS TEI-583; NMGS Uravan guidebook 32:165]. Sim broth (data/scenarios.json5): V=12, U=15. Uranophane gate (js/39-supersat-silicate.ts:877) requires only U≥0.5/Ca≥20/SiO2≥30 and is blind to V.
refuted.growth_condition_lever: Raise broth V to the documented 3–10× U excess (V≈45–150 for U=15) so the vanadate engines are the dominant uranyl sink, AND add a vanadium-scarcity suppression term to supersaturation_uranophane() (route uranyl to vanadate when V is abundant). Both are causal levers — broth chemistry + a nucleation-gate threshold — not a scoreboard/expects_species edit; uranophane should then only fire in genuinely V-depleted micro-zones, matching Uravan.

refutation.reasoning: Strongest defense: (1) Uranophane is a REAL, documented secondary of the Uravan Mineral Belt / Colorado Plateau, not an absent phase. Mindat records it at the Opera Box Mine, Gypsum Valley, Montrose Co. (a Uravan-belt district), and — decisively — the reviewer's OWN source, USGS TEI-583, states uranophane + boltwoodite occur in corvusite ore, "forming locally where uranyl ions are present and where the vanadium has not reached the quinquevalent state or where the pH is too low for formation of carnotite." The reviewer inventoried only the fully-oxidized-vanadiferous ore subset and mistook its silence for deposit-wide exclusion. So the objection's central premise ("a species real V-rich Uravan ore suppresses," "absent from the deposit") is factually wrong at the belt scale. (2) The sim's chemistry is textbook-correct for the phase: Ca-dominant (100), silica-saturated sandstone (SiO2=200), oxidizing (O2=1.2), pH 7 (within 5-8), 22 °C (<50 °C); uranophane correctly beats its cation analogs boltwoodite/sklodowskite/kasolite because Ca dominates — the sim's own minerals.json lists "Colorado Plateau" as a uranophane type-area and encodes the Finch & Murakami stability field (oxidizing, low-CO3, low-P, low-S, Ca+Si). (3) It is subordinate and non-diagnostic: 3 events (least of the three U phases, vs tyuyamunite 5 + carnotite 4) and NOT in expects_species — flagged as an emergent "surprise," matching a minor/local secondary, not the "co-equal primary" the objection asserts. (4) The gates are independent standing-concentration functions with no mass balance, so the reviewer's "orphan uranyl the vanadate pathway can't capture" is a rationalization, not a sim mechanism; and because the uranophane gate is V-blind, raising V would NOT suppress it — the broth V:U ratio is causally inert for the challenged species and does not threaten the diagnostic vanadates (their v_fraction = 12/13 = 0.92, cation competition intact). Doctrine check: uranophane is already under real causal control (raise CO3>60 → uranyl-carbonate, drop O2 → coffinite, raise P → autunite all suppress it), and it is not in expects_species, so no scoreboard edit is even at issue. Does anything survive? Only a minor realism note: the broth V:U ≈ 0.8 is inverted vs the documented Uravan average (~5:1). That is a legitimate calibration nit and a V-competition term could be added to the uranophane gate to model the fully-oxidized carnotite zone, but it neither falsifies the model nor changes the challenged outcome. The high-severity charge — that the sim over-represents a species nature excludes — does not survive: nature hosts uranophane in these deposits, and the sim renders it as a chemically-appropriate, correctly-ranked, non-diagnostic minor phase.

refuted.claim: tincalconite (Na2B4O7.5H2O) is in expects_species but never nucleates (no-show)
refuted.objection: Tincalconite is a Searles type-mineral and the expected summer dehydration paramorph after borax; four summer_bake events (steps 60/120/180/240, T->55C) were explicitly built to fire the borax->tincalconite conversion, yet none produced it. The one diagnostic expected species tied to this locality's dehydration cascade is absent - this is not defended by the author's notes (the notes assert tincalconite IS covered), so it is an open no-show, not a settled scope gap.
refuted.evidence: Tincalconite is the metastable dehydration product of borax (borax loses ~5 H2O on heating/low humidity); Searles is its type locality. The mechanism (borax->tincalconite paramorph on summer baking) is real and the scenario author designed events for it; the card lists it as a no-show.
refuted.growth_condition_lever: Tune the summer_bake dehydration gate: verify T actually reaches the borax->tincalconite dehydration threshold at the bake step AND that borax standing crop is large enough to convert. Lower the dehydration humidity/T threshold or raise borax abundance before the bake so the paramorph gate clears. Growth-condition/event tuning, not adding it to the scoreboard.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest defense of the SIM — and it fully holds. The objection's core factual claim ("tincalconite is absent / never produced") is false. The committed canonical baseline tests-js/baselines/seed42_v226.json — same version, seed, and step count the card was generated from — records searles_lake as tincalconite: {active:8, total:8}. An independent trace probe I ran (SIM_VERSION 226, seed 42, 300 steps) reproduces this exactly: 8 tincalconite crystals, first appearing at step 128. Neither borax NOR mirabilite survives in the final census (both keys absent) because all 8 borax converted to tincalconite and all mirabilite converted to thenardite — the full Searles dehydration paramorph cascade fired.

Mechanism check (js/75-transitions.ts): tincalconite is paramorph-only (grow_tincalconite returns null; no nucleation gate, no supersaturation path). borax→tincalconite fires via applyDehydrationTransitions on either the heat path (T>=75C) or the vadose dry-exposure path (25 cumulative steps at concentration>=1.5). The heat path never fires because summer_bake only reaches ~53C, but the DRY path does: each summer_bake sets fluid_surface_ring=0.0, making every ring vadose, and the borax crystals' dry_exposure_steps accumulate across the summer windows, crossing 25 around step 128 — inside the second summer-bake dry-down. So the summer_bake events DID drive the conversion, via the evaporative-efflorescence path they set up. This is arguably the MORE physically correct route: natural borax→tincalconite at Searles is low-temperature surface efflorescence in dry air (the type-locality "chalky white pseudomorph" crust), not a 75C thermal decomposition. The reviewer's claim that "four summer_bake events were built to fire the conversion, yet none produced it" is simply wrong.

Why the card says "no-show": the review-card generator (tools/review-claim-card.mjs) computes the "present" set purely from strip.nucleation_events (paragenesis() reads only nucleation events; noShows = expects.filter(m => !present.has(m))). A paramorph product that forms by an in-place mineral-field flip — no nucleation event, and grow_tincalconite emits no GrowthZone — is structurally invisible to that tracker. Thenardite escapes the same fate only because it ALSO has a direct nucleation path (so it appears in nucleation_events); tincalconite has none, so the event-based tracker misses it even though 8 are in the specimen. The "no-show" is a tooling artifact, not a sim-vs-nature gap.

Does the defense survive? Yes, decisively. The sim models exactly the phenomenon the reviewer says is missing (Searles borax→tincalconite dehydration paramorph), it fires on the geologically-correct evaporative-dry-down lever, and it yields 8 tincalconite crystals in the canonical baseline. Nothing in the sim needs changing; per the drift doctrine no scoreboard/species-list edit is warranted. The only real (separate, out-of-scope) item is that the review-card generator should census final crystal minerals in addition to nucleation events so paramorph products stop being misreported as no-shows.

refuted.claim: low-Mg calcite grows as a primary phase (2 events) alongside aragonite, HMC and dolomite in a brine with molar Mg/Ca ≈ 5.8
refuted.objection: A molar Mg/Ca of ~5.8 is precisely the regime that favors aragonite + high-Mg calcite + dolomite and strongly POISONS low-Mg calcite growth (Mg2+ adsorbs to calcite steps and stalls them). The primary hypersaline sabkha/Coorong carbonate assemblage is aragonite + HMC (± dolomite); ordinary low-Mg calcite is a later meteoric/burial diagenetic product, not a syn-brine primary phase. Growing LMC directly from this Mg-rich brine mildly contradicts the very high Mg/Ca that the scenario relies on to make the dolomite and aragonite.
refuted.evidence: Morse & Mackenzie carbonate geochemistry; the well-known Mg/Ca control on CaCO3 polymorph (high Mg/Ca → aragonite, Mg-poisoning of calcite). The sim's own dolomite description notes dolomite 'competes with calcite (low Mg)' — i.e. calcite is the low-Mg branch, which this brine is not. Broth: Mg 1400 / Ca 400 → molar Mg/Ca 5.77 (computed).
refuted.growth_condition_lever: Mg/Ca-gate calcite nucleation: above a Mg/Ca threshold (say >~2 molar) suppress low-Mg calcite and route the CaCO3 supersaturation to aragonite/HMC (which already fire correctly). The lever is the existing broth Mg/Ca ratio driving a nucleation inhibition on calcite, not editing the grown record.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest defense of the sim, and it survives. The reviewer's geochemistry is correct in the abstract — at molar Mg/Ca ~5.8 the primary hypersaline assemblage is aragonite + HMC ± dolomite, and Mg2+ stalls low-Mg-calcite {10-14} growth steps — but the objection mischaracterizes what the sim actually does with it.

(1) The sim implements the EXACT mechanism the reviewer cites. The reviewer's own evidence is "Mg2+ adsorbs to calcite STEPS and stalls them" — a growth/step-inhibition mechanism, not a nucleation prohibition. The sim applies mgPoisoningFactor (js/52b calciteRate) to the calcite growth rate, yielding 0.19 (81% step-growth suppression) at this brine. That is the textbook (Davis 2000 / Nielsen 2013) locus of Mg poisoning, applied faithfully.

(2) The observable output honors the Mg/Ca control. In seed42_v226 the calcite ends at max 0.5 um (2 crystals) — the SMALLEST carbonate, below HMC's own 0.4-um cluster and far below dolomite (1.5), aragonite (7.7). The carbonate hierarchy the sim produces IS aragonite/HMC/dolomite-dominant with LMC an arrested sub-optical trace — precisely nature's Mg-poisoning prediction. "Grows as a primary phase alongside aragonite/HMC/dolomite" is not true: 0.5 um is a poisoned, arrested nucleus, not a phase; it is ~5 orders of magnitude below the dominant selenite (87.8 mm) and invisible at any specimen scale. Calcite is also absent from expects_species, so no phase claim was ever made.

(3) The physical picture the sim renders — a calcite critical nucleus that forms but cannot propagate steps because Mg stalls them, freezing it at sub-micron size — is a MORE mechanistically faithful representation of "Mg poisons calcite steps" than a crude delete-the-species nucleation gate would be. It reproduces the correct end-state (essentially no LMC) via the correct cause (step-growth inhibition), which is exactly the drift-doctrine ideal: the Mg-rich broth chemistry is the lever that controls the outcome, and it demonstrably fires.

The one residual a purist could raise: the calcite NUCLEATION gate reads raw thermodynamic omega (SI_calcite up to 2.77) with no Mg penalty, so 2 sub-micron nuclei appear where a strict nucleation-suppression model would allow zero. That is a real but negligible fidelity fine-point — a doctrine-compliant refinement (fold mgPoisoningFactor into the calcite nucleation gate) exists but would change nothing observable, since 0.5 um is already indistinguishable from absent, and trace LMC in the micrite fraction of dolomitizing muds is itself defensible. This does not rise to a contradiction with nature. The medium-severity objection does not survive; the sim is defensibly right.

refuted.claim: pH rises monotonically 1.874 → 6.559 (max 6.559) over the run, while the card states the H2SO4 byproduct 'keeps fluid pH at 2–4'
refuted.objection: An acid-sulfate hot spring is buffered acidic by continuous H2SO4 generation from sulfide oxidation; drifting to near-neutral 6.5 abandons the acid-sulfate character that defines this mixing-zone environment and contradicts the cited White & Roberson pH range. It also creates late-run tension with the grown minerals: native sulfur's own gate is pH ≤ 5 (violated by end), and realgar/orpiment solubility rises sharply with pH via thioarsenite/thioarsenate complexing, so a 6.5 tail would tend to redissolve the As-sulfides rather than preserve them. They survive here only because they all nucleated at step 0 while pH was still ~1.9 — the environment arc no longer matches the assemblage it carries.
refuted.evidence: Card's own Deposit note: 'The H2SO4 byproduct keeps fluid pH at 2-4.' Environment trajectory shows pH 1.874 → 6.559 with max 6.559; native_sulfur engine gate is pH ≤ 5 per the author's Engine-fit note. Acid-sulfate character per White & Roberson 1962.
refuted.growth_condition_lever: Sustain the acid buffer across the run — keep the H2SO4-generation / concentration driver active (or cap the neutralization drift) so pH holds in the 2–4 band instead of climbing to 6.5. If a rising-pH tail is desired it should be modeled as an explicit waning-vent / dilution event, not an unforced monotonic creep, so the pH curve stays causally tied to a stated condition.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest form of the objection: the card's own Deposit note says H2SO4 "keeps fluid pH at 2-4," yet the sim reports pH up to 6.559 — a literal internal contradiction, plus stated tension with a pH-≤5 native_sulfur gate and As-sulfide thioarsenite redissolution. That is the best case, and it does not survive.

Four defenses dismantle it. (1) "Monotonic" is factually false. The per-step trace (archive/strips/v226/sulphur_bank.json) is a sawtooth: pH sits 1.5-2.7 for the first ~30 steps, min 1.543, with repeated re-acidification from the H2S-recharge (-1.0) and surface-oxidation (-0.5) events; it only drifts toward neutral during quiescent inter-pulse windows. The first→last [min,max] summary hid the oscillation.

(2) The near-neutral tail is faithful to the CITED source, which the reviewer inverted. White & Roberson 1962 (confirmed by search) states Sulphur Bank's deep ore-forming waters are NEARLY NEUTRAL and the pH-≤1 acid is a shallow steam-heated overprint ABOVE the water table; the principal ore bodies (cinnabar, marcasite, pyrite, quartz — exactly this scenario's grown assemblage) sit AT AND BELOW the water table in near-neutral fluid. A fluid that traverses acid-sulfate vent (early native-sulfur synproportionation) → near-neutral ore zone (cinnabar/marcasite/pyrite) models the real dual-fluid deposit. The deposit is not purely acid-sulfate; "abandons the acid-sulfate character" mistakes the shallow leaching cap for the whole system.

(3) native_sulfur's gate is pH_max 6.5, NOT 5. js/36 documents "was 5; broadened for BSR mode (v80)" and a bimodal pH factor deliberately peaking at both 2.5 and 6.0. The card's "pH ≤ 5" engine-fit note is stale. Final pH 6.559 exceeds 6.5 by 0.06 (one recovery step overshooting the cap that coincides with the gate), and all native_sulfur nucleation occurred at pH < 2 — no growth is claimed at the tail.

(4) As-sulfide redissolution via thioarsenite complexing is an ALKALINE (pH 10-12) phenomenon per the solubility literature; appreciable dissolution of realgar needs pH ~12, orpiment ~10. The engine encodes exactly this (realgar pH_max 9, orpiment pH_max 9.5, note: "alkali dissolves it as thioarsenite complex"). At pH 6.5 both are stable in sim and nature; all As-sulfide nucleation here is at pH 1.5-4.6, and the brief 6.5 excursions never reach the dissolution window. The sim does not, and should not, redissolve them.

No expected species fails and no spurious species appears from the pH tail; the drift is causally controlled by event flow_rates/cadence/starting pH (a legitimate growth-condition lever, re-movable on purpose), not scoreboard-patching. The only surviving residue is documentary: the card's "pH 2-4" prose and its stale "pH ≤ 5" note are inconsistent with both the code and the deposit's near-neutral ore-fluid reality, warranting a one-line card correction — not a sim change.

refuted.claim: T trajectory 180.1 → 159.4 °C, held as a peak-Alleghenian burial plateau (v184 movement), for Herkimer double-terminated quartz.
refuted.objection: 180°C is above every direct estimate of Herkimer formation temperature. A geologist reads the water-clear, inclusion-poor, low-Ti (TitaniQ-hallmark) habit as the signature of SLOW, COOL growth; 180°C is a hydrothermal-vein temperature, not a burial-diagenetic quartz-pocket one. The card's own defense ('no widely-indexed microthermometry publishes Th for Herkimer quartz') is falsified: a fluid-inclusion temperature IS reported, and it is ~3× cooler than the sim runs.
refuted.evidence: Univ. of Waterloo 'Wat on Earth' (uwaterloo.ca/wat-on-earth) cites a fluid-inclusion study giving Herkimer quartz formation at ~51°C, ~1 km depth, ~400–500 Ma. Even accepting the author's/Wikipedia Carboniferous deep-burial timing, a Mohawk Valley section at ~3 km and a normal 25–30°C/km gradient yields only ~100–140°C; the ~180–200°C end of Friedman & Sanders 1982 is anchored in the CATSKILLS (SE NY, ~6.5 km burial), not the shallower northern-edge Mohawk Valley where Herkimer sits. Every independent line (FI Th, clarity, low Ti) clusters well below 180°C.
refuted.growth_condition_lever: Lower the PEAK of the v184 burial smoothstep from 180°C toward ~120–150°C (with the end-of-run endpoint dropping proportionally), i.e. a T-trajectory change in the movement's thermal budget — not a relabel of expects_species or the scoreboard. If the enhydro shrinkage-bubble constraint is what forces a floor above ~50°C, state that as the justification for landing near ~120°C rather than the FI-minimum ~51°C.

refutation.reasoning: Strongest defense mounted, and it survives. The objection's core is a "falsification": that a reported ~51°C fluid-inclusion Th proves the card wrong. That falsification is unsound. (1) The Waterloo "51°C" is not a rigorous published microthermometric Th for Herkimer pocket quartz — it is arithmetically 21°C surface + 1000 m ÷ 33 m/°C = 51.3°C, a normal-gradient depth back-calculation that PRESUMES a shallow (~1 km), early (400–500 Ma, Ordovician–Silurian) genetic model. The card and Wikipedia both adopt the DIFFERENT mainstream model: Carboniferous Alleghenian deep burial ("quartz interpreted to have formed during the Carboniferous… as sediment buried the rock and temperatures rose"). Under deep burial you get 100–200°C, so the card's "no rigorous Herkimer Th published" defense is essentially intact. (2) The reviewer's own diagnostic evidence defeats 51°C: anthraxolite — the diagnostic solid inclusion in Herkimer — is thermally cracked pyrobitumen that only forms once petroleum passes the top of the oil window (~140–160°C+). A ~51°C pocket cannot generate anthraxolite; solid anthraxolite coexisting with residual liquid petroleum brackets peak T at ~140–160°C+, INSIDE the card's cited 140–200°C window and ~3× hotter than the reviewer's figure. (3) The "144.2–180.5°C" range that kept surfacing is a red herring from a 2018 Econ. Geol. METHODS paper, not Herkimer; Wikipedia carries no Th range — so there is no confirmed direct estimate at 51°C either. Where the reviewer is genuinely right: 180°C is at the hot edge — in-situ at ~3–3.5 km with a cratonic-foreland gradient (25–30°C/km) you'd expect ~100–140°C, and the 180–200°C end of Friedman & Sanders is Catskills (5–7 km) burial, not the thin northern Mohawk Valley edge; sustained liquid-oil inclusions also mildly cap a hot plateau. But the sim does NOT sit at 180°C — it runs 180 → 159°C, spending most of the run at 159–175°C and ENDING at 159°C, with every value inside the literature-cited [140,200] window and near the anthraxolite/oil-window sweet spot. Species (quartz), paragenesis (one slow doubly-terminated crystal, García-Ruiz fewer-nuclei), and assemblage-consistency are all correct. The residual is a legitimate calibration nudge (center the plateau ~150–160°C via the movement's base — a pure growth-condition lever), not the objection's claimed nature-contradiction. The medium-severity "falsification" fails; the objection does not survive as stated.

refuted.claim: No-show: fluorite (and fluorapatite) never nucleate (fluorite_morph null all 70 steps; not in expects_species), even though the author's notes foreground a 'strong F accessory suite (topaz, green fluorite, fluorapatite)' as Shigar character and topaz IS grown.
refuted.objection: Green fluorite is a documented, characteristic accessory of the Shigar intermediate-zone gem vugs, EPMA-analyzed by the card's own anchor source (Agheem et al. 2014). The pocket fluid here carries Ca=17.7 ppm (nonzero, constant) and F rising to 48 ppm — an abundant late F budget that precipitates topaz but, inconsistently, no fluorite. Growing one member of the F-Ca suite while silently dropping its most diagnostic halide misrepresents the deposit's mineralogy; the calcite SI is deeply undersaturated only because DIC≈0 (no carbonate), not because Ca is absent, so there is no chemical reason fluorite cannot form.
refuted.evidence: Card cites Agheem et al. 2014 (EPMA on Shigar 'beryl/schorl/topaz/fluorite/apatite'). Web-confirmed: beryl, tourmaline, garnet, apatite, topaz and fluorite co-occur in the cavities/vugs of the intermediate zone of Shigar zoned pegmatites (Agheem 2014 abstract; mindat loc-5420; eighthdimensiongems). Rough CaF2 ion product at F=48/Ca=17.7 ppm sits well above surface Ksp, so the budget exists; strip shows fluorite_morph=null throughout and no fluorite nucleation_event.
refuted.growth_condition_lever: Raise the broth Ca budget (currently a thin 17.7 ppm) so the late high-F fluid crosses fluorite's nucleation SI and precipitates green fluorite in the F-suite window (steps ~47+), optionally with a dedicated late-F event; do NOT force fluorite by editing the scoreboard.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest defense of the objection, then whether it survives.

The objection's load-bearing claim is "there is no chemical reason fluorite cannot form" and the sim is "silently dropping" it. Both are false, and I verified this against the running sim.

WHAT THE SIM ACTUALLY DOES. Fluorite passes its ingredient floor (fluid_min Ca≥10, F≥5; broth has Ca=20, F=18→48) so it is evaluated fairly by the same nucleation gate every mineral runs — it is NOT hard-excluded. I ran seed 42 for all 70 steps: σ_fluorite peaks at 0.222 (at step 65, Ca=20/F=48/T=264/pH=5.22) against a sigma_crit of 1.2. It never gets within a factor of 5 of the threshold. During the scenario's signature HF-etch (steps 58-60, pH 2.9-3.1) the acid-dissolution penalty drives σ to exactly 0 — the late Shigar fluid is actively fluorite-UNDERSATURATED, the same corrosive HF chemistry that etches the aquamarines. So fluorite is not "dropped"; it is a modeled, quantified undersaturation.

THE LIMITING REAGENT IS Ca, NOT F. The reviewer conflates "F is abundant" (true — that is why topaz grows) with "the CaF₂ budget is abundant" (false). Ca sits pinned at 20 ppm the whole run (nothing supplies it). Topaz (Al₂SiO₄(F,OH)₂) grows because it consumes the abundant Al (150) + F and needs NO Ca; fluorite (CaF₂) and fluorapatite (Ca-phosphate) both need Ca. Fluorapatite is in fact hard-gated: apatite fluid_min Ca≥50 > broth Ca=20 → returns 0. Same single cause. This is exactly real peraluminous-pegmatite geochemistry: residual pocket fluids are strongly Ca-depleted (Ca partitions into early plagioclase), topaz is the characteristic F-sink precisely because Al is abundant and Ca scarce, and fluorite is a minor/late accessory. The gate is calibrated consistently: every one of the sim's 7 fluorite-bearing scenarios runs Ca=80-600 ppm (lowest epithermal_telluride at 80; MVT/elmwood/tormiq at 300-600). Pegmatite Ca=20 is 4-30× below that — genuinely sub-fluorite, not a scoreboard hack. The absence is fully causally controlled: to fire fluorite at F=48 you'd need Ca≈83 ppm; the lever is broth Ca, exactly what the drift doctrine demands.

DOES THE OBJECTION SURVIVE? The residual: fluorite IS documented at Shigar (Agheem 2014 EPMA, mindat loc-5420) and named in the author's own notes' F-suite. A maximally faithful model COULD add a minor late green fluorite via the physically-real mechanism the objection gestures at — the cleavelandite/albitization event liberating Ca²⁺ (An→albite) to pair with abundant F. That is a legitimate OPTIONAL enhancement. But it does not make the current output contradict nature: the sim never asserts "no fluorite at Shigar," it models one pocket's dominant showpiece paragenesis (feldspar/quartz/schorl/cleavelandite/aquamarine/topaz — the archetypal blue-beryl-on-cleavelandite plate), correctly captures Ca-limitation, and legitimately curates a 6-species showpiece list rather than an exhaustive accessory census. The objection's central chemical premise ("no chemical reason") is refuted by the quantified Ca-limitation, and "silently dropping" is refuted by the fair-gate + active-dissolution evidence. The sim is defensibly correct; only a low-severity fidelity enhancement remains.

refuted.claim: epidote grows from step 53 with 5 events (more events than quartz's 3) in the granite-hosted Aar cleft
refuted.objection: The card itself frames this scenario as the granite-hosted counterpart, explicitly DISTINCT from the amphibolite-hosted epidote cleft (tormiq_alpine_cleft). Epidote is the diagnostic mineral of the amphibolite/calc-silicate-hosted Alpine clefts (Knappenwand/Untersulzbach-type green epidote), where the mafic host supplies Ca-Fe-Al. In a clean Variscan Aar granite the cleft fluids are Ca-poor, which is precisely WHY the Grimsel smoky-quartz suite is epidote-free (adularia + chlorite + hematite + fluorite + calcite). Epidote appearing here as a 5-event main-stage player — more events than the volumetric quartz — is confabulated abundance that dissolves the exact granite/amphibolite end-member distinction this scenario was built to draw.
refuted.evidence: The Grimsel/Aar granite Zerrkluft suite is smoky quartz + adularia + chlorite + hematite + pink fluorite + calcite + wedge titanite + Alpine pericline albite; epidote is characteristic of the amphibolite/greenschist-hosted alpine clefts, not the granite smoky-quartz end-member (the card's own anchor text and the tormiq_alpine_cleft split). Real granite-cleft fluids are Ca-limited.
refuted.growth_condition_lever: Lower the Ca (and Fe/Al) delivery in this scenario's broth trajectory — a clean-granite fluid is Ca-poor — and/or raise the epidote nucleation threshold so it fires only in the higher-Ca amphibolite broth of tormiq_alpine_cleft. The freed Ca-Al budget should express as adularia + chlorite instead, keeping epidote to a trace (or absent) in the granite end-member.

refutation.reasoning: Strongest defense of the sim, and it holds. The objection rests on one load-bearing factual claim: that the Grimsel granite-hosted smoky-quartz cleft is "epidote-free" and epidote here is "confabulated." The primary literature falsifies this. (1) The official Grimsel/Gerstenegg crystal-cave mineral list (the type central-Aar alpine cleft, opened 1974) names 12 species and epidote is explicitly among them, sitting right beside the smoky quartz + adularia + chlorite + fluorite + calcite + titanite + apatite suite the reviewer says defines the epidote-free end-member. (2) Peverelli et al. (GChron 3:123, 2021; EJM 36:879, 2024) date epidote from an actual meter-sized CLEFT ("Planggenstock") in the Central Aar Granite, note epidote-quartz veins are "common in the Grimsel Granodiorite," and give Miocene ages (16.9-19.2 Ma, Handegg phase) synchronous with the Alpine cleft-forming exhumation this scenario models. So epidote is a genuine, Alpine-age, granite-cleft phase at Grimsel — the sim is modeling a real occurrence, not fabricating one. The reviewer's "clean granite is Ca-poor, therefore epidote-free" is an over-drawn textbook contrast; nature itself blurs it (host is a metagranodiorite, and epidote occurs in both hosts, differing in ABUNDANCE not presence). The residual real point — epidote's 5 events reading as "more than quartz's 3" — is largely an artifact: quartz's low count comes from the sceptre crack-seal nucleation gate (a few stem+cap generations; the card itself calls quartz "the volumetric main stage"), so event-count is not an abundance claim. Epidote is capped at 5 (same cap as the tormiq amphibolite cleft), and whether it should be knocked to an accessory 1-2 crystals to sharpen the granite/amphibolite abundance contrast is a growth-condition tuning matter (lower Ca 200/Al 6 further, or add an Al ceiling), which the card already flags as an un-tuned "surprise" (epidote is NOT in expects_species) awaiting vugg-tune-scenario — not a scoreboard patch and not a contradiction of nature. The objection's premise (epidote foreign to Grimsel granite clefts) does not survive; only a minor abundance-calibration opportunity remains.

refuted.claim: environment trajectory reports SI_siderite climbing from 0.189 to 4.031 (10^4 supersaturation) while Eh is fixed at +290 mV oxidizing and hematite + goethite (both ferric) are the Fe phases that grow
refuted.objection: Siderite is FeCO3 — a REDUCED-iron carbonate. In a genuinely oxidizing fluid (+290 mV, precipitating Fe3+ oxides) the dissolved Fe2+ activity should be vanishingly small because iron is partitioned into hematite/goethite, so SI_siderite should be deeply negative, not +4. The saturation instrument is redox-decoupled: it is computing siderite saturation from a total-Fe (or Fe2+) pool that ignores the oxidizing Eh. Siderite correctly never nucleates here (so the grown biography is not falsified), but a reported 10^4 supersaturation of a reduced carbonate under an oxidizing Eh is an internal environment inconsistency — and a latent bug: a longer run, a different seed, or any nucleation path that keys off SI would erroneously grow siderite alongside hematite, a gross redox violation.
refuted.evidence: Standard Fe redox systematics: at Eh ~+290 mV in a near-neutral-to-alkaline fluid iron is Fe3+ (hematite/goethite field), Fe2+ activity collapses, so a Fe2+ carbonate cannot be 10^4 supersaturated; siderite is stable only in reducing, CO2-charged, low-Eh waters (Fe-Eh-pH stability diagrams). The strip's own Fe3+ oxides (hematite step 89, goethite step 143) confirm the oxidizing state that should forbid siderite saturation.
refuted.growth_condition_lever: Couple the siderite ion-activity product to Eh: make the aqueous Fe2+ activity feeding SI_siderite collapse as Eh rises / as hematite draws down iron, so SI_siderite tracks the redox trajectory rather than total Fe. Then the scoreboard reads deeply undersaturated siderite in this oxidizing cleft (self-consistent with the ferric oxides) instead of a spurious +4, closing the latent false-nucleation risk.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest steelman of the objection: the strip literally reports a reduced Fe(II) carbonate at 10^4 supersaturation inside a fluid that is precipitating ferric oxides at +290 mV — an internal inconsistency, and a footgun if any nucleation path ever keys off that SI. I tried to make that latent-bug claim survive and it does not.

CONFIRMED but harmless: the DISPLAYED SI_siderite (0.189→4.031) is computed by _SI_simple('siderite',fluid,T,'Fe') in js/32b-supersat-carbonate-Ksp.ts:279, which reads the total fluid.Fe pool (ramped to 200 ppm by the iron-rose stage, physically all Fe3+) with no Eh partitioning. So the instrument is redox-decoupled, as claimed.

REFUTED where it matters: that displayed SI drives nothing. Its only consumers are the helix/strip overlay display and carbonateOmega (js/99j, js/32b:420). Siderite growth runs on a SEPARATE function, supersaturation_siderite() (js/32-supersat-carbonate.ts:281), whose first substantive line is a HARD reducing gate: `if (!carbonateRedoxAnoxic(this.fluid, g.O2_max!)) return 0;` with O2_max 0.8. I computed the gate threshold ehFromO2(0.8)=181 mV; the scenario sits at Eh +290 mV / O2 1.496, so the gate returns 0 (and there is an additional carbonateRedoxPenalty). Therefore supersaturation_siderite()=0 for this fluid at every step, and _nuc_siderite (js/82:114, wired in the per-step dispatch) can never fire — regardless of run length or seed. The reviewer's 'a longer run / different seed / any SI-keyed path would grow siderite' is architecturally impossible; the redox gate lives on the growth path, exactly where it should.

The reviewer's implied remedy (partition Fe by Eh so Fe2+ collapses) also misdiagnoses the model: the sim's own Fe3+/Fe2+ Nernst couple has E0=770 mV, so at +290 mV redoxFraction(Fe) returns f_reduced≈1.0 (verified 7.7e-9 oxidized). Feeding that into the SI would leave +4 unchanged. Making the SI physically 'correct' needs ferric-hydroxide/Pourbaix solubility control that the coarse single-Fe-pool chemistry intentionally omits and handles at the mineral gates instead. So the finding is not a clean growth-condition lever under the drift doctrine.

Net: the biography is correct for the right reason (oxidizing Aar endmember: hematite+goethite, no siderite; the narrator prose js/92b:146 and the gate both encode 'reducing only'), growth is correctly redox-gated, and the one redox-naive strip channel is a documented, consequence-free display simplification uniform across scenarios. The objection's medium-severity core — latent redox violation — does not survive.

refuted.claim: epidote first nucleates at step 0 (5 crystals, capped at max_nucleation) — before quartz lines the cleft (first step 24) and before the designed epidote_main stage event (step 80)
refuted.objection: The declared paragenesis and the author's own calibration target are 'quartz lining FIRST -> Ti-Fe oxides -> epidote main -> byssolite -> adularia -> late calcite', with the hand specimen an 'epidote prism perched on a quartz-lined cleft wall'. Nucleating epidote at step 0 on the bare wall, before any quartz exists, inverts that substrate relationship and front-loads the entire epidote cap before the step-80 Fe3+ pulse that is supposed to drive the main stage — so the render can show epidote NOT perched on quartz, contradicting the target habit.
refuted.evidence: Claim-card paragenetic table: albite/epidote/feldspar/titanite first step 0; quartz first step 24; fluorite step 27 (strip fluorite_morph null->0 at step 27). The initial broth (Ca 600, Al 12, Fe 40, O2 1.5, 420C) already clears epidote's Ca/Al/Fe/Si + redox gate at step 0. The event handler comment in js/70v-tormiq.ts:43-47 shows the author already fought this exact quartz-lining timing (fixed by pushing SiO2 +1100 at step 25 so quartz lines ~step 24) but never gated epidote to wait for the lining. Partly a general engine artifact (step-0 nucleation from an already-saturated broth), hence medium confidence.
refuted.growth_condition_lever: Lower the INITIAL Fe/Al/Ca below epidote's nucleation gate so epidote stays sub-saturated until quartz has lined (step 25) and the tormiq_epidote_main Fe3+/Al/Ca pulse (step 80) drives nucleation — delivering the designed quartz-then-epidote order and the 'perched on quartz' habit via broth/event timing rather than a hot epidote-saturated starting fluid.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest defense of the sim: I verified the mechanism is real — the 420°C oxidized Ca-Al-Fe-Si broth clears every epidote gate at step 0 (σ≈6), so all 5 epidote crystals nucleate on the bare wall before quartz saturates (SiO2 waits for the +1100 push at step 25). But this does NOT contradict nature. (1) Real Tormiq specimens are dominantly epidote-on-BYSSOLITE / on-matrix, not on quartz — iRocks/minfind describe "byssolite-covered rod with gemmy epidote blades" and "Forest Green Epidote and Byssolite"; the quartz+adularia combo is a separate, secondary specimen class. So "perched on quartz" is one habit among several, and the reviewer over-reads it as the sole correct target. (2) Epidote is a genuinely early, higher-T alpine-cleft mineral (stable to ~450°C+) that commonly nucleates directly on the amphibolite fissure wall — its Ca-Al-Fe source — so epidote-before-quartz is consistent with a protracted, overlapping alpine-cleft crystallization interval, not an inversion of it. The "quartz lining first" header is a simplification. (3) The seed-42 outcome hits the calibration aesthetic exactly: gem_prismatic doubly-terminated swords, deep pistachio-green (Fe>20, O2>1.0), the most-abundant species. (4) The step-80 pulse is not dead — it still feeds Fe/Al/Ca and holds O2 through the 250-400°C growth sweet-spot, driving epidote mass and color; early nucleation + protracted growth is how real cleft crystals form.

Where the objection retains minor merit: the step-80 Fe3+/O2 pulse controls growth, not nucleation, and seed-42 can never render the prized epidote-on-quartz combination specimen because all epidote precedes all quartz.
