That is a coverage/causal-control refinement, achievable via a clean growth-condition lever (start O2 just below the 0.5 redox gate, or Al below 4, then let the epidote_main handler cross the gate — restoring the pulse's nucleation control and unlocking the on-quartz combo). But "the sim shows a real habit and not every real habit" is not "the sim contradicts nature." The defense survives: the render is within nature's envelope, so the objection as a contradiction-with-nature fails.

refuted.claim: dolomite grew as a surprise (1 event, first step 0) as an early primary precipitate in the dripstone cavity, ahead of the headline calcite (step 10)
refuted.objection: The dolomite problem: ordered/primary dolomite does not precipitate directly from cool (~24 C) meteoric water on any realistic timescale (Mg2+ dehydration kinetic barrier), and here f_ord=0 means it is not even ordered. Dripstone caves precipitate calcite, aragonite, and at most high-Mg calcite / huntite / hydromagnesite; primary speleothem dolomite is essentially unknown at Frasassi (an aragonite+gypsum sulfuric-acid cave), Carlsbad, or Reed Flute. Growing it BEFORE calcite also inverts the real cave paragenesis, where CaCO3 drip phases come first and any Mg-carbonate is a late/rare evaporative crust.
refuted.evidence: MINERAL_GATES_dolomite (js/32-supersat-carbonate.ts:65-83) sets T_min:10 with the explicit note 'ambient T thermodynamically fine, kinetics handled by ordering factor'; the Kim-2023 f_ord gate lives in grow_dolomite / dolomiteRate and throttles only GROWTH RATE, leaving nucleation unblocked at 23.6 C. Strip chips: SI_dolomite +5.7 log Omega (Omega ~5e5), f_ord=0 across all 150 steps, driven by broth Ca=2000/Mg=1500/CO3=2200 ppm. Real cave drip water is far below dolomite saturation AND cannot nucleate it at ambient T.
refuted.growth_condition_lever: The scenario sits in dolomite's field only because Mg (1500) and carbonate are brine-level. Bring the broth to realistic cave-drip concentrations (Ca ~100, Mg ~10-40, DIC giving modest SI) so dolomite Omega falls under the heterogeneous-nucleation barrier, and drive CaCO3 precipitation with a CO2-degassing / mild-evaporation event rather than by dissolved-carbonate loading. Remove dolomite's cause, not the species.

refutation.reasoning: STRONGEST DEFENSE, AND IT SURVIVES. The reviewer is mechanically correct about the code — supersaturation_dolomite (js/32-supersat-carbonate.ts:301-329) gates nucleation only on sigma_crit=10 + T_min=10, and the Kim-2023 f_ord gate is growth-only, dolomiteRate = pwpNetRate × (0.30 + 0.70·f_ord) (js/52b:315-319), so at f_ord=0 the crystal still grows at 30% rate (v145 note confirms "1 active 1180 µm"). So yes, the sim nucleates and grows a ~1.2 mm poorly-ordered dolomite at 23.6 °C meteoric water. The question is whether that contradicts NATURE — and it does not. The reviewer's premise ("ordered/primary dolomite does not precipitate from cool meteoric water on ANY realistic timescale"; "primary speleothem dolomite is essentially unknown") is factually overstated. Alonso-Zarza et al. 2008 (Sed. Geol. 205:160) document RECENT dolomite forming at 17 °C in oxic-vadose meteoric conditions on the host rock AND aragonite speleothems of Castañar Cave, Spain — initially Ca-rich, non-stoichiometric, POORLY ORDERED. Fischbeck & Müller 1971 report dolomite as a real speleothem phase (Fränkische Schweiz). This flips the objection: (1) the T_min=10 °C nucleation gate is directly supported by the 17 °C Castañar occurrence; (2) f_ord=0 — which the reviewer wielded as damning ("not even ordered") — is precisely the disordered protodolomite state of young cave dolomite, so the sim's product is MORE correct than an ordered gate would be; (3) the reviewer cherry-picked three caves (Frasassi/Carlsbad/Reed Flute) that lack it while the SCENARIO IS AN EXPLICIT COMPOSITE modeling exactly the Mg-rich cool oxic cave-drip-on-aragonite regime that Castañar shows hosts it. The doctrine's test — "does a real rock agree?" — is met: Castañar is that rock. The strongest surviving reviewer prong is the paragenetic one (dolomite at step 0 before calcite at step 10, whereas natural cave dolomite forms LATE via a huntite→Ca-dolomite aging pathway ON aragonite). But that prong is weak here because the environment is completely STATIC (T, pH, Eh, concentration all flat across all 150 steps), so first-nucleation-step is a steady-state RNG lottery, not a modeled time-sequence — and the sim ALSO fires HMC (the Mg-carbonate precursor analog) at step 0, directionally consistent with the precursor-first mechanism. Genuine residual mismatches remain but are CALIBRATION, not existence: SI_dolomite +5.7 (Ω≈5e5) is far above real cave water (broth is inflated sim-scale), and a 1.2 mm crystal is coarser than the 50–300 µm micritic reality — both addressable via growth-condition levers (realistic Ca/Mg/CO3 to lower Ω, or routing through an HMC/huntite aging path), and the team already flagged this scenario a "Phase 1c re-anchor candidate." Those do not sustain a "high-severity, contradicts nature" verdict. The objection as framed fails.

refuted.claim: The broth (Fe 35, CO3 20, pH 7.2→6.7, O2 0.4, T 160→68°C) is held reducing throughout, giving SI_siderite +1.575→+1.197 with EVERY siderite nucleation gate satisfied (fluid_min Fe≥10 & CO3≥20, pH 5–9, O2≤0.8 anoxic, T 20–300, σ>σ_crit 1.0). Only quartz grew.
refuted.objection: Siderite (FeCO3) is a reducing ferrous-carbonate phase that is NOT part of the Ametista do Sul paragenesis. The documented reducing stage-I ferrous sink there is PYRITE (celadonite+chalcedony+pyrite), and the deposit's other iron is all FERRIC (goethite/hematite stain, amethyst Fe³⁺ chromophore). A siderite-supersaturated fluid is the wrong iron regime for this deposit — and the sim only avoids growing siderite by RNG/substrate competition, not by chemistry. It is one nucleation roll or one tuning nudge away from precipitating a mineral that would flatly contradict the locality.
refuted.evidence: js/32 MINERAL_GATES_siderite = {Fe:10,CO3:20}, pH 5–9, O2_max 0.8, T 20–300, σ_crit 1.0 — all met by the broth; strip SI_siderite +1.2..+1.6 (supersaturated). Real paragenesis (Proust & Fontan; 'Mineralisation of amethyst-bearing geodes … low-temperature sedimentary brines', Min. Deposita 2014): stage I celadonite+chalcedony+PYRITE (reducing), stage II amethyst+GOETHITE+anhydrite (oxidizing) — no siderite reported. The broth carries no S, so nature's reducing ferrous sink (pyrite) is unavailable and the model defaults to siderite as the only ferrous product.
refuted.growth_condition_lever: Move the broth into the ferric field: raise Eh/O2 above siderite's anoxic cutoff (O2>0.8 / higher Eh) so SI_siderite falls below σ_crit and Fe partitions ferric — which ALSO grounds the goethite stain and the amethyst Fe³⁺ from real chemistry. Alternatively drop fluid.CO3 below 20 (below siderite fluid_min) so the ferrous-carbonate product cannot clear the gate. If an early reducing stage is wanted, add reduced S so the ferrous sink is pyrite (the real stage-I phase), not siderite.

refutation.reasoning:
Strongest defense of the sim, tested by running amethyst_geode headless (seed 42, 110 steps):

The objection's load-bearing claim — "the sim only avoids growing siderite by RNG/substrate competition, not by chemistry ... one nucleation roll away" — is factually wrong. The empirical siderite nucleation gate returns EXACTLY 0.0000 at every step, blocked by a hard, deterministic chemistry gate, with no RNG in the path.

Mechanism: siderite is NOT KSP-promoted (js/32b:157 `siderite:false`), so `supersaturation_siderite()` runs the empirical engine. Its first guard is `effectiveCO3(fluid,T) < fluid_min.CO3(20) → return 0`. `effectiveCO3` Bjerrum-speciates the 20 ppm carbonate POOL to actual CO3^2- activity: 14.24 ppm at pH 7.2, falling to 11.20 at pH 6.7 — never clearing the 20 floor. The reviewer's "fluid_min CO3≥20 satisfied" is a misread: the gate uses the pH-speciated carbonate, not raw broth CO3=20. And `_nuc_siderite` (js/82:117) fires on `if(sigma>1.0 && !existing && !cap)` with no RNG roll before nucleate(); with sigma≡0 it is unreachable.

The card's "SI_siderite +1.575" (I reproduced +1.556→+1.183) is the THERMODYNAMIC OBSERVER (carbonateSaturationIndex = log10 ω), a diagnostic strip that does not gate nucleation. The reviewer conflated the observer with the operative gate.

"One tuning nudge away" is also weak: even KSP-promoting siderite wouldn't grow it, because the effectiveCO3<20 hard floor executes BEFORE the kspSupersatActiveFor branch — two independent layers keep siderite out. The gate is geochemically principled (siderite needs CO3^2- activity, genuinely pH-suppressed at 6.7-7.2 where carbonate is mostly bicarbonate), not a lucky Ksp accident. The broth's Fe (Fe3+ amethyst chromophore + goethite + celadonite) and modest carbonate (Gilg et al. 2003 document calcite in these geodes) are both defensible, and the output matches the ferric-iron-dominated Ametista locality: only quartz/amethyst, no siderite.

Does the defense survive? Yes. The sim gives the correct, locality-consistent answer via robust deterministic chemistry, not RNG. Honest residual caveat (kept low-grade, doesn't overturn the verdict): there is a real internal two-number inconsistency — the thermodynamic observer says ω≈36 (supersaturated) while the operative gate says 0 — and the thermo engine treats all dissolved Fe as ferrous-available, ignoring the ferric sequestration into goethite/celadonite the scenario itself invokes. That is a documentation/observer nuance and a coarse-model simplification, not a wrong or RNG-fragile output, and needs no growth-condition fix. Per the drift doctrine there is nothing to move and no scoreboard to patch.

refuted.claim: Eh is pinned at +77.165 mV (O2 0.394 mg/L) for all 110 steps — dead constant to 3 decimals — so amethyst nucleates and grows in a reducing fluid, and the goethite/hematite base stain is imposed as a film_coat event at step 84 in that same reducing fluid.
refuted.objection: The signature of Ametista do Sul genesis is a reducing→oxidizing redox EVOLUTION: stage I (celadonite/chalcedony/pyrite) reducing, then amethyst + goethite + anhydrite forming as the fluid OXIDIZES (stage II), then barite/gypsum (stage IV, most oxidizing). The sim flattens this to a single reducing state and grows amethyst on the wrong limb; worse, ferric goethite cannot precipitate from an O2=0.4 fluid at all, so the diagnostic base stain is painted on rather than grown, in a fluid whose redox forbids it.
refuted.evidence: Strip Eh/O2/salinity/concentration are single-valued across the run (only temperature has a movement). Mineralisation-stages study: 'During stages II (amethyst, goethite, anhydrite), III (calcite) and IV (barite, calcite, gypsum), the oxidation state of the fluid changed towards more oxidising conditions' — amethyst is the oxidizing-limb mineral, coeval with ferric goethite.
refuted.growth_condition_lever: Add an Eh/O2 movement that rises through the fill (reducing early → oxidizing late), timed so the celadonite film sits on the reducing early limb and amethyst + the goethite base-stain land on the oxidizing limb — reproducing the documented stage I→II→IV redox climb instead of a flat reducing plateau.

refutation.reasoning:
The objection rests on two load-bearing factual claims, and both fail.

(1) "Amethyst grows in a REDUCING fluid." False. Eh = +77.165 mV is a POSITIVE potential — mildly OXIDIZING, not reducing. O2 = 0.4 mg/L is suboxic/dysoxic groundwater, not anoxic; low dissolved O2 is not the same as a reducing redox state. The reviewer conflates "low O2" with "reducing." A positive Eh at pH ~7 sits on the oxidizing side of every Fe redox front relevant here. Crucially, amethyst's colour is a Fe³⁺ (ferric) centre — the sim's own gate reads growth-weighted quartz trace_Fe and requires the iron to be ferric to reach the amethyst band. Ferric iron REQUIRES non-reducing conditions. So the sim implicitly and correctly grows amethyst under oxidizing conditions, i.e., on the SAME oxidizing (stage II) limb the reviewer's own cited study assigns to amethyst — not "the wrong limb."

(2) "Ferric goethite cannot precipitate from an O2=0.4 fluid at all." Thermodynamically false. Goethite (α-FeOOH) is the diagnostic Fe³⁺ oxyhydroxide of LOW-O2 groundwater redox fronts — it forms precisely where anoxic Fe²⁺ water meets even trace oxygen; it does not need high DO. At pH 7.2 the Fe²⁺/goethite boundary (Fe²⁺ + 2H₂O = FeOOH + 3H⁺ + e⁻) sits well BELOW +77 mV for any realistic Fe²⁺ activity (order −80 to −230 mV for [Fe²⁺] 10⁻⁴–10⁻⁶). At +77 mV / pH 7.2 goethite is comfortably inside its stability field. So the base stain is not "painted on in a fluid whose redox forbids it" — the redox permits it, and the same mildly-oxidizing state that keeps the amethyst chromophore ferric also stabilizes the goethite. That self-consistency (one ferric state serving both phases) is a strength, not a contradiction.

What survives: the reviewer is correct that Ametista do Sul shows a real reducing→oxidizing EVOLUTION (stage I pyrite/celadonite reducing → stage II amethyst+goethite → stage IV barite/gypsum most oxidizing), and the scenario flattens Eh to a single value. But the scenario deliberately models only the oxidizing-limb assemblage — quartz/amethyst plus a goethite staining film_coat — and expects_species is just quartz. It contains NO reduced stage-I phase (no pyrite) and no stage-IV sulfate, so nothing is grown on the wrong limb. Co-nucleating amethyst + goethite at one oxidizing state reproduces exactly the stage-II assemblage the reviewer cites. The flattening is a defensible simplification of a quartz-focused scenario, not a contradiction of nature. Under the drift doctrine it leaves a legitimate FUTURE-ENRICHMENT opening — an explicit reducing→oxidizing Eh movement plus stage-I reduced phases would add causal control — but that is an enhancement, not a correctness bug, and it would be wired as a growth-condition Eh trajectory, not a scoreboard patch. The objection as filed (amethyst in a reducing fluid; goethite forbidden) does not survive.

refuted.claim: aragonite co-nucleates with calcite at step 0 (97 °C, 0.5 kbar) as a discrete phase, SI_aragonite 0.69 → -0.06
refuted.objection: At ~100 °C and 0.5 kbar, calcite is the thermodynamically stable CaCO₃ polymorph (aragonite's stability field needs several kbar at this T), so aragonite can only appear metastably — and there is no aragonite-selection driver in this broth to make it do so. Aragonite is favored when Mg/Ca is high enough to poison calcite growth (and by elevated Sr/SO₄ or rapid CO₂-degassing spikes). Here Mg/Ca is molar ≈ 0.09 (Mg 15.7 ppm vs Ca 301 ppm), SO₄/S = 0, Sr = 0, Ba = 0, and calcite is actually MORE supersaturated than aragonite (SI_calcite 0.82 > SI_aragonite 0.69). With calcite uninhibited and out-competing it, a low-Mg closed vug grows calcite only; aragonite as a coexisting discrete phase is unmotivated.
refuted.evidence: Verified: aragonite precipitation is favored by increasing BOTH temperature and Mg:Ca, and 'aragonite can be precipitated from waters with low CO₂ degassing provided the Mg:Ca ratio is high enough to inhibit calcite precipitation' — i.e., calcite inhibition is the gate. Caveat noted honestly: lab studies show aragonite yield peaks ~80 °C even at low Mg, so the 74–97 °C window gives some temperature cover (hot-spring 'flos ferri' aragonite exists) — hence medium confidence, not high. Sources: pubs.geoscienceworld.org/sepm/jsedres/article-abstract/79/6/363 (SI state × Mg/Ca controls polymorph) and sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0016703716304124 (cave-dripwater aragonite vs calcite, Mg/Ca control). Broth (strip v226): Mg 15.7, Ca 301, S 0, Sr 0.
refuted.growth_condition_lever: Gate aragonite nucleation on an aragonite-promoting broth signature — Mg/Ca high enough to inhibit calcite (raise Mg or lower Ca), or elevated Sr/SO₄, or a degassing/supersaturation spike. In this low-Mg, sulfate-free broth that gate stays shut, so the fluid grows calcite only. (If the boss wants aragonite in the teaching specimen, the lever is to raise broth Mg/Ca; do not just let it fire whenever SI_aragonite>0.)

refutation.reasoning:
STRONGEST DEFENSE — and it holds. The reviewer's whole case rests on one premise: "the aragonite-selection gate is Mg/Ca poisoning of calcite, and it's absent here." That is only ONE of three literature-recognized aragonite drivers, and the engine explicitly models all three as an ADDITIVE weighted sum, not a product (js/32-supersat-carbonate.ts:459, with the code comment at :458 stating "Mg/Ca alone is enough at high values" — i.e. any single driver can carry it). Reading the actual firing arithmetic for this broth: mg_factor ≈ 0.008 (reviewer correct — Mg contributes nothing), but T_factor ≈ 0.958 at 97 °C (sigmoid midpoint 50 °C, Burton & Walter 1987 GCA 51:777 "temperature control on aragonite/calcite precipitation rates, even in low-Mg") and omega_factor ≈ 0.26 at Ω_arag ≈ 4.9 (Ostwald step rule — the metastable polymorph nucleates first from strongly supersaturated solution, Morse 1997 / Sun 2015). Favorability ≈ 0.23, × Ω 4.9 ≈ 1.14 > sigma_crit 1.0 — a MARGINAL, single-event firing. So aragonite here is thermally driven hot-spring/"flos ferri" aragonite, the documented proximal-facies phase of hot travertine (Mammoth, Pamukkale, Kele et al.) that transitions to calcite as water cools — and indeed the sim's SI_aragonite falls 0.756 → −0.06 as the broth cools 97 → 74 °C, so aragonite fires once while hot and then goes undersaturated. That IS the natural pattern.

The reviewer's thermodynamic argument ("calcite SI 0.82 > aragonite 0.69, so calcite out-competes it") misreads polymorph selection as thermodynamic when it is kinetic: aragonite routinely nucleates metastably ALONGSIDE more-stable calcite (the entire premise of the Ostwald step rule and of aragonite-calcite coexistence in hand specimens). Calcite being more stable does not suppress aragonite nucleation. And "co-nucleates at step 0" is one marginal event on a serial gate (max 1 crystal) — a subordinate co-phase, not aragonite dominating. If anything the sim is MORE accurate than the objection: at 97 °C the hot proximal facies of real travertine is aragonite-DOMINANT, so calcite-only would be the less faithful outcome.

Doctrine check: causal control is fully intact and lever-based — cool the broth to 25 °C (T_factor collapses) or drop supersaturation (omega_factor collapses) and aragonite stops. We understand exactly why it moved and can move it back by changing growth conditions, never by editing the scoreboard. No fix warranted; the only residual is a pedagogical one (aragonite as a "surprise" in a calcite tutorial), which is presentation, not a physics defect, and which the doctrine forbids "fixing" by deleting the species. The one thing I could not independently re-verify is the exact logged firing value vs my hand-calc, but the claim card confirms the qualitative behavior (fires step 0, undersaturated by end) and the arithmetic is unambiguous that T, not Mg, carries it. The objection's own medium-confidence caveat already concedes the temperature cover that refutes it.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: marcasite never nucleates; only pyrite (FeS2 cubic) grows, though marcasite is in the mvt nucleatable set
minor_or_abstraction.objection: Marcasite (orthorhombic FeS2), locally 'mundic', is a characteristic and abundant Tri-State phase — colloform/cockade marcasite is diagnostic and it partners pyrite roughly equally. A Tri-State biography that grows pyrite but no marcasite misses a district fingerprint. (Partly a polymorph abstraction, hence low severity.)
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: minerals.json lists marcasite in the mvt scenario nucleatable set (so it is intended to be reachable) but it produced 0 events at seed 42. Tri-State reference: 'Marcasite and pyrite are commonly occurring with the ores.' Marcasite is favoured at low pH (<~5-6) and low T relative to pyrite.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: The mvt pH stays 6.2-7.2, a touch high for marcasite over pyrite. Introduce a low-pH acidification pulse via the existing fluid_mixing/fluid_pulse events (or lower marcasite's pH gate threshold) so the fluid crosses marcasite's low-pH stability window and the district's diagnostic mundic grows alongside pyrite — a pH-trajectory / gate-threshold lever.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: dolomite never nucleates despite SI_dolomite being the single most supersaturated phase in the run (0.945 -> 3.654, max 4.535); the scenario advertises 'replacement dolomite' as the first paragenetic phase and renders a dolomite host matrix.
minor_or_abstraction.objection: Saddle/replacement dolomite is THE diagnostic gangue of MVT deposits and the documented host/waste at Elmwood. Growing zero dolomite while it is ~4.5 log units supersaturated — and instead precipitating aragonite + calcite in the void — misses the deposit's defining carbonate and contradicts the scenario's own stated paragenesis.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: Web: Elmwood 'waste material consists predominantly of dolomite'; saddle dolomite is a standard MVT gangue (dolomite C-O-isotope MVT-vector paper, ScienceDirect). Strip SI_dolomite max 4.535 is the highest of any carbonate; grown list contains 0 dolomite. The dolomite matrix is render-only (comment: 'composition defaults limestone for the carbonate dissolve()'). The 'dolomite problem' (kinetic inhibition of low-T dolomite nucleation despite high SI) is a legitimate real reason a void doesn't fill with dolomite — so the suppression is defensible, but the scenario should not then advertise replacement dolomite it cannot grow.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: If the showcase wants the replacement/saddle dolomite visible, add a kinetically-gated dolomite nucleation pathway (high T + high Mg/Ca + long dwell) so the large SI converts to early crystals; otherwise honor the dolomite problem and drop 'replacement dolomite' from the advertised paragenesis. Either way the lever is a nucleation-gate/broth change, not a scoreboard edit.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: siderite (FeCO3) nucleates at step 14 together with pyrite, from Fe 8 in the same brine that is dropping sphalerite/galena.
minor_or_abstraction.objection: When reduced sulfur is present (as it must be to drop sphalerite/galena/pyrite at that step), Fe partitions to iron sulfide (pyrite/marcasite), not to FeCO3 — siderite requires low sulfide activity, an S-poor carbonate facies. Elmwood's iron mineral is marcasite, not siderite, and siderite is not in its documented species.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: Elmwood species (web) include marcasite, not siderite. Strip grows both pyrite and siderite at step 14 with SI_siderite 1.5-3.6. Sulfidic MVT brines route Fe to disulfide (pyrite/marcasite); FeCO3 forms only where sulfide is scarce.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: With the sulfidic Eh trajectory plus adequate reduced S, let Fe route to marcasite/pyrite and starve siderite (raise S activity / close the siderite nucleation window under sulfidic Eh). Siderite should only appear in an S-poor carbonate facies, which this ore stage is not.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: Acanthite (Ag2S) nucleates as a discrete phase 5 times (step 19), outnumbering galena's 4 events, from Ag=5 in the broth.
minor_or_abstraction.objection: Silver in a basinal-brine lead-zinc vein reports chiefly as argentiferous galena (Ag riding in the PbS lattice / micro-inclusions), not as five discrete Ag2S grains that outnumber the galena itself. Discrete acanthite over-nucleation overstates a trace metal's mineralogical independence; North Pennine lead is broadly modest-silver, so a system where Ag2S out-fires galena reads as an implausibly silver-rich confabulation.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: Card: acanthite 5 events vs galena 4, from Ag=5. Silver geochemistry: Ag is typically carried in galena as solid solution / micro-inclusions in Pb-Zn veins; discrete Ag2S is a minor late phase. BGS North Pennine notes the sulphide zone lead is commonly low-tenor (silver mixed historically). Ag2S out-nucleating galena is not credible for the role.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: Lower Ag in the broth toward a genuine trace and/or raise acanthite's nucleation threshold and let Ag partition into galena, so silver manifests as argentiferous galena rather than 5 free Ag2S events. Broth-concentration + threshold lever, not a scoreboard delete.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: wurtzite co-nucleates with sphalerite at step 0 (pH 6.5, T ~118°C), i.e. both ZnS polymorphs grow together at the outset
minor_or_abstraction.objection: Sphalerite is the stable ZnS polymorph at these low-T, near-neutral conditions. Metastable wurtzite is favored by acidic (pH < ~5.5), sulfur-deficient, high-supersaturation regimes — the settings that produce colloform 'schalenblende'. Co-growing wurtzite and sphalerite on equal footing at pH 6.5 is unrealistic; at near-neutral pH you'd expect sphalerite-dominant with wurtzite confined to acidic/colloform microbands, if at all. This is not a howler — wurtzite genuinely occurs in low-T MVT schalenblende — but the polymorph selection here looks ungated by the pH/supersaturation controls that actually decide it.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: ZnS polymorph selection: wurtzite metastably favored at low pH (< ~5.5) / high supersaturation / S-deficiency; sphalerite the stable form at near-neutral pH and low T. Wurtzite is documented in low-T MVT colloform schalenblende (sphalerite+wurtzite+galena banding), which is why this is borderline rather than clearly false. Card: initial pH 6.5, T 118°C, wurtzite first step 0 alongside sphalerite.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: Gate the sphalerite/wurtzite polymorph choice on a low-pH (< ~5.5) and/or high-sigma window rather than letting both nucleate unconditionally; at the scenario's pH 6.5 the default should be sphalerite-only, with wurtzite reserved for an acidic-pulse / colloform-supersaturation excursion if schalenblende banding is the goal.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: native_gold — the flagship species of the type gold-telluride district and a member of expects_species — never nucleates (no-show).
minor_or_abstraction.objection: Cripple Creek is THE gold-telluride locality and native gold is a primary chief ore mineral there (liberated where local Te is depleted by earlier telluride growth, exactly as the scenario mechanic describes). Its total absence leaves the biography without the district's defining metal in native form. The author has logged this as a known 'aspirational miss' (expects-tune arc), so this is confirmation rather than a new charge — but the lever matters.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: mindat + jsjgeology Cripple Creek: native gold is a chief ore mineral alongside the tellurides. Scenario notes (data/scenarios.json5) explicitly acknowledge native_gold absent at base seeds. native_gold gate is Au>=0.5 (js/36) while the broth sits at Au=0.4 by design, so it can only fire on LOCAL Au accumulation after Te depletion, which never materializes in this run.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: Drive a genuine Te-depletion trajectory (or a late Au-recharge pulse) so local Au climbs above the 0.5 gate once the tellurides have consumed the Te — i.e. make native_gold a Te-exhaustion product via the fluid path, rather than lowering the gate to force it. This is the correct place for the 'residual native' liberation the mechanic already describes.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: fluorite — a diagnostic gangue phase in expects_species with F=20 in the broth (above its F>=10 threshold) — never nucleates (no-show).
minor_or_abstraction.objection: Fluorite is a documented, diagnostic gangue mineral in Cripple Creek quartz-pyrite-fluorite veins; F is well above the nucleation gate, yet no fluorite grows. A diagnostic gangue no-show where the ligand is present and above threshold points at a saturation/nucleation-window problem, not a chemistry absence. Author-acknowledged as an aspirational miss.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: jsjgeology Cripple Creek gold-ore: veins carry 'high-grade gold-silver tellurides in quartz-pyrite-fluorite gangue'. Scenario notes confirm fluorite absent at base seeds despite F=20 being above the F>=10 gate.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: Fluorite forms on cooling/mixing when Ca-F supersaturation is reached; check whether Ca (80 ppm) or the cooling-pulse timing keeps fluorite sigma just under its nucleation threshold. Nudge via a cooling/meteoric-mixing event (lower T raises fluorite SI) or a modest Ca bump so the Ca x F product clears saturation during the epithermal window — a saturation-trajectory lever, not a threshold edit.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: siderite oversaturates strongly (SI_siderite → +1.26) and grows 3 events at step 74, in a scenario built to make Fe-POOR pale-pink rhodochrosite.
minor_or_abstraction.objection: The scenario's whole rhodochrosite aesthetic (pale-pink, 'Fe-poor signature') requires a genuinely Fe-depleted carbonate-stage fluid. But the same run oversaturates siderite (FeCO3) by +1.26 and also grows abundant pyrite and arsenopyrite — a large Fe budget. An Fe-rich fluid that precipitates its own iron carbonate would poison the pale-pink rhodochrosite toward brown Mn-Fe carbonate; the two cannot both be true. Siderite is also not a Sunnyside Stage V/VI phase (that stage is rhodochrosite-dominated Mn carbonate).
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: Casadevall & Ohmoto 1977 Stage V is Mn-carbonate (rhodochrosite) dominated; siderite is not a headline phase. Rhodochrosite–siderite form a solid solution — a fluid at SI_siderite +1.26 delivers Fe into the carbonate, which is incompatible with a pale-pink Fe-poor rhodochrosite target.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: Lower dissolved Fe in the mid-to-late carbonate-stage broth (keep SI_siderite < 0) so the carbonate budget goes to Mn-calcite/rhodochrosite and the Fe-poor pale-pink signature is chemically honest, rather than growing an Fe-carbonate the deposit doesn't show.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: native_arsenic first nucleates at step 4, T ~333°C — very early and hot (4 events, onset with the earliest arsenides)
minor_or_abstraction.objection: Class paragenesis places native arsenic as a LATE-stage reduction product that post-dates the zoned Co-Ni-Fe arsenides at cooler temperatures; a first appearance at 333°C essentially syn with the earliest skutterudite (step 0) / nickeline (step 3) is premature and too hot. This is a premature-onset issue rather than a wholesale mis-stage, since the sim does also grow native As later — but the hot opening event should not be its birthplace.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: Scharrer, Kreissl & Markl 2019: the association comprises zoned Ni-Co-Fe arsenides/sulfarsenides and 'late-stage native As'; native As forms by reduction after the arsenide framework. Staude/Markl 2012: Wittichen As is dominantly hosted in Co-arsenides + secondary arsenates — native As is not the hot early phase.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: Gate native_arsenic's onset later/cooler — tie its window to the reduced native-metal stage near the Eh pulse (T well below 333°C) rather than the 340°C opening, so the Co-Ni arsenides precede the native arsenic reduced from the same As-rich fluid.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: pharmacolite and haidingerite (Ca-hydrogen-arsenates) are in expects_species but never nucleate (no-shows).
minor_or_abstraction.objection: Pharmacolite and haidingerite are classic Schneeberg/Erzgebirge supergene efflorescences from oxidative weathering of the Co-Ni arsenides — pharmacolite is a well-known Schneeberg species. Their absence leaves the arsenide-weathering story incomplete: the run makes the Co/Ni ARSENATES (erythrite, annabergite) but not the Ca-arsenates that share the same oxidation source.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: Erzgebirge arsenide oxidation zones are type/classic occurrences for pharmacolite (Ca(AsO3OH)·2H2O) and haidingerite; they co-occur with erythrite/annabergite in the Schneeberg-Wittichen supergene suite the scenario is otherwise modelling.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: Their SI never crosses because Ca and AsO4 don't co-peak at low T — the step-145 late As pulse is consumed by uranospinite. Add a final low-T Ca+AsO4 beat AFTER the uranyl-arsenate stage (or raise Ca in the cooling oxidation tail) so the Ca-arsenate field saturates. A late-stage Ca/As broth-sequencing lever.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: Aragonite nucleates at step 89 (T ~95 °C, Mg/Ca ~0.3) alongside calcite and dolomite.
minor_or_abstraction.objection: Aragonite is the metastable CaCO3 polymorph; at ~95 °C with LOW Mg/Ca the stable carbonates are calcite and dolomite, both of which the sim already grows. Aragonite is favoured by HIGH Mg/Ca (Mg-poisoning of calcite), high SO4, or near-surface low-T kinetics — none of which obtain here — and it is not a documented Viburnum gangue phase. It reads as a redundant metastable-polymorph confabulation on top of an already carbonate-crowded assemblage (calcite+aragonite+dolomite+siderite+rhodochrosite).
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: Card: aragonite first_step 89, 1 event, at T≈95 °C; broth Mg=100 / late Ca≈336 ppm → Mg/Ca≈0.3. The simulator's OWN aragonite rationale (cooling/sabkha notes) sets the aragonite threshold at Mg/Ca ~1.5 and states 'Aragonite stays absent' below it — yet it fired here at Mg/Ca 0.3, contradicting the model's own gate logic.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: Enforce aragonite's Mg/Ca gate (require >~1.2, i.e. real Mg-poisoning of calcite) or a low-T/high-SO4 trigger; at Mg/Ca 0.3 and 95 °C route CaCO3 to calcite/dolomite. Tighten the aragonite nucleation threshold, don't hand-remove the crystal.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: Chalcopyrite nucleates at step 24; bornite appears later at step 38.
minor_or_abstraction.objection: Bornite (Cu5FeS4, Cu-rich) is the higher-temperature Cu-Fe sulfide; the classic Bingham cooling trend is bornite→chalcopyrite (bornite-dominant hot potassic center grading outward and down-temperature to chalcopyrite + pyrite). Firing chalcopyrite before bornite reverses the diagnostic Cu-sulfide zoning/cooling order.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: Landtwing et al. 2005 (EPSL 235) / 2010 (Econ. Geol. 105): Cu-Fe sulfides precipitate 350–425°C with bornite in the hotter center. The scenario comment itself flags bornite as nucleation-finicky under sim_version 20, so the ordering is partly a pulse/RNG artifact rather than pure chemistry.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: Make the first Cu pulse (step 25) Cu-richer/hotter so the high-Cu/S bornite gate clears before chalcopyrite; as Cu/S falls on cooling, chalcopyrite then pyrite follow — an event/broth-chemistry ordering change, not a baseline edit.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: erythrite (step 165) and annabergite (step 188) grow as supergene Co/Ni arsenates, enabled by the Round-8c-1 broth additions Co=80, Ni=70.
minor_or_abstraction.objection: Bisbee is a Cu-Au-Ag district; a cobalt/nickel sulfarsenide signature (cobaltite/nickeline/safflorite) feeding a cobalt-bloom / annabergite supergene cascade is not part of its recognized character, so these arsenates read as an imported assemblage rather than a Bisbee one.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: Could not substantiate a notable Co/Ni sulfarsenide suite at Bisbee via web/mindat (results confirm Cu-Au-Ag focus and 325+ species but not cobaltite/erythrite specifically). The author defends the addition with Graeme, Graeme & Graeme 2019 + Bryant 1968 — a real, on-card citation I could neither confirm nor refute. Given the discipline against re-raising a citation-defended choice, this is flagged for a specimen/monograph check, not asserted as a lie; single-event frequency (erythrite 1, annabergite 1) is at least appropriately trace.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: If the Co/Ni signature cannot be substantiated in Graeme 2019/Bryant 1968, return Co and Ni in the initial broth to sub-threshold traces (they were set to 80/70 specifically to activate the sulfarsenide + arsenate engines); leaving them below nucleation thresholds keeps cobaltite/erythrite/annabergite dormant without touching expects_species.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: SI_siderite is supersaturated at +1.26 -> +1.64 across the ENTIRE run, i.e. at T from 304 up to 700 C.
minor_or_abstraction.objection: Siderite is a low-temperature diagenetic / very-low-grade carbonate; it is thermodynamically unstable and reacts out well below contact-metamorphic temperatures, so a persistent FeCO3 supersaturation at 500-700 C is impossible in a marble aureole. Either the siderite Ksp lacks T-dependence (a scoreboard artifact) or the fluid Fe budget is higher than the 'Fe-poor 200 ppm' design claims — and the latter would leak straight into the chromophore budget this scenario is built to suppress.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: Standard carbonate phase relations: no metamorphic marble carries stable siderite at 700 C; siderite breaks down at low metamorphic grade. Siderite never nucleates here, so the grown assemblage is not wrong — but the SI scoreboard reads a thermodynamic impossibility. Exact Ksp(T) behavior of the sim's siderite engine was not verified in this pass.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: Give siderite a T-dependent Ksp so its SI falls below saturation above its ~300 C stability ceiling (fixing the thermo, not hiding the number); and audit the actual fluid Fe — an SI_siderite of +1.6 hints the Fe budget may exceed the 200 ppm 'Fe-poor' wall the ruby-vs-sapphire control depends on.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: SI_siderite rises to +3.78 (strongly supersaturated) through the run
minor_or_abstraction.objection: A ferrous carbonate cannot be supersaturated in an oxidizing Ni-laterite (Eh +290->+323 mV, O2 ~1.5 mg/L) that is simultaneously precipitating ferric goethite. The saturation panel implies siderite is ~10^3.8 oversaturated in a fluid whose iron is essentially all Fe3+ — a redox self-contradiction a geologist would flag on sight.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: The grown paragenesis is actually CORRECT here: siderite's nucleation is hard-gated by carbonateRedoxAnoxic at O2_max=0.8 (js/32-supersat-carbonate.ts:287), and O2=1.5 > 0.8, so siderite correctly never grows and goethite forms instead at step 129. The +3.78 is only a raw SI telemetry value computed from total Fe (js/32b) without Eh speciation; at Eh +300 mV a(Fe2+) ~ 0 and the true SI_siderite is deeply negative. So the biography is right; only the scoreboard panel misreports.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: Eh-speciate Fe in the siderite SI calculation (partition total Fe into Fe2+/Fe3+ by Eh) so the panel reports undersaturation consistent with the goethite that actually grows. Telemetry-level chemistry-model fix; does not change the grown paragenesis — flagged for honesty, not as a paragenetic lie.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: opal fires 7 times in the late supergene window (first step 199) — the single most frequent nucleation phase in the run, exceeding quartz (4) and topaz (6).
minor_or_abstraction.objection: Opaline silica does form in deeply-weathered silica-rich saprolite, so its presence is defensible — but making it the numerically dominant phase of an imperial-topaz vein overstates its role; opal is not a hallmark of the Ouro Preto assemblage, where topaz + quartz + Fe-oxides + kaolinite define the weathered zone.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: Weathering-hosted opal-A/CT is real in tropical saprolites; the concern is abundance, not existence. No literature makes opal a diagnostic Ouro Preto phase.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: Lower late-stage silica supersaturation (or tighten the opal nucleation cap / raise its sigma_crit) in the supergene window so opal precipitates as a minor phase rather than dominating — a saturation-driver / gate-threshold lever.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: Author mechanic: step-60 'roll-front contact ... slight reducing pulse — concentrates carnotite + tyuyamunite at the redox front.'
minor_or_abstraction.objection: This inverts roll-front redox paragenesis. The REDUCING front precipitates the PRIMARY reduced ore (uraninite/coffinite + montroseite/roscoelite); the bright-yellow carnotite/tyuyamunite are a later SUPERGENE RE-OXIDATION overprint formed when that reduced roll is re-oxidized near surface. Attributing vanadate concentration to a 'reducing pulse' is backwards. It doesn't corrupt the grown crystal (the sim keeps Eh oxidizing, so the correct oxidized species still forms), but the stated causal story a geologist reads is wrong and it shares a root with the impossible siderite SI.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: Britannica 'roll-front deposits' and USGS: U precipitates in the REDUCING zone as primary U(IV) minerals; oxidized carnotite/tyuyamunite form by subsequent oxidation of that ore. The scenario explicitly scopes out the primary reduced phases ('the primary V minerals; not yet in sim'), so only the framing is inverted.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: Relabel/retune the step-60 event so the oxidized vanadates are concentrated by an OXIDATIVE/evaporative near-surface driver (rising concentration factor, K/Ca cation swing) rather than a 'reducing pulse' — keep Eh oxidizing as it already is and drive vanadate saturation via evaporitic concentration, aligning the event's causal label with the redox state that actually grows the mineral.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: Celestine (SrSO4) nucleates early (first at step 4, 3 events) as a surface co-product of the evaporating brine.
minor_or_abstraction.objection: Celestine is geologically reasonable as an early trace phase from a Permian-evaporite-sourced sulfate brine (SrSO4 is less soluble than CaSO4 and scavenges out first), but I could not find a locality-specific report of celestine growing in the modern Salt Plains surface selenite beds. Its presence is inferred from the ion budget, not documented for this deposit — a mineralogist would want a specimen or lit citation before accepting it as a GSP surface phase.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: Celestine 'occurs primarily in sedimentary rocks, often forming through the interaction of gypsum or anhydrite with Sr-rich waters' and is a documented accessory of the Permian Blaine Formation evaporites — the very strata the GSP salt-dissolution brine passes through — so the Sr supply is real. But the USFWS/OGS surface-mineralogy sources name only halite and selenite gypsum at the land surface; no GSP-surface celestine occurrence surfaced in the search.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: This is a broth-Sr control, not a scoreboard edit: if a lit/specimen check finds no GSP surface celestine, trim the broth Sr so σ_celestine stays subcritical at 150‰ (celestine then stays dormant here); if celestine IS documented, keep the current Sr and the phase is vindicated. Either outcome is driven by the Sr concentration in the initial broth.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: Quartz is a grown species (step 0, 3 events) at T 23–32°C.
minor_or_abstraction.objection: Authigenic quartz precipitation at 23–33°C is kinetically implausible — silica polymerization is negligibly slow at surface temperature, which is why low-T evaporite silica appears as chert/chalcedony over diagenetic timescales, not as quartz nucleating in-season. The card correctly frames the SiO2 as suspended detrital red-bed silt mechanically trapped on the hourglass sectors, so labelling quartz a 'grown' species conflates included detrital sand with a precipitated crystal. It's an honest abstraction of a phase that IS present, not a fabrication.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: USFWS/OGS mechanism: GSP selenite grows within Permian red-bed quartz sand and traps fine sand + clay on the fast-growth sectors to form the hourglass. Detrital quartz is unquestionably present at the locality; low-T authigenic quartz nucleation is not a real GSP process, so the quartz here is inclusion/substrate, not saturation-driven growth.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: No broth change is warranted — the SiO2 is mechanical silt, not a saturation phase. If tightening is desired, tag quartz's events as detrital sediment-trapping (inclusion) rather than nucleation/precipitation so the biography doesn't imply the fluid grew quartz at 28°C.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: calcite is the sole carbonate and the single most abundant phase (12 events); trona / hanksite / sulfohalite / burkeite absent
minor_or_abstraction.objection: Searles is THE type locality for hanksite and sulfohalite, and its dominant bedded/commercial minerals are the sodium carbonates trona/nahcolite/burkeite - the defining signature of a soda lake. Modeling the entire carbonate budget as calcite (a minor Ca-carbonate that should be volumetrically trivial in a Ca-poor brine) at top abundance, with the diagnostic Na-carbonate + double-salt suite absent, under-represents the deposit type.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: Smith 1979 PP1043; Searles is type locality for hanksite/sulfohalite; trona/burkeite are dominant (web-confirmed major-mineral list). NOTE: the author's own notes explicitly flag 'Trona, hanksite, sulfohalite, glauberite, gaylussite, pirssonite, ulexite, colemanite, kernite... worth adding later' - so this is an ACKNOWLEDGED scope gap, not a defended lie. Raised only for completeness.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: Future content: plumb the Na-carbonate gates (trona/nahcolite/burkeite precipitating from the CO3 80 broth on evaporation) and a K-sulfate-carbonate double salt (hanksite), so the carbonate budget precipitates as Na-carbonates and calcite is reserved for the trace early Ca. Adding these would also supply the Ca/Sr carbonate sink that fixes the selenite/celestine findings above.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: quartz nucleates 3 events at ~24 °C from SiO2 = 20 ppm
minor_or_abstraction.objection: Direct crystalline quartz precipitation at ambient surface T is kinetically very difficult — low-T silica normally appears first as amorphous silica / opal / chalcedony and matures to quartz only over long diagenesis. Naively this looks like a too-easy quartz.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: However, displacive authigenic euhedral (often doubly-terminated) quartz is genuinely documented in sabkha and evaporite carbonates (e.g. authigenic quartz nodules and 'floating' euhedra in Persian Gulf sabkha dolomites and Permian evaporites). Treating the silica endpoint as 'quartz' is a defensible abstraction for this setting, so this is not a lie about the deposit.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: No growth-condition change required. Only if strict phase honesty were wanted would one gate the crystalline-quartz label behind long residence / higher T and emit amorphous silica or chalcedony at true ambient surface conditions.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: celestine (SrSO4) nucleates at step 4 (2 events); SI_celestine tracks SI_selenite almost exactly (-0.063 -> -0.189).
minor_or_abstraction.objection: Celestine is not a prominently reported Naica cave mineral, so its presence reads as a surprise. However, Sr routinely co-precipitates with / substitutes into gypsum in any sulfate-saturated Ca-SO4 brine, and the broth carries Sr=6 ppm; a minor Sr-sulfate phase tracking selenite saturation is chemically defensible rather than a contradiction.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: Broth Sr=6 ppm (data/scenarios.json5); SI_celestine mirrors SI_selenite in the strip, consistent with Sr behaving as a Ca analogue in the same sulfate-saturated water. Web search returned no explicit celestine report at Naica but also no contradiction; Sr is a known trace in Ca-SO4 systems. Treat as a minor, mineralogically-sound accessory unless a specimen/lit check demands its removal.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: If de-emphasis is wanted, trim broth Sr toward ~1-2 ppm so celestine stays sub-threshold; but as a low-abundance co-precipitate it is a legitimate outcome of the Ca-SO4 chemistry and needs no fix. Flag as needs_specimen_or_lit_check on actual Naica Sr content.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: quartz is the silica phase and grows abundantly (8 events — joint-most-abundant with orpiment) at 44–77 °C
minor_or_abstraction.objection: Below ~150 °C, hot-spring silica precipitates as amorphous opal-A / opal-CT (siliceous sinter) and later chalcedony, not directly as crystalline quartz — quartz does not nucleate and crystallize from ~75 °C fluid on relevant timescales. Sulphur Bank's silica is dominantly opaline/chalcedonic sinter; quartz is present but not the abundant primary phase the run makes it. So the presence is defensible but the polymorph identity and abundance at this temperature are off.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: Standard silica-phase kinetics for geothermal systems (opal-A/opal-CT/chalcedony sinter below ~150 °C); mindat Sulphur Bank lists opal + chalcedony + quartz, with the amorphous/microcrystalline forms dominant in active sinter.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: Gate the silica polymorph on temperature — below ~150 °C route dissolved SiO2 to an opal/chalcedony (amorphous-sinter) phase and reserve quartz for higher-T or long-duration crystallization. If vugg has no opal/chalcedony phase yet, this stays an acceptable abstraction pending a T-dependent silica-polymorph engine; only the over-abundance would then be worth a minor nucleation-rate trim.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: Calcite (in expects_species) is a total no-show while siderite (Fe=5 ppm) and rhodochrosite (Mn=2 ppm) nucleate from a fluid whose dominant cation is Ca=200 ppm; SI_calcite stays pinned at -2.4 (never approaches 0) while SI_siderite reaches 0.
minor_or_abstraction.objection: In a Ca-dominated (Ca >> Fe,Mn) reducing carbonate fluid at ~250-320 C, calcite (or ferroan/manganoan calcite) is normally the volumetrically dominant carbonate and the Fe/Mn ride into it; it is unusual for such a fluid to precipitate the trace-metal carbonate endmembers yet crystallize no calcium carbonate whatsoever. To keep SI_calcite at -2.4 while SI_siderite=0 at the same CO3 activity, the engine needs siderite ~10^4x less soluble than calcite here (vs. ~10^2.4x at 25 C), which reads as a high-T Ksp extrapolation that over-favors the reduced carbonates.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: Calcite has retrograde solubility (less soluble as T rises), so at 300+ C a Ca=200 ppm fluid should be MORE prone to saturate calcite, not stuck 2.4 log units under. Reduced hydrothermal fluids do drop siderite/rhodochrosite (Sweet Home Mine, CO; Butte, MT) but calcite/ankerite is the typical co-precipitate, not absent. Real Fe/Mn/Ca carbonates form a continuous rhombohedral solid solution (ferroan calcite - ankerite - kutnohorite), so a Ca-rich broth normally yields Ca-bearing carbonate. The ~10^4 solubility contrast the SI numbers imply exceeds the ~2.4 log-unit calcite-vs-siderite contrast at 25 C, suggesting the high-T Ksp curve is the mover.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: Per doctrine, do NOT drop calcite from expects_species. Make the step-40 fluid_pulse ('fresh hydrothermal fluid') carry higher alkalinity (raise CO3 and/or nudge pH up toward 7) so SI_calcite crosses 0 and the headline calcite actually nucleates; a genuinely 'fresh' hydrothermal recharge reasonably resets pH/CO3 upward. Secondarily, sanity-check the siderite/rhodochrosite high-T Ksp so a 5 ppm Fe carbonate does not saturate while a 200 ppm Ca carbonate sits 2.4 log units undersaturated.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: Three discrete carbonate endmembers are grown (calcite expected, siderite + rhodochrosite grown) rather than a single mixed Fe-Mn-Ca carbonate.
minor_or_abstraction.objection: Ca2+, Fe2+, Mn2+ and Mg2+ all occupy the same rhombohedral carbonate site and substitute freely; a single fluid carrying all four at 300 C would grow one zoned solid-solution carbonate (manganoan/ferroan calcite or an ankerite-kutnohorite intermediate), not separate pure endmember crystals.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: The calcite-siderite-rhodochrosite-magnesite series is fully miscible at hydrothermal T; intermediate members (ankerite, kutnohorite, ferroan calcite) are the norm. The vugg engine deliberately models carbonates as discrete endmembers (it already carries HMC as the one intermediate), so this is a consistent engine-wide simplification, not a scenario-specific lie.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: No growth-condition change warranted — this is a settled engine abstraction, not an environment inconsistency. Flagged only to show it was considered and dismissed; the load-bearing issue is the calcite no-show (finding 1), not the endmember representation.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: SI_siderite is positive the entire run (+0.126 → +0.252) with Fe = 4.7 ppm sustained, yet siderite never nucleates.
minor_or_abstraction.objection: The broth is held oversaturated in an iron carbonate that is NOT a documented Herkimer phase, so the (correct) 'no siderite' outcome is riding a nucleation gate rather than the chemistry — the exact 'supersaturated-but-suppressed' pattern the drift doctrine flags as a loss of causal control. If Fe were dialed up the model would presumably grow a siderite that Herkimer pockets never contain.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: Herkimer pockets carry quartz + anthraxolite (host = dolomite, minor pyrite); iron carbonate is absent from the paragenesis. Siderite is genuinely kinetically sluggish and commonly persists metastably supersaturated in reducing basinal brines, so no-precipitation is defensible — which is why this is an abstraction rather than a lie — but the positive SI makes the suppression a gate artifact, not a broth fact.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: Trim broth Fe (from 4.7 ppm) until SI_siderite falls below 0, so the absence of siderite is CAUSAL (undersaturated) rather than gate-suppressed; alternatively keep Fe and add an explicit kinetic-inhibition note so the metastable supersaturation is owned on purpose.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: S = 0 ppm broth; a reducing (Eh −74 mV), petroleum-charged pocket grows only quartz, no sulfide.
minor_or_abstraction.objection: Zeroing sulfur forecloses pyrite — a documented minor Herkimer constituent — in a pocket the card itself describes as reducing and hydrocarbon-saturated, conditions that in nature carry dissolved H2S/sulfide. The redox+anthraxolite story implies a sulfide phase the broth cannot make.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: Wikipedia's Herkimer entry lists pyrite among the minor rock constituents alongside organic matter and silica; reducing petroleum-charged basinal pockets typically host trace framboidal/euhedral pyrite. The scenario deliberately narrows to the doubly-terminated-quartz signature, so omitting pyrite is a legitimate scope choice rather than a contradiction.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: If the minor diagnostic pyrite is ever wanted, add a trace S term to the broth (enough to clear the pyrite nucleation gate at Eh −74 mV) rather than expanding expects_species alone; otherwise the S=0 narrowing is fine and should be documented as an intentional single-phase scope.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: aragonite grew (1 event, step 19) co-occurring with calcite in the same warm alkaline vesicle.
minor_or_abstraction.objection: Two CaCO3 polymorphs crystallizing together invites scrutiny, and the classic aragonite driver — Mg/Ca > ~1.5 poisoning calcite steps — is absent here (broth Mg=8, Ca=180, Mg/Ca ~0.04, deep in the calcite-stable field). On the dominant control, calcite alone is expected.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: aragonite's own engine docstring (data/minerals.json + grow_aragonite) lists the secondary stabilizers T > ~50 C in low-Mg water, high supersaturation (Omega > ~10), and Sr substitution; the broth satisfies these (T 250 falling, SI_aragonite reaches 1.89, Sr=2 which favors the orthorhombic polymorph), and aragonite is a real, if minor, reported Deccan cavity phase. So this is a defensible metastable co-precipitate (Ostwald step rule) rather than an outright lie — flagged for honesty, not as a hard inaccuracy.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: If a purer calcite-only carbonate stage is wanted, drop fluid.Sr toward 0 during the Stage II carbonate window (removing the aragonite-stabilizing chemistry) and/or let SI fall below the Omega~10 metastability threshold before the carbonate event — both are broth/trajectory levers, not baseline edits. Otherwise leave as-is; it does not misrepresent the deposit.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: Topaz (in expects_species; the diagnostic late F-rich phase of a miarolitic gem pocket) never nucleates.
minor_or_abstraction.objection: Late F-rich topaz is one of the defining pocket phases of a miarolitic gem pegmatite, and its total absence hollows out the phase-3 'F crosses saturation as T drops' story that the scenario is built around. Flagging it for completeness — the biography's low-T tail is missing its signature mineral.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: Author note already concedes this: topaz is 'aspirational at seed 42, absent in BASE too — a separate vugg-tune-scenario arc.' Topaz is a standard late miarolitic-pocket phase (London 2008); this is a known, author-owned open item rather than a newly discovered defect, so it is raised only lightly and not counted against the scenario's core accuracy.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: Preserve F into the 300–400 C phase-3 window: F starts at 25 and is drawn down by lepidolite/elbaite; raise initial F or throttle the early F-consumers so topaz (sigma 1.4) reaches saturation once T enters its window. Removing topaz from expects_species is explicitly NOT the fix.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: opal nucleates at step 93 (6 events) as a late low-T silica phase in the cooled pegmatite
minor_or_abstraction.objection: Opal is amorphous/short-range-order hydrated silica of the low-T hot-spring-sinter and weathering regime; evolved-pegmatite late silica crystallises as chalcedony->quartz, not opal-A. It squeaks in here only because the scenario pins pH constant at exactly 6.504 for the entire 600->24 C arc — a 0.004 margin above the engine's opal pH>=6.5 gate — which is itself unphysical, since a 600 C melt-derived fluid and 24 C oxidizing meteoric water cannot share one pH.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: data/minerals.json opal (~line 13304): 'Forms at T<100C from silica-supersaturated waters, pH 7-9 optimal alkaline silica regime... hot-spring sinter is the canonical habit' (Fournier 1977; Jones & Segnit 1971). Opaline silica does occur in weathering profiles, so this is not a categorical impossibility for a late meteoric overprint — hence medium confidence rather than high — but opal is not a characteristic pegmatite phase and the frozen pH sitting a hair above the gate is a fragile reason for it to appear.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: Replace the frozen pH=6.504 with a real pH trajectory: acidify the late oxidizing-meteoric tail toward the mildly-acidic pH 5-6 of CO2-charged granite-weathering water. That both raises realism and drops below the opal pH gate, routing late silica to chalcedony/quartz instead of opal.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: A full LCT gem suite (spodumene, lepidolite, Cs-beryl morganite, elbaite/schorl tourmaline, topaz, cassiterite) co-grows with abundant uraninite
minor_or_abstraction.objection: Uraninite/U enrichment is the signature of NYF (Nb-Y-F, anorogenic A-type) pegmatites, whereas spodumene + lepidolite + Cs-beryl + cassiterite define the LCT (Li-Cs-Ta) family. The two derive from geochemically distinct sources, and a single pocket carrying a rich version of BOTH is uncommon in nature.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: Cerny & Ercit (2005) pegmatite family classification (LCT vs NYF); London (2008) Pegmatites. The distinction is real, but the CLAIM CARD explicitly frames this as a non-anchored 'generic testing scenario' built to exercise many engines with U + Li-pegmatite gems, so the family-mixing is a deliberate abstraction, not a lie — no fix required for a test scenario.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: If a mineralogically-tighter scenario is ever wanted, split the broth into an NYF variant (U + REE + F -> uraninite + fergusonite/samarskite-adjacent phases) versus an LCT variant (Li + Cs + Ta + B -> the gem suite); but for the stated generic-test purpose, leave the broth as-is.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: Environment: salinity held at 7.874 psu (~0.79 wt% NaCl-eq) throughout, asserted in the notes to 'follow the same analogs' as the cited ~8 wt% NaCl-eq Luumäki gem-beryl pocket fluid.
minor_or_abstraction.objection: psu ≈ g salt per kg ≈ ppt, so 8 wt% NaCl-eq ≈ ~80 psu; the modeled 7.874 psu is ~0.8 wt%, roughly an order of magnitude more dilute than the analog the card leans on (Michallik et al. 2021, ~8 wt% NaCl eq). The stated fluid framework and the modeled salinity therefore disagree by ~10×. This does not by itself grow a wrong mineral (the silicate assemblage is salinity-insensitive here), so it is a low-severity environment-vs-cited-data inconsistency, and it hinges on vugg's salinity-unit convention — if the scenario input '8' is treated internally as wt% and merely labeled psu, the value is fine and only the label misleads.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: Standard oceanographic/geochemical conversion: 1 psu ≈ 1 g/kg ≈ 0.1 wt%; seawater 35 psu = 3.5 wt%; so 8 wt% NaCl-eq ≈ 80 psu. Strip salinity chip = 7.874 psu constant (units:'psu', range 0-200). Card fluid note cites Luumäki 8 wt% NaCl eq as the anchoring analog. Needs a check of the vugg salinity-unit convention to confirm whether this is a real 10× dilution or a display-label artifact.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: If the input number is genuinely psu, raise the scenario salinity to ~80 psu to match the 8 wt% NaCl-eq pocket-fluid analog; otherwise reconcile the psu label so the trajectory reads out in the same units the analog is cited in — a broth/trajectory parameter change, not a baseline patch.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: saturation panel reports SI_siderite +1.7 log Omega at Eh +497 mV, O2 5 mg/L, Fe 1 ppm
minor_or_abstraction.objection: Siderite (FeCO3) is a reduced-Fe phase; at strongly oxidizing Eh with 5 mg/L O2 the iron is ferric and would form goethite/ferrihydrite, so a positive siderite SI is thermodynamically incoherent for this cave. Importantly this is a display inconsistency only — siderite did NOT grow, the nucleation gate correctly held, so the crystal biography itself does not lie about it.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: supersaturation_siderite (js/32-supersat-carbonate.ts:281) plus MINERAL_GATES_siderite (js/32:110-119) require Fe>=10 (broth Fe=1) and carbonateRedoxAnoxic O2<0.8 (broth O2=5) — both fail, so the gated sigma is 0 and siderite is absent from the assemblage. The SI_siderite=1.701 chip is a separate ungated Ksp display that ignores the Fe floor and the redox gate.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: Cosmetic: drop broth Fe to ~0 (oxygenated cave water carries no dissolved Fe2+ — it drops out as Fe-oxide upstream), which zeroes the misleading SI display; or suppress the SI_siderite chip when the redox/Fe gate is closed. No effect on the grown biography.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: Author bridges the T debate by nucleating amethyst warm (~160°C, the Triz-quarry 152–238°C primary-inclusion camp) and cooling to ~70°C.
minor_or_abstraction.objection: The weight of evidence — including the scenario's own primary source Gilg et al. 2003 — favors LOW-T amethyst genesis (Th 95–98°C; amethyst 120–50°C; calcite ~30°C), with the 152–238°C inclusions widely regarded as metastable/stretched. Nucleating amethyst at 160°C leans on the minority high-T camp against the deposit's dominant low-T interpretation.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: Gilg et al. 2003 (Min. Deposita 38:1009) and the 2014 low-T sedimentary-brine study both argue <100°C (prob <50°C). The author's notes explicitly acknowledge the debate and cite the Triz high-T study as a real published position — a defended, disclosed choice, not a concealed error.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: Optional tightening toward consensus: lower the temperature movement base so amethyst nucleates <120°C rather than at 160°C (keeping the cool finish). Not required — the high-T camp is a genuine cited position, so this is a calibration preference, not a correction.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: Sulfates (anhydrite stage II, barite + gypsum stage IV) and calcite (documented throughout, esp. very late euhedral) never nucleate; SI_anhydrite/barite/celestine floored at -8 and SI_calcite held -1.07→-1.58; the whole S system is absent from the broth.
minor_or_abstraction.objection: Calcite (which Gilg et al. isotopically studied because it is present) and the late sulfates are characteristic accessory phases of these geodes; a hostile reader could call them diagnostic no-shows.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: Search: 'Calcite formed throughout the whole crystallization sequence, but most commonly as very late euhedral crystals, sometimes with gypsum'; stages II/IV add anhydrite/barite/gypsum. Author's notes explicitly de-scope: 'a little late calcite is documented but this scenario keeps the headline to quartz'; broth carries no S, so sulfates are impossible by design. These are subordinate to the DEFINING mineral (amethyst quartz), which IS present — a disclosed scoping choice, not a lie.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: If a fuller assemblage is later wanted: add a late oxidizing carbonate/sulfate pulse (raise CO3/Ca into calcite saturation and introduce SO4 with the oxidizing limb) so calcite→barite/gypsum close the cavity as stage III–IV, rather than editing expects_species.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: Broth stays siderite-supersaturated (SI_siderite +0.3 → +1.07, Ω up to ~11×) for the entire 30-step run under reducing Eh (−201 mV) with dissolved Fe²⁺ (8→17 ppm) and carbonate (CO3 100 ppm) present, yet only quartz nucleates.
minor_or_abstraction.objection: In a reducing, near-neutral fluid carrying ferrous iron and carbonate that remains siderite-supersaturated the whole run, siderite (FeCO3) is the stable Fe-carbonate and would be a plausible co-precipitate with quartz — a silica+siderite assemblage is common in reducing hydrothermal/BIF-related veins. Growing strictly pure quartz while the sim's own scoreboard reads siderite Ω≈11× is a visible internal tension a geologist would seize on.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: Sim's own SI_siderite chip peaks +1.07 (strip v226, steps 17–28). Siderite is thermodynamically the stable Fe-carbonate at reducing Eh + near-neutral pH, so the supersaturation itself is chemically correct — the only question is non-nucleation. Siderite has a genuine, well-documented kinetic nucleation barrier (Fe²⁺ persists in solution in many reducing groundwaters despite carbonate saturation), which makes non-nucleation at Ω~11 defensible rather than an outright lie. Author notes confirm this is a deliberate single-mineral teaching scaffold, not a real-locality paragenesis.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: If strict single-mineral purity is desired: drop broth Fe (8 → ~2 ppm) and/or CO3 to push SI_siderite negative — a broth-chemistry change, not a baseline/scoreboard patch. Alternatively wire a modest siderite nucleation-gate threshold above Ω~11 so the sustained supersaturation reads as 'latent, kinetically withheld' rather than silently ignored. Preferred: leave as-is (acceptable abstraction) — siderite's real nucleation lag already covers it for a tutorial.

minor_or_abstraction.claim: quartz nucleates as the silica gangue at 74–97 °C (SiO₂ background 59 ppm, pulse to 236 ppm at step 15)
minor_or_abstraction.objection: Direct quartz nucleation below ~150 °C is kinetically very sluggish; at 100 °C precipitating silica from a fluid that is quartz-supersaturated but amorphous-silica-undersaturated (236 ppm < ~370 ppm amorphous solubility at 100 °C) would in nature deposit chalcedony/microquartz that only ripens to macroscopic quartz over geologic time. Calling the phase 'quartz' outright slightly overstates crystallinity for the temperature.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: Quartz/chalcedony gangue in ~100 °C carbonate-hosted veins (e.g., MVT-adjacent low-T quartz) is well documented, so 'quartz' as the modeled silica phase is a standard, defensible sim abstraction rather than a nature-lie; SiO₂ 59 ppm sits right at quartz saturation at 100 °C. Included only for completeness of the hostile pass.
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: No growth-condition change warranted — acceptable abstraction. (If crystallinity fidelity were ever desired, it would be a phase/kinetics model change, not a broth lever, so out of scope under the drift doctrine.)

minor_or_abstraction.claim: the subaerial degassing hot-spring pool holds Eh flat at -201.6 mV and O₂ at 0 mg/L for all 60 steps
minor_or_abstraction.objection: A pool that is losing CO₂ to the atmosphere (the scenario's entire premise) is by definition exchanging gases with air, so it should also in-gas O₂ and oxidize as it flows across the terraces. Mammoth's terraces are famously orange-brown from Fe-oxyhydroxide and sulfur-oxidizing microbial mats — the degassing surface is OXIDIZING. A permanently anoxic, reducing (-201 mV) arc is the vent/subsurface signature, not the terrace where travertine actually plates. It is internally inconsistent to out-gas CO₂ while treating the water as a closed anoxic system.
minor_or_abstraction.evidence: Mammoth vent water carries H₂S (reducing at source) but oxidizes on the aerated terraces; travertine is a subaerial CO₂-degassing + microbially-mediated surface deposit (Pentecost 2005; Fouke et al. 2000 on the Mammoth flow path). Calcite is redox-insensitive so the biography's calcite survives, but the flat reducing/anoxic arc props up SI_siderite (supersaturated 0.57→2.0 the whole run, Fe 5 ppm held as Fe²⁺) — a lurking false Fe-carbonate the wrong redox keeps viable (it happens not to nucleate at seed 42, but the state is wrong).
minor_or_abstraction.growth_condition_lever: Couple an oxidizing Eh/O₂ trajectory to the co2_degas events: as CO₂ out-gasses, O₂ in-gasses (O₂ 0→~6 mg/L, Eh rising toward +100…+300 mV over the three pulses). That matches the aerated-terrace reality and drops SI_siderite below saturation, removing the lurking Fe-carbonate. A broth/event-trajectory lever, not a baseline patch.

scenario_verdicts.verdict: No — this "MVT" biography grows a suite of OXIDIZED/supergene phases (willemite, cerussite, abundant greenockite+hawleyite) inside what should be a reducing, H2S-charged hypogene brine, inverts the abundance of the defining ore (sphalerite fires once and LAST while trace CdS fires 5x), and misses the district's diagnostic saddle dolomite and marcasite; the root cause is an erroneously oxidizing early broth (O2 0.28-0.35 mg/L, Eh +40 to +58 mV) that opens supergene nucleation gates a sulfide-depositing MVT fluid should never cross.

The signature quartet and the honey-sphalerite -> fluorite+barite -> giant golden-calcite backbone are faithful, but the run is redox-dishonest: it never leaves a mildly-oxidizing +25 mV Eh, so it grows a supergene oxidation blanket (smithsonite, cerussite) and an oxidized evaporite (selenite) that a deep hypogene MVT brine actively dropping sphalerite/galena/pyrite cannot, while the diagnostic saddle dolomite (the run's single most supersaturated phase) never nucleates.

A believable generic fluorite-galena-barite MVT-analog vein, but it lies about being NORTH PENNINE in three load-bearing ways: it makes baryte its single most abundant phase (11 events) yet grows ZERO witherite — the barium-carbonate that is the orefield's world-unique diagnostic (type locality Alston Moor; Settlingstones, North Pennine, was the world's largest witherite producer) and whose engine already exists; it grows metastable wurtzite as the dominant ZnS instead of the district's textbook sphalerite; and it precipitates aragonite in a 180 °C vein where CaCO3 must be calcite — plus trace-metal over-nucleation (greenockite, acanthite) that should instead ride inside the sulfide lattices.

The intended signature is sound — sphalerite nucleates first, then barite grows in pulsed zones with per-zone Mn — but the fluid confabulates two impossible-for-this-deposit phases: halite crystallizes 12× from a near-fresh 4.7-psu brine that is ~4 orders of magnitude below NaCl saturation, and late Franklin-type willemite grows where carbonate-hosted Cumbrian oxidation makes hydrozincite/smithsonite (and would give the wrong green UV instead of the specimen's expected blue-white).

The core gold-telluride trio (calaverite, sylvanite, hessite) and the adularia+quartz gangue are grown faithfully, but the biography lies in two load-bearing ways — it grows native silver alongside native tellurium (opposite ends of the same fTe2 buffer, a thermodynamic contradiction) and it deposits Na-feldspar (albite) as a vein phase in a strongly potassic adularia-type system where albite has no place — plus a reversed sylvanite-before-calaverite order and the two acknowledged flagship no-shows.

No — this crystal biography fails a mineralogist: it inverts Casadevall & Ohmoto's own paragenesis (Stage-I pyrite and quartz nucleate AFTER Stage-V rhodochrosite), drops the deposit's diagnostic Stage-III base-metal ore (sphalerite, chalcopyrite) and its namesake gold, never grows the headline manganocalcite cap, yet over-blooms a silver-sulfosalt suite (tetrahedrite dominant) that the deposit does not feature — nearly all traceable to a missing Period-VI boiling/flashing event and a too-reducing, silver-loaded broth.

The supergene half of the biography is genuinely convincing (linarite→caledonite→brochantite→leadhillite along a real CO3:SO4 fork, plus pyromorphite, cerussite, anglesite, mottramite and the plumbogummite type-mineral all in their documented type-district), but the primary/hypogene stage is corrupted: realgar is the single most-abundant phase and orpiment + proustite ride with it, none of which are documented at Roughton Gill and all of which are low-T hot-spring/epithermal As-Ag sulfosalts wrong for a 127 °C basinal Pb-Cu vein; chalcocite grows as a step-0 "primary" while chalcopyrite (the actual primary Cu ore) never nucleates (daughter-before-parent inversion); and two arid-climate exotics (vanadinite at 6 events, turquoise) confabulate their way in even though the broth never evaporatively concentrates and the district is temperate/wet.

The flagship dendritic-bismuth reduction shock and the Co-Ni-arsenide spine (skutterudite/nickeline/safflorite) are faithful, but the late biography leaks reduced sulfides — realgar and reduced-stage proustite — into an OXIDIZING, sulphur-starved tail at a locality documented as sulphide-absent, and precipitates aragonite at ~160°C where only calcite is stable, so a Wittichen mineralogist would reject the closing assemblage even while accepting the dendrite core.

The supergene finale — the Cu-then-Ca uranyl-mica cation fork (torbernite→zeunerite→autunite→uranospinite) under a rising-Eh meteoric front — is well-researched and defensible, but the PRIMARY stage is not believable: the sim grows LCT rare-element-pegmatite index minerals (spodumene, morganite) that are foreign to the Erzgebirge, while the Fünfelement district's own defining Co-Ni tri/di-arsenides (skutterudite, safflorite — whose type locality IS this district — rammelsbergite) never nucleate, so a mineralogist rejects the early paragenesis even while accepting the uranyl endgame.

A believable MVT skeleton (galena-sphalerite-marcasite-pyrite ore with dolomite/calcite gangue over a cooling, acid-pulsed carbonate wall) that nonetheless fails a Viburnum specialist on three hard points: the district-DEFINING Cu-Ni-Co suite (siegenite/chalcopyrite) never forms, gypsum (selenite) precipitates at 80-140 °C where only anhydrite is stable, and silver sulfide (acanthite) crystallizes as an abundant discrete phase BEFORE the very sulfides it should be enclosed in — while barite over-dominates the ore it is supposed to merely accompany.

It does not hold up: the biography inverts the deposit's defining paragenesis — quartz (the stockwork host) grows last and only once, epithermal-cap Au-Ag tellurides grow first and hottest, a supergene lead-arsenate crystallizes at 310°C, and the magnetite that defines a magnetite-series Cu-Au porphyry never appears; the broth is well-chosen but the timing lies.

Mostly holds as a Bisbee Cu-porphyry-plus-oxidation biography (primary chalcopyrite/bornite/pyrite → chalcocite/covellite enrichment → cuprite/native copper → malachite/azurite/chrysocolla/brochantite/turquoise oxide zone is textbook Warren District), but the SIGNATURE azurite→malachite→chrysocolla cascade grew in reverse, and two foreign species intrude — dioptase (a Tsumeb/Christmas-Mine phase, not Bisbee) and sylvite (a potash evaporite that cannot form at 3× seawater).

The core call is right — ruby grown under a SiO2-undersaturation gate, in a marble host, with late seal-vein calcite is a genuinely clever and correct abstraction — but the biography lies twice about the deposit's DEFINING chemistry: it grows ruby straight from a magnesium-rich broth that in nature would make SPINEL first (Mogok's more-abundant gem), and it runs an oxidizing, open, infiltrating 'skarn' fluid where the real deposit is a graphite-buffered, strongly reducing, CLOSED metamorphic system — the exact conditions its own cited source (Garnier et al. 2008) makes central to ruby genesis.

Redox, pH, and the andalusite/chiastolite core are sound, but the biography lies about being "silica-saturated": it grows andalusite with NO quartz even though quartz is the defining matrix phase and the explicitly-cited co-precipitate — the sim's own solubility table rates the 350 ppm SiO2 broth at only ~22–35% of quartz saturation across the entire 600→422°C path; a stray sodic albite also leaks through the Na=10 floor against the scenario's own alkali-poor thesis (cordierite, the other diagnostic companion, is simply unmodeled, so that omission is a catalog gap, not a fixable drift).

The oxidized Pb-Zn-Cu-As-V-Mo suite (cerussite, anglesite, mimetite, wulfenite, smithsonite, adamite, malachite, mottramite, scorodite, jarosite, alunite) is a faithful, genuinely Tsumeb-grade gossan — but the biography lies by growing FRESH reduced sulfides (sphalerite, galena, molybdenite, chalcocite, covellite) inside a fully oxygenated gossan, precipitating exactly the protore minerals that a gossan is supposed to be destroying; the falling-Eh trajectory (355→129 mV) plus a reduced-S broth (S=50) is sweeping one cell down through the entire redox column instead of holding the uppermost oxidation zone oxidizing.

The oxidizing-laterite oxide suite (goethite, pyrolusite, opal) is a convincing Ni-laterite, but the biography fails on its own headline: the deposit is named for and anchored on chrysoprase yet grows none, and it substitutes kinetically-impossible step-0 low-T quartz and a Ca-poor dolomite for the chalcedony + magnesite that actually define Marlborough — so it does NOT hold up to a mineralogist.
