The core biography is credible — a clean 360→50°C cooling curve, an F-pulse-gated topaz stage on early quartz, and a late supergene Fe-oxyhydroxide overprint (goethite + lepidocrocite) fit a deeply-weathered Ouro Preto imperial-topaz vein — but it is spoiled by tiger's eye, a banded-iron-formation crocidolite pseudomorph that cannot exist in a phyllite/quartzite-hosted topaz vein, and by vein feldspar + a near-neutral pH that contradict the feldspar-destructive greisen regime the topaz itself implies.

The oxidized-limb carnotite↔tyuyamunite cation-fork biography itself is sound and diagnostic, but the broth is under-vanadium'd (V<U, the inverse of the documented 3–10:1 V excess), which confabulates a co-equal uranophane the vanadium-rich Uravan ore does not actually carry — and the same fluid simultaneously reports siderite supersaturation that only the reduced zone permits, so a mineralogist would flag two redox/ratio inconsistencies even while accepting the two diagnostic vanadates.

The biography mostly holds — the star selenite, the halite crust, an early Sr-sulfate, detrital quartz, and the gypsum→halite evaporite order are all consistent with an evaporating Permian-red-bed salt-flat brine — but it lets ANHYDRITE grow as a prominent near-surface co-precipitate, which contradicts the cited USFWS/OGS data showing the Salt Plains surface precipitates are gypsum (selenite) + halite only, with anhydrite confined to depth (>15–30 m).

The alkaline-brine backbone is sound (pH 9.5 soda-lake chemistry, Na/B/S/Cl broth, the halite->mirabilite->borax->thenardite seasonal cascade), but the biography grows three phases the chemistry forbids and no-shows the one diagnostic paramorph: sylvite fires BEFORE halite (an inverted bittern sequence), and gypsum (selenite) and celestine both nucleate while the sim's OWN saturation index keeps them undersaturated for all 300 steps - a chemical-divide violation the instrument already flags - while the type-locality dehydration paramorph tincalconite never appears. The Na-salt cascade is right; the Ca/Sr-sulfate and K-chloride phases are wrong, and the root cause is nucleation gates reading raw ion concentration instead of the SI plus no carbonate sink to drain Ca/Sr.

The dolomite headline is well-grounded (Kim 2023 cyclic-Ω mechanism, huge SI_dolomite, high Mg/Ca) and defensible, but a mineralogist rejects the surrounding assemblage: a bittern potash salt (sylvite) precipitates 4× with no halite precursor in a brine that never concentrates past 3.4× seawater, and the sabkha's own diagnostic mineral (anhydrite) never appears — so the evaporite paragenesis is upside-down and incomplete.

The broth and redox arc are largely right for near-surface BSR sulfur, but the biography fails a mineralogist on three counts: the deposit's DIAGNOSTIC secondary calcite never nucleates (SI_calcite stays undersaturated all run), while a spurious fluorite and a dominant low-temperature quartz — neither a Cianciana mineral — invert the assemblage into a quartz vug with a fleck of sulfur.

The buffered ~56C thermal pool and its selenite core are faithful to Garcia-Ruiz and the diagnostic mineral (gypsum/selenite) is present, but the post-drainage chapter fabricates a sodium-sulfate PLAYA evaporite (thenardite) that Naica's calcium-sulfate chemistry forbids, and it over-promotes quartz in a cave that is essentially monomineralic gypsum.

The headline biography is right — native sulfur, cinnabar (+ genuinely-Sulphur-Bank metacinnabar), realgar, orpiment, pyrite and marcasite are all present and all low-T-appropriate for a 75 °C acid-sulfate hot spring — but the scenario plants arsenopyrite, a 300–500 °C mineral, in a vent that never exceeds 77 °C and where it is not documented, omits the district's characteristic stibnite, and lets pH drift out of the cited acid-sulfate window.

Largely holds up: a reducing/anoxic/near-neutral cooling fluid honestly grows siderite and rhodochrosite (rhodochrosite fittingly nucleating right at the step-70 meteoric-cooling pulse), and calcite's absence is a real undersaturation effect at pH 6.5 — but a fluid where Ca outweighs Fe and Mn by 40-100x growing NO calcite/ferroan-calcite at all, only the trace-metal endmembers, is the one spot a mineralogist would push on.

Mostly holds — quartz is correctly the sole phase and the undersaturated carbonate broth honestly explains the clean single crystal — but the burial plateau runs hotter (180→159°C) than the direct fluid-inclusion evidence (~51°C) and the cool-slow-growth clarity/low-Ti hallmark support, and the broth carries a latent siderite oversaturation plus zero sulfur that quietly foreclose Herkimer's minor pyrite.

The core zeolite biography is faithful (correct Stage I/II/III order, all 9 expects grow, hematite-phantom apophyllite is right, and the 250 to 68 C arc is literature-supported at 250-280 C), but it is marred by one flagrant alien species — tiger's eye, a Precambrian banded-iron-formation crocidolite pseudomorph that cannot exist in a 66 Ma basalt amygdale — and one wrong-grade, over-abundant Ca-Al silicate (epidote standing in for the diagnostic prehnite/pumpellyite); a mineralogist accepts the zeolite stack but rejects the tiger's eye outright.

The tourmaline–aquamarine/morganite–lepidolite–cassiterite–quartz backbone is a faithful Cruzeiro LCT gem-pegmatite assemblage, but the biography is falsified in two load-bearing places: an emerald forced by a hand-spiked Cr/V broth (Cruzeiro grows no emerald, and the spike also suppresses the correct aquamarine/morganite), and a step-0 simultaneous bloom of 7 of 8 species that erases the zoned fractionation cascade the scenario claims to model.

The LCT/NYF pegmatite skeleton is sound and the U story is even elegant (uraninite under reducing Eh, then supergene plumbogummite from radiogenic Pb once meteoric O2 arrives — correctly favoured over the author's intended galena, which the final +181 mV Eh would destabilise); but the run grew tiger's eye — a chalcedony pseudomorph after asbestiform crocidolite, a banded-iron-formation gemstone — with no crocidolite (or any Fe-oxide) parent anywhere in the run, which a mineralogist rejects outright.

The crystal biography is mostly sound — a reducing Fe²⁺ fluid for blue aquamarine, the Be/B dormancy-then-rupture-pulse cascade, and the late HF etch are all well-grounded — but it drops the deposit's signature green fluorite (and fluorapatite) despite carrying the Ca+F to grow them, and it crystallizes every albite crystal before schorl even though its own albitization signal fires 20 steps later, so the iconic aquamarine-on-late-cleavelandite habit is chemically asserted yet paragenetically inverted.

The granite smoky-quartz backbone is faithful (adularia + Alpine pericline albite coatings, crack-seal quartz main stage with the sceptre mechanism, specular hematite iron-roses as the oxidizing Fe3+ tell, honey wedge titanite, pink Aar fluorite, late calcite) — but the retrograde tail leaks three phases a hot cleft cannot make: metastable aragonite grown at ~200-215°C, primary goethite grown at ~260°C, and a scoreboard that claims 10^4 siderite supersaturation in a fixed-oxidizing fluid; plus a 5-event epidote bloom that belongs to the amphibolite end-member this scenario was explicitly built to exclude.

The core biography holds up well and is honestly cited — an oxidized amphibolite alpine cleft growing Fe3+ pistachio epidote as the star alongside titanite, albite, adularia, byssolite and quartz, all genuine Tormiq associates; the weak points are a confabulated fluorite that rides on the engine's default fluorine instead of a modeled F source, a redox-blind siderite SI that reports the oxidized fluid as ferrous-carbonate-supersaturated, and epidote nucleating before the quartz lining it is meant to perch on.

It does not hold up: a "generic limestone-cave dripstone" that nucleates crystalline quartz FIRST, then grows more aragonite (4 events) than calcite (2) at a molar Mg/Ca of ~0.008 and a run temperature (23.6 C) that contradicts its own cited caves, misrepresents real speleothem mineralogy on three independent counts — the habit-bias visual works, but the underlying crystal chemistry lies.

The headline calcite-stalagmite / aragonite-stalactite Mg/Ca zoning is sound and the per-vertex + air-mode habit mechanism is elegant, but the biography does not hold up: it grows fluorite and primary dolomite — neither of which occurs in a meteoric karst dripstone cave — and its whole saturation panel reflects a hydrothermal/evaporitic brine 1.5–2 orders of magnitude more concentrated than real Frasassi/Carlsbad/Reed Flute drip water.

The grown biography itself (quartz-only, celadonite→agate→clear→amethyst, cooling 160→68°C, masking sceptre) is faithful to the deposit's headline and inward structure — but the fluid's REDOX is mis-posed: held dead-constant on the reducing side, it makes a non-paragenetic siderite fully supersaturated and gate-eligible, and forces the amethyst + ferric goethite stain to sit in the wrong redox regime, inverting the deposit's documented reducing→oxidizing evolution.

The Ca-Al-silicate core (grossular, diopside, cyprine vesuvianite, prehnite, pectolite, wollastonite, datolite) is a faithful Jeffrey rodingite, but the biography fails the one test every serpentinite must pass — it grows awaruite yet zero magnetite — and it confabulates a fluorite plus Fe/Mn-carbonate suite out of a leaked default fluorine value and pH-inflated carbonate saturation on a fluid with essentially no dissolved carbon.

The quartz biography holds up to a mineralogist — it is a textbook silicification story (hydrothermal quartz nucleating on a calcite-undersaturated limestone wall at ~180 °C, SiO₂ depleting as it grows, growth of existing crystals continuing through the scripted cool-down rather than committing the 'cooling stops silica' fallacy) — with only one latent tension: a broth that stays siderite-supersaturated (Ω~11×) under reducing conditions yet nucleates nothing but quartz, which is an acceptable single-mineral teaching abstraction rather than a real inaccuracy.

The core fluorescence lesson is mineralogically sound (Mn²⁺ activator / Fe²⁺ quencher, with the Fe/Mn molar ratio flipping ~7.5→~0.30 across the step-16 Fe drop, correctly recording dim-early/bright-late zones) and the reducing, S-free carbonate assemblage is redox-consistent — but two step-0 co-nucleation calls, pure rhodochrosite grown from an Fe-dominated fluid and metastable aragonite grown from a low-Mg fluid where calcite is more supersaturated, are things a mineralogist would disbelieve.

The core lesson is sound — calcite crusts as pH rises, and the co-precipitated aragonite is actually correct Mammoth travertine (calcite+aragonite) — but the biography is contaminated by an impossible halite evaporite crust and a kinetically-forbidden quartz phase, both nucleating from realistic dilute spring-water values through mis-scaled gates, so it does NOT hold up to a mineralogist.
